The use of SUCRA rankings also provides a transparent and interpretable framework for comparing interventions. These methodological strengths collectively support the credibility, reliability, and applicability of the study’s findings, making them valid for informing both clinical practice and future research on mental health interventions in PCOS.

Article Title: Exercise alleviates allodynia and hyperalgesia concomitant with improvements in aberrant primary afferents and spinal circuit inhibition in the dorsal horn of rats with incomplete spinal cord injury
Abstract: Neuropathic pain (NP) is a common complication following spinal cord injury (SCI), significantly impairing patients’ physical/mental health and quality of life. While activity—based exercise has demonstrated efficacy in alleviating SCI-NP in rodent models, the mechanisms underlying this effect remain unclear. Specifically, it requires investigation whether exercise mediates its benefits through the following mechanisms: improving aberrant primary afferents and circuit inhibition in the lumbar dorsal horn after thoracic contusion SCI. In this study, we implemented an exercise training protocol in rats with incomplete thoracic SCI. We evaluated its effects on several factors, including mechanical withdrawal thresholds (MWTs), thermal withdrawal latencies (TWLs), and rate-dependent depression (RDD) of H-reflex. Additionally, we examined the changes in vesicular glutamate transporter 1 (VGluT1, specifically labeled in myelinated primary afferent terminals), calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP)-labeled nociceptive unmyelinated primary afferents, glutamic acid decarboxylase 65+67 (GAD 65+67), and potassium-chloride cotransporter 2 (KCC2). Our results showed that exercise training significantly improved MWTs and TWLs, enhanced the RDD of H-reflex, increased the synthesis of VGluT1, GAD 65+67, and KCC2 within the spinal dorsal horn, while reducing the CGRP-labeled nociceptive unmyelinated primary afferents. These findings indicate that exercise alleviates mechanical allodynia and thermal hyperalgesia, which is paralleled by improvements in aberrant primary afferents, enhanced RDD of the H-reflex, and increased synthesis of GAD 65+67 and KCC2 in the lumbar dorsal horn following thoracic contusion SCI.
Review:
Basic reporting
• 1. Basic Reporting
The language used is adequate. Although, to ensure that an international audience can clearly understand your text and also to improve the overall quality of the manuscript, the English language should be improved.
Some examples where the language could be improved include lines 60, 86 (previously – previous), 80 (with acetaminophen), 81 (for infection prevention). The authors should check throughout the text for such errors.
References are also adequate and contextual. Introduction to the relevance of the theme is sufficiently provided, and justifications for the performance of the work are clearly articulated. Overall, the structure of the article is correct and the conclusions are substantiated by the provided data.

Experimental design
• 2. Experimental design
The experimental paradigms and methods used are adequate to address the research questions.
My main concern derives from the total lack of clear explanations as to how the image analysis (which the authors relies to substantiate several of their claims) were performed.

1 - Saying that analysis was carried out using Fiji, is insufficient to aid future researchers to replicate such analysis. Although the researchers provide correlative WB quantifications for most image analysis, the incorporation of a section regarding Image analysis into the methods of the manuscript is crucial.

2 - This section should include a better description of the imaging set up, (objectives used, numerical apertures, mode of acquisition).

3 - The validation of antibody staining specificity, should be provided in the supplemental materials, at least with no primary staining controls of spinal cord sections for all the used markers. The signals from KCC2 and GAD, appears somewhat unspecific.
In this context, I would encourage the authors to provide a lower mag image, encompassing not only the superficial lamina of the dorsal horns, but also showing, deeper layers as well as the central canal, that would help show that the staining is specific as these areas receive much less input from neurons expressing these markers.

4 - Also, authors should provide a table, with the acquisition settings used for each marker assessed (excitation time, gain of the camera/detector, off-set values if used this is normally present in the metadata of the raw image coming out of the microscope).

5 – The imaging data presented is based on the rexed laminae, the authors should state how they segmented the laminas, (using any atlas alignment software tool, or manually).

6 – In figure 4-C, authors present a representative mask of the overlapping signal area of cGRP and IB4. However, no clarifications on how this overlap was segmented was provided. It is imperative for the authors to clarify this.

7 – In figure 5, as the authors have also stained for NeuN, it would be interesting to present data on the gabaergic synaptic inputs to motor neurons of the lamina 9. This would further validate the plasticity modulation theory, extending to the synaptic level and the motor output of the spinal cord.

8 – In all images analyzed, the normalization was done by comparing to sham levels. However, it is noticeable from the segmented laminaes, that they have different sizes (thickness, length, curvatures). In this context, a dual normalization is required in the sense that the area or the optical density measured within each lamina should be normalized by the actual size of the lamina and then expressed as a % of the SHAM values. From the graphs axis, it seems this was not carried out. This observation is important as it may significantly alter the results from both area and optical density measurements.

Finally, authors present data as either optical density or labeling area normalized to sham values. Although this is adequate, no description of the image processing pipeline used to acquire such values is provided. It is important to state if intensity based segmentation was used (thresholding) and if so, which algorithm and the respective values (in case of fixed value thresholding).

Validity of the findings
• 3. Validity of the findings
Overall, the findings presented in this work although not entirely novel, provide confirmatory and extend the evidence for the existence of a mal-adaptive plasticity response on sensory fibers below the level of injury that correlate with hypersensitive phenotypes showing the modulatory effect of physical exercise.

One key aspect that should be well discussed in the discussion section is the reliance of a motor output for the functional assessment of both mechanical and thermal hypersensitivity tests used in this work. For instance, coupling the stimulations with ultrasound vocalization recordings would yield more solid results (PMID: 35354072 and PMID: 20346101), as in SCI animals their motor function is severely affected. Additionally, authors should tone down their claims in the description of their results (lines 257 and 269), to reflect the actual data that is presented. For instance, increased GAD expression does not functionally mean more GABA is being released at the correct synaptic contacts.

Additional comments
• 4. General comments
The work is relevant to the field of pain research and specifically for the spinal cord injury and rehabilitation fields. The manuscript clearly articulates its aims. However, key points should be addressed before it can be considered for publication.

Article Title: Multifocal transcranial direct current stimulation enhances lower limb jump performance and neuromuscular adaptation in female collegiate basketball players
Abstract:
Background
Transcranial direct current stimulation (tDCS) has been reported to enhance explosive strength in lower limb skeletal muscles. Nevertheless, findings regarding the impact of tDCS on jump performance remain inconclusive, potentially due to variations in stimulation montage and current intensity. Therefore, we aimed to elucidate the effects of multifocal tDCS on lower limb jump kinetics and neuromuscular adaptation.

Methods
Fourteen female collegiate basketball players were enrolled in a randomized, crossover, controlled trial. Each participant underwent three intervention sessions in a randomized sequence: 2 mA tDCS, 4 mA tDCS, and sham tDCS, all targeting the primary motor cortex (M1). After each stimulation session, countermovement jump (CMJ), squat jump (SJ), drop jump (DJ), and surface electromyography (EMG) data were collected. Statistical analysis was performed using one-way repeated measures ANOVA.

Results
The 4 mA multifocal tDCS condition produced a significant increase in jump height compared to baseline, sham, and the 2 mA condition. Similarly, the concentric impulse was markedly higher in the 4 mA group relative to all other conditions. Relative peak force was significantly improved in the 4 mA group versus baseline, and relative peak power was significantly greater under 4 mA tDCS compared to sham stimulation. The modified reactive strength index (RSImod) was also enhanced considerably following 4 mA tDCS, relative to both baseline and sham conditions. However, EMG analysis indicated that none of the tDCS interventions significantly affected the root mean square (RMS) values of lower limb muscle activation, including the rectus femoris (RF), vastus lateralis (VL), vastus medialis (VM), biceps femoris (BF), semitendinosus/semimembranosus (SEM), medial gastrocnemius (MG), lateral gastrocnemius (GL), and tibialis anterior (TA).

Conclusion
Multifocal anodal tDCS at an intensity of 4 mA significantly improves lower limb jump performance in female collegiate basketball athletes. Integrating multifocal anodal tDCS into routine training regimens may serve as a practical and effective adjunct for enhancing performance in this population.
Review:
Basic reporting
The manuscript is written in clear and technically sound English, with a professional structure that follows the standard Peer J format. The introduction provides sufficient context, highlighting the current debate regarding the effects of tDCS on athletic performance and identifying a clear gap in research involving female athletes and high-intensity multifocal stimulation. The literature is well cited and relevant. Figures (especially Figures 1–3) and tables are precise, appropriately labeled, and appropriate to the study objectives. Raw data have been shared, supporting transparency.

Suggestions for improvement:
- In the introduction, the discussion of factors contributing to inconsistent findings in previous tDCS studies could be slightly expanded (e.g., beyond current intensity to include electrode placement and task specificity).
- Ensure consistent formatting of references and inclusion of DOIs.

Experimental design
The research question is well defined and addresses an important gap in the literature. The study uses a randomized, crossover, controlled design, which is an appropriate and robust methodology for this type of intervention. The stimulation protocol is carefully described, including the use of NIBS modeling to optimize current distribution, and the power analysis is clearly reported. Ethical approval and trial registration are provided. However, why did the authors change the electrode montage between the 2 mA and 4 mA conditions? If the montage had remained unchanged, the effect of stimulation intensity could have been more directly isolated, allowing a clearer interpretation of whether the observed differences were due solely to current strength rather than variations in current flow distribution.

Suggestions for improvement:
- As 4 mA stimulation is relatively high, more detailed reporting of tolerability and adverse event data (e.g., subjective sensation ratings) would strengthen the safety profile of the study.

Validity of the findings
Statistical analyses are appropriate and well-reported. Tests for normality, sphericity, and multiple comparison corrections are properly conducted. The main results—significant improvements in jump performance under four mA multifocal tDCS—are clearly supported by the data. All participants completed the protocol with no dropouts, increasing internal validity. The conclusions are appropriately aligned with the results and avoid overgeneralization. The lack of significant EMG changes is discussed in the context of possible central neural mechanisms. However, I wonder whether it is entirely accurate to assume that improved neuromuscular coupling efficiency would not be reflected in EMG activity. Could the authors elaborate on the physiological rationale or supporting evidence for this interpretation?

Suggestions for improvement:
- Including effect sizes (e.g., partial ?˛ or Cohen’s d) would help readers interpret the practical relevance of the observed effects.

Article Title: The temporal dynamics of resting-state EEG microstates reflected the differences in socioeconomic status among college students
Abstract:
Background
Socioeconomic status (SES) is a distal ecological factor that predicts the trajectory of human development. Exposure to low SES may have lasting effects on brain structure and function. Although prior research has identified static neural correlates of SES disparities, it remains unclear how socioeconomic contexts shape dynamic brain states. Therefore, the present study employs electroencephalography (EEG) microstate analysis to investigate how SES influences the dynamics of resting-state brain activity.

Methods
Based on SES scores, participants in the top and bottom 27% were categorized as the high-SES group (n = 29), and the low-SES group (n = 29). Resting-state EEG signals were collected from all participants, and microstate analysis identified the temporal features of four canonical large-scale neural networks (microstates A, B, C, and D) to explore socioeconomic differences in brain dynamics across different SES groups.

Results
(1) The correlation between SES and the temporal characteristics of both microstates A (ps < 0.05) and C (ps < 0.05) was significant, suggesting that SES may be associated with neural dynamics involved in auditory-language processing and the default mode network (DMN). (2) High- and low-SES groups exhibited divergent temporal characteristics in microstate dynamics. Compared with the high-SES group, participants in the low-SES group demonstrated larger duration (p = 0.025), occurrence (p = 0.002), and time coverage (p < 0.001) in microstate A, while exhibiting reduced occurrence (p < 0.001) and time coverage (p = 0.005) in microstate C. The results indicate that the low-SES individuals may have compensatory reinforcement of the auditory-language network and a weakened DMN activity. (3) High- and low-SES groups exhibiting different microstate transition patterns may reflect distinct cognitive control mechanisms. Compared with the high-SES group, the low-SES group demonstrated that the transition probabilities between microstates A and B (ps < 0.05), A and D (ps < 0.05) were significantly higher, whereas those between microstates B and C (ps < 0.05), C and D (ps < 0.05) were significantly lower.

Conclusion
These findings reveal a robust association between SES disparities and spatiotemporal EEG microstate dynamics. The reconfiguration of metastable brain states may represent the way the brain responds to challenging environments.
Review:
Basic reporting
This is an interesting study investigating the association of individuals' traits on the dynamics of EEG microstates. I hope the authors can address several concerns before publication. My biggest concern is problems with the experimental design, analysis, and choice of EEG microstate clustering.

Experimental design
There are a number of design features of the current study that I think are problematic.

First, the authors chose to utilize an post-hoc extreme groups design, in which 107 participants were recruited and participated in the study. Later, the authors excluded participants post-hoc to construct a low and high SES group. This post-hoc choice to utilize an extreme groups design (excluding participants who could contribute valuable data to the current study) is not a rigorous approach. Instead, the authors should include all the participants they recruited and collected data from, and examine correlations between microstates and SES across the entire range of SES (rather than correlations calculated on just low vs. high SES groups). Are the results still significant when including the middle-SES participants?

Second, the authors chose to constrain their microstate clustering solution to 4 microstate clusters "to maximize comparability with previous research." This rationale is no longer justified. The current literature on EEG microstates has repeatedly shown that 4 microstates is inadequate to describe the range of microstates present in the broader literature. Several methodological articles have described the various problems that can arise by constraining the microstate solution to only 4 microstates (e.g., Custo et al., 2017; Murphy et al, 2024; doi:10.1007/s10548-023-00992-7). A recent meta-analysis of microstate clustering solutions has also found that 7 microstates are commonly reported in the literature (Koenig et al., 2024; doi:10.1007/s10548-023-00993-6). The authors need to better justify why they chose to use only 4 microstate configurations, including examining whether the 5, 6, or 7 cluster solution matches with the common clustering solutions found from the recent meta-analysis. If the 5, 6, or 7 cluster solutions are good matches with the meta-analytic solution, the authors should use that cluster solution instead.

**PeerJ Staff Note:** It is PeerJ policy that additional references suggested during the peer-review process should only be included if the authors agree that they are relevant and useful.

Third, the authors measured SES with only single-item measures of parental education and parental income. More robust measures of SES would have been valuable. At a minimum, the authors should evaluate whether the results depend primarily on either parental education or parental income, as well as the aggregate score they use to calculate SES.

Validity of the findings
The validity of the findings are unclear without the authors addressing the concerns discussed above. The authors should examine the significance of their results when including all participants, not just those constrained to low or high SES. In addition, the authors should investigate additional microstate configurations beyond microstates A through D.

Article Title: Deciphering the modulatory role of short-chain fatty acids in Parkinson’s disease via phosphorylation-dependent signaling mechanisms
Abstract: Parkinson’s disease (PD), the world’s second most prevalent neurodegenerative disorder, is characterized by progressive neuronal degeneration mediated through intricate pathological mechanisms. Phosphorylation signaling pathways have been increasingly recognized as critical modulators in the development and progression of PD. Meanwhile, short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs), primarily produced by gut microbiota, have shown considerable neuroprotective potential by promoting autophagy, alleviating mitochondrial dysfunction, and regulating neuroinflammatory responses. Recent research suggests that SCFAs may influence the phosphorylation dynamics of key signaling pathways, including MAPKs, NF-?B, JAK/STAT, PI3K/Akt, AMPK, and Nrf2/Keap1/ARE, thereby modulating disease pathophysiology. This review aims to systematically evaluate how SCFAs modulate phosphorylation pathways to influence neuroinflammation, ?-synuclein aggregation, and mitochondrial dysfunction in PD. By investigating this issue, we identify potential molecular targets and propose future research directions, offering new insighreviewts and strategies for the development of novel therapeutic and preventive interventions for PD.
Review:
Basic reporting
This is a well-organized and timely review, but needs stronger critical analysis of contradictory
findings (especially MAPK, PI3K/Akt). There are many redundancies in this review. Author should remove this and unify discussions for better readability.
The manuscript is clear and unambiguous and English is good.
Literature references provides sufficient background of the study.
Article Structures like figure, Tables are sufficient for the paper.
Yes, the article is under the interest and the scope of the the journal.
Introduction is adequately sufficient and clearly stated.

Experimental design
No Comment

Validity of the findings
No Comment.

Additional comments
L35-47: Abstract is well written but mainly descriptive - please add a clear objective or
research question for the review.
? L103-129: The methodology section lacks details on how article quality was assessed;
consider adding inclusion/exclusion criteria table or PRISMA flow.
? L133-155: Physiological SCFA concentrations in the brain are much lower than in vitro
levels - please discuss this limitation.
? L165-197: The roles of GPR41, GPR43, and GPR109A overlap; clarify their distinct
contributions or summarize in a comparative table.
? L211-248: HDAC inhibition part is strong but too long; condense and highlight specific
PD-related evidence.
? L251-284: Use consistent symbol “?-synuclein”; also add a brief note on how
phosphorylation interacts with aggregation.
? L287-334: The mitochondrial dysfunction section repeats earlier points - please merge
and simplify.
? L336-368: Inflammation section is informative; add a short schematic showing
gut–brain–immune link.
? L382-431: MAPK pathway section is well detailed but confusing whether SCFAs activate
or inhibit MAPKs — clarify direction of effect.
? L469-509: Add one line comparing JAK/STAT activation in PD vs. other
neuroinflammatory disorders.
? L511-553: Discuss whether PI3K/Akt activation by SCFAs has been experimentally
validated in PD models or is extrapolated.
? L555-604: AMPK pathway section good; mention dose dependence of SCFA activation.
? L697 onward: Conclusion should briefly state future perspectives or key unanswered
questions.

Article Title: Searching for the neural correlates of emotional intelligence: a systematic review
Abstract: The concept of emotional intelligence (EI) has gained significant interest in the scientific community in recent years. Despite its demonstrated impact on social and personal functioning, the neural bases underlying EI remain poorly understood. This study aimed to conduct a comprehensive systematic review of the existing literature on the neural correlates of EI. The search was conducted in Web of Science, Scopus, PsycINFO, and PubMed databases. A total of 849 studies were initially identified (after duplicates were removed), of which 34 met the inclusion criteria and were selected for the final synthesis. These studies employed various brain mapping techniques, including lesion studies, grey and white matter structural magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), task-based functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI), resting-state fMRI, and electroencephalogram (EEG). The findings of this review suggest that EI is supported by a complex and widespread brain network primarily implicated in the integration of cognitive and emotional processes, with significant involvement of structures commonly linked to social cognition. The literature mainly emphasized the role of the insula, ventromedial prefrontal cortex, orbitofrontal cortex, cingulate cortex, and amygdala in conjunction with brain networks comprising these areas, such as the somatic marker circuitry and the social cognition network. Other brain regions, including the dorsolateral prefrontal cortex, cuneus, precuneus, fusiform gyrus, superior temporal gyrus, cerebellum, parahippocampal gyrus, inferior frontal gyrus, frontopolar gyrus, superior parietal lobule, and superior longitudinal fasciculus (SLF) were also frequently mentioned. However, further research is needed to clarify the roles of some of these regions in EI. Limitations and future lines of research are discussed.
Review:
Basic reporting
I would like to point out that the manuscript is written in clear and professional English, with an orderly and concise presentation. The introduction provides a solid context, accurately explaining why a systematic review of the neural correlates of emotional intelligence (EI) is needed at this time. A good command of the topic and a thorough review of relevant and current literature are evident. The overall structure of the article is well-structured, following both the journal's guidelines and the field's standards. The figures and tables are useful and well integrated into the text, although they could benefit from more descriptive labels to facilitate their understanding. Likewise, the inclusion of supplementary data is a positive point, although these could be better organized using more detailed identifiers.

Experimental design
The review covers a well-defined and highly interesting question: understanding which brain structures are associated with emotional intelligence, a key skill for personal and social adaptation. The research addresses a clear gap in the literature, particularly regarding the lack of integration between psychological models of EI and neurobiological findings. Recognized databases (Scopus, Web of Science, PubMed, PsycINFO) were used, and PRISMA criteria were followed, providing transparency and rigor. However, greater clarity would be appreciated regarding how the methodological quality of the included studies was assessed, and whether tools to assess bias or the quality of evidence were considered.

Validity of the findings
The results are well presented, and the conclusions clearly answer the initial question. Key brain regions are identified, such as the insula, cingulate cortex, amygdala, vmPFC, and OFC, which are repeatedly cited in different studies as relevant to EI. This point is especially valuable for future lines of research and for moving toward an integrative model between psychology and neuroscience. However, it would be interesting to reinforce the discussion with some additional works that could enrich the theoretical approach. For example: Studies that link EI with subjective well-being and perceived social support; Research on emotional intelligence as a predictor of prosocial behaviors or on its role in the symptoms of gaming disorder; Studies that explore the improvement of EI in ASD populations through intervention; Cross-cultural studies or systematic reviews on the cultural dimension of EI, which is still little discussed. These references are not essential, but they could provide depth and a more complete perspective in the final discussion.

Additional comments
In my opinion, this is a well-crafted work, with a clear narrative and an honest approach to the limitations of the field. The discussion demonstrates a good balance between critical analysis and interpretive caution, recognizing that progress is still needed toward studies with greater methodological homogeneity. As a minor improvement, the section on practical applications could be briefly expanded, highlighting possible uses of this neuroscientific knowledge in the clinical, educational, or professional settings.
In summary, the manuscript is well designed, well written, and a useful and necessary contribution to the debate on the neurobiological basis of emotional intelligence.

Article Title: AVOKE: an open-source toolbox for audiovisual web experiments in jsPsych
Abstract: As web-based experiments become increasingly popular, the need for advanced, accessible research methods is greater than ever. Existing solutions for designing and building an experiment are often closed source and proprietary, which limits configurability, affordability, and testability. Moreover, inconsistent terminologies, domain-specific coding and documentation, and practices such as open-washing impair the ease of adoption and effective use of these tools by novice researchers and practitioners. To address these shortcomings, we release AVOKE—a collection of free and open source tools for web-based behavioural experiments. AVOKE extends the functionalities of the widely adopted jsPsych library, matching community standards for code reusability and comprehensive documentation. The current release of AVOKE supports temporally-precise presentation of audiovisual media (including generated animations, preloaded files, and external sources like YouTube), as well as the collection of behavioural responses, like keypresses and video capture (e.g., for recording face videos of participants). This paper elaborates on the functionalities, implementation, and usage of the included plugins and extensions in AVOKE. These extensions and plugins have been validated with simulation testing and utilised in previous and ongoing studies. AVOKE is openly available at https://www.github.com/beatlab-mcmaster/AVOKE and archived on Zenodo. We welcome contributions to AVOKE and discuss potential future additions to ease the development of advanced web-based experiments for all users.
Review:
Basic reporting
I have no major issues with the basic reporting in this article. However, I do have some minor changes.

- The introduction is well-written and engaging, but I recommend adding a discussion of accessibility, particularly for individuals with disabilities.

- At line 33, the phrase “including those with unique or less common traits” is vague. I suggest clarifying what is meant here.

Experimental design
My main issue here is rigorous investigation and the methods described.

Timing and Lag in Audio-Visual Presentation
A key concern for me is the issue of timing delays between audio and visual presentation. The authors briefly touch on this, but I would like to see more detail. Specifically, how does AVOKE’s timing compare to lab-based results? For example, in a visual world paradigm, the simultaneous presentation of audio and pictures is critical, and even a small lag is noticeable. Providing timing metrics for AVOKE alongside benchmarks from established lab-based software would substantially strengthen the paper and help researchers evaluate its practical utility.
As an aide, how does performance suffer if multiple components (e.g., video capture with YouTube video) are used together?

Live Examples
While I appreciate that examples are provided on GitHub (The webpage is awesome). I was disappointed that no direct links to experiments were included in the paper. I strongly recommend linking the webpage with examples. This would allow readers to interact with the tools directly and better appreciate their potential—similar to the Gorilla Open Materials page.

Template Experiments
I have never used jsPsych. How would someone go about programming an experiment like this? I would include full experiments for download for users like myself to use as a template.

Validity of the findings

Article Title: Evaluating portable EEG: a comparison between two wireless systems (EPOC Flex and LiveAmp) and the wired BrainAmp system
Abstract:
Background
Recent advances in equipment miniaturization have led to low-cost, portable electroencephalography (EEG) systems that facilitate data collection in real-world settings and with larger samples. Although wireless EEG systems were originally developed for non-research applications, recent studies have provided valuable information to help researchers make informed choices, particularly about participant comfort, mobility during recordings, and data validity. This study aimed to assess the impact of portability by comparing the performance of portable consumer- and research-grade systems (EPOC Saline Flex, EM; LiveAmp, LA) with fixed research-grade systems (BrainAmp, BA).

Method
Continuous EEG was recorded with each system in healthy adults performing five benchmark tasks in fundamental and clinical cognitive neuroscience. Mental states (alpha power variations in open/closed eyes) and unconscious perception (steady-state visual evoked potential, SSVEP) were analyzed through time/frequency methods, while active (N200 and P300 components during active listening and N170 component during face recognition) and passive cognitive processes (Mismatch negativity, MMN component during passive listening) were examined using time/amplitude analyses (event-related potential, ERPs). Our analyses compared system efficiency at native and equalized sampling rates and examined 100%, 75%, and 50% of the datasets to determine the required trial number for satisfactory signal quality.

Results
Despite the smaller amount of signal retained for EM, all systems recorded the expected resting state alpha power decrease and SSVEP responses, with EM showing lower spectral effects ([EM < (LA?BA)]). ERPs for active (N170, N200, P300) and passive (MMN) processes emerged across all systems, with EM and LA showing lower amplitudes only for N170 compared to BA. Furthermore, the dataset reduction resulted in a decreased N170 at P7 only for EM ([EM < LA < BA]). EM also exhibited shorter latencies for all ERPs except for MMN.

Conclusion
This study provides concrete guidance for designing EEG experiments in real-world settings, with significant potential for investigating children and vulnerable populations. The efficiency of the three EEG systems is more influenced by task duration than sampling rates. A wireless EEG device, such as the EM, can effectively support both time/frequency and time/amplitude analyses in cognitive science, provided that the number of trials is sufficient and latencies are controlled.
Review:
Basic reporting
This may be a review formatting issue, but the way in which the references are presented makes it difficult to read, please use indenting.

Experimental design
I am curious about the difference in the N170 time windows between the LA/BA and the EM devices. I presume this relates to the triggering system and EEG record latency/asynchrony - was the variability of the event records measured for the different EEG devices? In other words, did the triggering system accurately index the time of the presentation events, or was there a systematic delay in any of the EEG recording devices? A more relevant factor though is was there any variability in any such delays? Such an imprecision of temporally indexing the events in the EEG record will have an effect of eroding the sharpness (and amplitude) of the ERP peaks obtained, due to jitter and subsequent smoothing. I know that the authors mention such a possibility towards the end of the manuscript but I do not think enough has been done to clarify the genesis of the difference. The absolute latency of peaks is less problematic as conditions may be compared that are subject to the same event-marking delays within an EEG system, however if there is variability in the precision of when event-markers are aligned with the EEG recording then both latency and amplitude effects are eroded.

Did the authors check this in the three systems? I would assume that if any variability or systematic delay existed, it would be in the EM system due to the apparent difference in the ERP peak latencies. However, a longer latency for ERP peaks in the EM system would suggest that the event triggers arrived earlier in that system, which might be true or it might be an artefact of an algorithm used by Emotiv to correct systematic delays in trigger events being recorded. Either way, I think this should be followed-up by the authors to clarify the clear latency differences that exist and to rule out improper triggering causing a reduction in amplitude and possibly latency effects in the EM system. It is relevant to note that this would only apply to data that relied on event markers, so resting state and SSVEP should not be impacted. If, however, event triggers were sent for each screen change in the SSVEP paradigm then any trigger latency variability should be able to be measured in an inconsistency of the event trigger interval timing between screen updates. Again, this shouldn't cause a reduction in the SSVEP power, but rather provide insight into trigger reception variability alone.

However, in relation to discerning the differences between the EM and the other systems in terms of some of the SSVEP, might there there a utility in looking at inter-trial coherence along the six testing blocks to see whether the EM device differs in this factor? I am not specifically advocating it, I am just curious about the source of the lowered SNR and would wonder whether temporal imprecision in the EEG recordings can be ruled out.

Validity of the findings
Figure 4 - The scalp topographs in this figure do not appear to express as clearly as I would expect a face-sensitive N170 effect to be, especially considering the waveforms in Fig 3. I would assume this is mostly due to having taken a mean voltage difference window that is too wide. I would expect a clearer view of this effect by taking the mean N170 peak latency for faces in each separate EEG system and performing a subtraction (faces minus texture) at those times to depict the differences. The same applies to Fig 5b, 5c, and 6b, where peak latency differences should be accounted for between the EEG devices. In the case of the MMN the group peak MMN latency might be used.

Additional comments
This is a well-written and conducted study. My main concerns relate to the reliability of the event-triggering system and record for the EM device, which to a degree may be addressed by the authors performing some further analyses of their data or by running additional tests which clarifies whether there are variability differences in the timing of when events were presented and when they are indexed in the EEG records.

Article Title: Knowledge, attitude, and practice toward perioperative neurocognitive disorders among healthcare workers in Shandong, China: a cross-sectional study
Abstract:
Background
Perioperative neurocognitive disorders (PND) refer to neurocognitive abnormalities detected during the perioperative period, currently including postoperative delirium, delayed neurocognitive recovery, postoperative neurocognitive disorder, and both mild and major cognitive impairments. It is a prevalent complication among surgical patients, particularly in older populations. Moreover, PND can have a profound effect on the quality of life for these patients. PND has been previously reported to occur with a prevalence of 10–54% during the first few weeks following surgery, and when persistent at the time of discharge or in the longer term, PND may increase mortality. Therefore, it is of considerable importance for healthcare professionals to accurately identify the occurrence of PND and implement appropriate management measures. This research aims to investigate the knowledge, attitude, and practice (KAP) of healthcare workers concerning perioperative neurocognitive disorders (PND) in Shandong Province, China.

Methods
This cross-sectional study recruited healthcare workers from twelve hospitals in Shandong Province, China, utilizing self-administered questionnaires distributed between February and May 2025. There are 12 items in the knowledge dimension, and the total score range for this dimension is 0 to 12 points. There are a total of seven items in the attitude dimension, and the total score range for this dimension is 7 to 35 points. There are a total of nine items in the practice dimension, and the total score range for this dimension is from 9 to 45 points.

Results
The analysis included a total of 309 valid questionnaires, which represents an impressive 87.78% response rate. Among them, a total of 193 doctors (62.5%) and 116 nurses (37.5%) participated in the survey, respectively. The PND knowledge score was 9.14 (SD = 2.85, range = 0–12). The PND attitudes score was 30.89 (SD = 3.81, range = 7–35). The PND practices score was 36.16 (SD = 7.77, range = 9–45). Furthermore, Path analysis indicated that participants’ knowledge of PND among healthcare workers had a direct and positive effect on their attitudes (? = 0.545, P < 0.001) and practices towards PND (? = 0.230, P = 0.002). And attitudes towards PND also had a direct and positive effect on practices (? = 0.238, P = 0.001). In addition, the knowledge exerted an indirect effect on practice through attitudes (? = 0.581, P = 0.003).

Conclusions
Healthcare practitioners have sufficient knowledge, and they demonstrate positive attitudes and adopt proactive approaches regarding PND. The understanding and perspectives of healthcare workers are closely linked to their actions towards PND. Educational training is pivotal in shaping their knowledge, attitude, and practice.
Review:
Basic reporting
Introduction
1. The introduction is informative but contains overly dense sentences; for example, the long list of interventions (lines 48–51) should be broken into shorter sentences for better clarity and readability.
2. While the introduction defines perioperative neurocognitive disorders, it does not clearly explain the differences between its subtypes—such as postoperative delirium and postoperative neurocognitive disorder. These conditions differ in timing, symptoms, and diagnostic criteria. Adding a brief explanation of each subtype would enhance clarity and help readers understand the scope and complexity of PND.
3. The authors note prior focus on anesthesiologists but offer limited justification for including other healthcare workers (e.g., nurses, surgeons, operating room technicians). Without clarifying these groups’ roles in PND prevention, the rationale for their inclusion feels weak. This gap undermines the study’s scope and claims novelty. A clearer explanation of their relevance is needed.
4. The rationale for selecting Shandong Province is unclear; adding regional context would strengthen the study’s relevance and justification.

Experimental design
Materials & Methods
1. The manuscript does not mention adherence to the STROBE guidelines, nor does it include a completed STROBE checklist. Including this would ensure transparent and standardized reporting, especially for an observational, cross-sectional design.
2. The study lacks a detailed description of participant recruitment and flow (STROBE item 13), such as how many individuals were approached, how many declined, and how many were excluded after applying the data quality criteria. A flow diagram would greatly improve clarity and reproducibility.
3. STROBE recommends discussing sources of potential bias (item 9), but the manuscript does not address important biases inherent in convenience sampling or self-reported data, such as selection bias, social desirability bias, and non-response bias.
4. While the pilot study reports satisfactory internal consistency (Cronbach’s alpha), the manuscript lacks information on content and construct validity assessments or expert review of the questionnaire. Internal consistency alone is insufficient to establish the validity and robustness of the measurement instrument.
5. While the exclusion of rapid responses and logically inconsistent answers is commendable, the threshold of 90 seconds is arbitrary and lacks justification. A more evidence-based or pretested time threshold would strengthen the rigor of the data cleaning process.
6. The explanation of study size is limited to a minimum sample size calculation based on the number of predictors, but it lacks a detailed justification considering expected response rates, effect sizes, or power analysis. A more comprehensive sample size determination would strengthen the study’s methodological rigor and credibility.

Validity of the findings
Discussion
1. The limitations are mentioned superficially without discussing how sample bias, regional confinement, and self-reporting might significantly skew results. A more in-depth critical appraisal is necessary to contextualize the findings and temper conclusions regarding generalizability and reliability.

Article Title: AIMP1: multifunctional regulator in physiology and pathology with therapeutic implications
Abstract: Aminoacyl tRNA synthetase complex interacting multifunctional protein 1 (AIMP1), also referred to as p43, serves as an auxiliary factor of the macromolecular aminoacyl tRNA synthetase complex. Beyond its classical role in the assembling the multisynthetase complex (MSC) for protein translation, growing evidence has elucidated that AIMP1 plays a pivotal role in regulating immune response, brain function and angiogenesis. Furthermore, accumulating studies have demonstrated that AIMP1 is involved in a spectrum of pathological processes, including cancer, immunity associated disorders, and neurological diseases. Herein, we summarize the current research regarding the functions of AIMP1 under both physiological and pathological conditions, with a particular focus on its therapeutic potential in these diseases.
Review:
Basic reporting
This manuscript tackles an important topic by pulling together what we know about AIMP1’s roles in normal physiology and various diseases.The authors compile a huge number of studies, from its basic function in the multisynthetase complex to its emerging roles in cancer, immunity and neurobiology, and even outline therapeutic angles. That breadth is impressive and the tables of mutations, animal models and signaling pathways are useful. Yet at nearly 6,000 words it often reads like a list of facts rather than a narrative that guides the reader through “why this matters” or “where the real gaps are.” A tighter focus, clearer linkage between sections and more critical discussion of conflicting data would take it from a catalog of findings to a compelling story.

Experimental design
ok

Validity of the findings
ok

Additional comments
In the Abstract, you introduce AIMP1 clearly but then jump straight into “accumulating evidence” without giving a hint of the scale or sources of that evidence. It might help to say “over 100 papers” or “work from labs X, Y and Z” to ground the reader in why this protein suddenly deserves its own review. In the Survey methodology subtitle, the description of search terms is fine but the criteria (“mechanistic or therapeutic focus”) could be backed by a reference to established review–writing standards (e.g., PRISMA guidelines). A sentence like “We followed PRISMA recommendations for literature selection” and a citation would strengthen your rigor claim.
Under “AIMP1 in physiological conditions,” the first sentence in the “immune response” section starts “Generally speaking, immune response was marked with…” which is awkward. One paper suggested to cite is “Clinical powers of Aminoacyl tRNA Synthetase Complex Interacting Multifunctional Protein 1 (AIMP1) for head-neck squamous cell carcinoma, 2022” as an example for AIMP1 in cancer immunity. You might rephrase it as “Immune responses are characterized by activation of immune cells and inflammation.” Also, I noticed you refer to EMAPII sometimes as “EMAP a” (line 74)—that looks like a typo. In the CNS section, the subtitle is “AIMP1 in central nervous system (CNS),” but later you say “As described above, AIMP1 regulated TGFł signaling,” even though TGF-? wasn’t introduced in that subsection. It would flow better to remind the reader that you first discussed AIMP1–TGF-? links in the immune section.
Throughout the Results-like subsections you often list one study after another without noting where the data conflict or how strong the evidence is. For instance, you cite multiple papers on AIMP1’s anti-tumor versus pro-tumor roles in different cancers, but you don’t discuss why it seems to suppress breast cancer growth yet promote myeloma progression A comparative table or a paragraph weighing these opposing roles, and suggesting reasons like tumor microenvironment or dosage, would add depth.
I also spotted a few missing or sparse citations. In the opening of the angiogenesis section you state “Angiogenesis generally referred to the formation of new blood vessels,” but don’t cite any classic reviews (e.g. Carmeliet & Jain 2011). Likewise, when you describe TGF-?’s complex signaling, you list only two recent reviews; a landmark reference on SMAD versus non-SMAD pathways would help readers unfamiliar with the pathway. And when asserting that AIMP1 modulates NF-?B in macrophages, a direct citation right after that clause would avoid any ambiguity.
Grammar and style could use a light copy-edit. Sentences like “Previous studies have shown that AIMP1 was present in the conditioned medium…” (in the cancer section) could be tightened to active voice—“Smith et al. detected AIMP1 in conditioned medium from GH-secreting adenomas.” I’d also watch for consistency in gene/protein nomenclature (sometimes you write “TGFł,” other times “TGF-?”) and correct small mistakes like “AIMP1 might played a role” (should be “might play a role”) in line 99.
Finally, the Discussion/Conclusions read like another Results paragraph rather than a place to highlight unanswered questions. What are the top three critical gaps? Where should future mechanistic work focus—on cleavage to EMAPII, on receptor identification, on cell-type specificity? More forward-looking commentary and fewer summary sentences would make the ending more memorable. I recommend opening your report with a cohesive paragraph that introduces cancer biology and treatment modalities, cite review by Chief of US National Cancer Institute D. Sonkin, A. Thomas “Cancer Treatments: Past, Present, and Future (2024)” . and discuss how AIMP1 can be apply for cancer therapy.
In short, this is a solid compendium of AIMP1 literature, but it needs more narrative drive, critical synthesis of conflicting findings, a few extra foundational citations and some light polishing of language. Once those issues are addressed, this could be a go-to reference for anyone entering the AIMP1 field.

Article Title: An intrusion detection system based on lightweight BERT with meta-classifier for internet of things environments
Abstract: The rapid increase in Internet of Things (IoT) devices has significantly transformed modern technological environments. However, this growth has also introduced numerous security vulnerabilities and various attacks. Traditional Machine Learning (ML) and Deep Learning (DL) have been intensively utilized in Intrusion Detection Systems (IDSs) implementation to detect and classify cyberattacks in an IoT environment. ML relies on a feature engineering approach for selecting optimal features, and DL requires high computational resources to achieve adequate classification accuracy. Therefore, this study proposes an IDS model implemented based on lightweight Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers (BERT) models integrated with a meta?classifier. This is accomplished by employing three lightweight models named DistilBERT, MobileBERT, and TinyBERT as base models, with their outputs combined by a Random Forest (RF)-based meta-classifier to achieve robust detection and classification accuracy while using a lightweight architecture. The proposed model was evaluated on three diverse IoT datasets: ToN_IoT, Edge-IIoTset, and X-IIoTID to demonstrate its effectiveness in detecting and classifying cyberattacks in IoT environments. In addition, the results were compared with existing benchmark IDS models. The experiments confirm the robustness of integrating lightweight BERT models with a meta-classifier for the IoT IDS field.
Review:
Basic reporting
This study proposes a lightweight Intrusion Detection System (IDS) combining DistilBERT, MobileBERT, and TinyBERT models with a Random Forest meta-classifier to detect and classify IoT cyberattacks efficiently. Evaluated on multiple IoT datasets, the model achieved strong accuracy and robustness while significantly reducing computational requirements compared to traditional ML and DL methods.

Experimental design
The paper is interesting; however, the following issues are suggested to consider:

+ In the first para of the introduction, the authors mentioned DDoS, eavesdropping, reply attacks in relation to IoT, but failed to mention memory obfuscation attack (https://doi.org/10.3390/s23115348 ), which is also another serious attack on IoT applied in various contexts.
+ The “Related Works” section is pretty comprehensive and it is good that works are summarised in a Table. However, the authors simply describe the works without pointing out the limitations of those works. Summarising the current works in each section or each work individually will enrich this section.
+ Figures 3, 4 & 5 are using different scales. For example, y-axis is 0-100 in Fig. 3 & 5 while is it is 92-100 in Fig. 4. It makes things difficult to judge.
+ The number of samples in “Fake_notification” & “MITM” is very small in the X-IIoTID dataset (Table 5). Still, the accuracy of these classes is very high as per Fig. 5. Though SMOTE helps, it is surprising to see that high accuracy.
+ Comparing Tables 7 and 8, for “Edge-IIoTset” dataset, the individual class accuracy is better than the overall accuracy by the RF classifier. What is the explanation of this?
+ The reliability and trust of sensors and their data are vital, which is not discussed in this paper. It would be better to consult (https://doi.org/10.1109/TR.2024.3416967) to see how the reliability of sensors matters, and this point should be briefly discussed in the manuscript.
+ Table 3 shows the model size. The model size is quite big to be accommodated within the sensors themselves. Where does the model reside then? Is it in the edge devices? Model size and inference time should be included in the discussion.

**PeerJ Staff Note**: It is PeerJ policy that additional references suggested during the peer-review process should only be included if the authors agree that they are relevant and useful.

Validity of the findings
I think, adding AUC (area under the curve) as another metric will add value to the paper.

Additional comments
none

Article Title: Disease diagnosis and prediction using deep learning: a review
Abstract: Deep learning (DL) is a machine learning technique that processes data in a manner influenced by the functioning of the human brain. It is an effective tool for deciphering complicated data and may be applied to many other processes, such as decision-making, image recognition, and natural language processing. The requirement to process large amounts of data rapidly and precisely drives the demand for deep learning technologies in the healthcare industry. Deep learning can find patterns in medical data, including genomic data, patient records, and medical imaging. Additionally, it can be utilized to create prediction models that can aid clinicians in selecting the course of treatment for patients. This article employed deep learning models to examine medical data for better diagnoses. DL models efficiently improve accuracy, handle complicated medical data, and detect subtle trends. A comparative analysis of deep learning architectures revealed that DL helps boost diagnostic accuracy and recognize subtle disease patterns. However, issues like the need for vast training data, overfitting, model interpretability, and high computational resources exist. Also, we presented the applications in diagnosing heart disease, cancer, Alzheimer’s, and other specific diseases, demonstrating the potential of deep learning in predictive modeling for clinical decision support. This article comprehensively reviews deep learning architectures and comparative research for disease identification and prediction, and explores emerging solutions such as federated learning and explainable artificial intelligence (AI). The study also tackles research obstacles and potential advantages by presenting the current status and probable future directions of deep learning in disease diagnosis and prognosis.
Review:
Basic reporting
Revise the introduction section thoroughly. Reconstruct it to clearly articulate the limitations of each model discussed and explain how subsequent models address those limitations. For example, explicitly describe how Vision Transformers (ViT) overcome specific drawbacks of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), such as limited receptive fields or inductive biases. This comparative framing will provide a stronger foundation for the study and clarify the motivation behind the model selection.

Experimental design
(1) Revise the survey methodology section to include mathematical descriptions of each method discussed. Providing formal representations will enhance the technical depth and clarity of the methodology, making it more informative for readers with a research or engineering background.

(2) Improve the quality of all figures throughout the manuscript. Ensure that images are high-resolution, clearly labeled, and visually consistent to support the readability and professionalism of the paper.

(3) In Section 5.1, add a table summarizing the datasets used. This table should include columns for disease type, image type, and the number of images. Presenting this information in a structured format will significantly improve clarity and reader engagement.

(4) Highlight the role of recent deep learning-based disease diagnosis methods to strengthen the relevance and currency of the study.

(5) In Section 5.3, provide a detailed explanation of the hyperparameters used in the deep learning methods. Include values, rationale for selection, and any tuning strategies applied. This information is essential for reproducibility and for evaluating the robustness of the models.

Validity of the findings
(1) Add a separate section dedicated to explaining the different performance evaluation metrics used for deep learning methods. Clearly define each metric and justify its relevance to the specific tasks addressed in the study to enhance the reader’s understanding of model effectiveness.

(2) Clarify how the results of the deep learning methods are validated. Include appropriate statistical validation techniques such as cross-validation, confidence intervals, or hypothesis testing to ensure the reliability and robustness of the reported outcomes.

(3) Discuss the impact of dataset size on model performance. Explain how limited data can affect generalization and accuracy, and provide strategies to mitigate these issues. Specifically, elaborate on the role of data augmentation techniques in enhancing model performance when working with small datasets. Include examples of augmentation methods relevant to the image types used in the study.

Additional comments
(1) Add a separate table listing all abbreviations used in the manuscript. This will improve readability and help readers unfamiliar with specific terms to follow the content more easily.

(2) Revise the conclusion section to provide a more comprehensive summary of the study’s findings. Additionally, expand the discussion on future research directions by identifying specific challenges, potential improvements, and emerging trends that could guide subsequent work in this area.

Article Title: IoT-based WISNE-SDN detection and DDOS attack mitigation using machine learning techniques
Abstract: The Internet of Things (IoT) refers to a system of interconnected computing devices, sensors, and supporting infrastructure. Attacks from Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) and insufficient resources are common issues for this network. Security and access control might be enhanced by integrating the IoT with Software-Defined Networking (SDN). A method for detecting DDoS attacks in Wireless Sensor Networks (WISNE) using machine learning (ML) is discussed in this article. The WISNE-SDN IoT controllers could make use of this technique. In a testbed environment that mimics DDoS attack traffic, the WISNE-SDN controller may gather network events into a pre-processed dataset. For tasks like packet sorting and attack detection, the framework employs some ML algorithms, such as K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN), XGBoost (XGB), and Naive Bayes (NB). Accuracy levels of 97% for KNN, 100% for XGB. The suggested approach improves the security and stability of IoT networks in SDN-IoT environments by making them more resistant to DDoS attacks.
Review:
Basic reporting
-

Experimental design
-

Validity of the findings
-

Additional comments
Main research question
The authors address an important problem concerning IoT network security, specifically protection against DDoS attacks. Traditional security mechanisms often prove insufficient in dynamic IoT environments, which is why it is necessary to develop new, more effective methods of detecting and counteracting threats. The article examines how machine learning can be used in the SDN (Software-Defined Networking) architecture to improve the detection of DDoS attacks and optimize the consumption of system resources.

What is the article about?
The authors propose a solution based on the WISNE-SDN framework, which combines SDN with machine learning techniques to effectively detect DDoS attacks. They use three different classification algorithms: KNN, XGBoost, and Naďve Bayes, comparing their effectiveness. A key element of the work is also the analysis of the system's performance, in terms of both the accuracy of threat detection and resource consumption.

Strengths of the article
-Currentness of the problem - IoT is becoming more and more common, and DDoS attacks on this type of network are a real threat. Research in this area is therefore necessary and practical.
-Experimental approach – The authors conducted real tests of their solution, which increases the credibility of the results.
-Resource optimization – The research took into account a key aspect of IoT systems, which is limited computing power. The proposed solution reduces memory and CPU consumption, which is important in practical implementations.
-Comparison of different ML methods – The use of three different classifiers allows for a better assessment of their effectiveness in detecting threats.

Suggestions for corrections
-Although the authors test different algorithms, there is no reference to results from the literature. How does their solution compare to other approaches to DDoS detection in IoT?
-The experiments were conducted in a controlled environment, but it would be worth checking how the system works on real IoT networks.
-What features were analyzed exactly? How was the data prepared for model training? Adding this information would increase the transparency of the study.
- It would be worth analyzing how the system copes with a larger number of IoT devices and different attack intensities.

Summary
The article makes a valuable contribution to IoT security research, especially in the context of DDoS attack detection using machine learning. The results presented by the authors are promising, especially in terms of the effectiveness of the XGBoost model and resource optimization. To strengthen the article, it would be worth adding a comparison to other methods, conducting tests in a real environment, and specifying the data preparation method.

Article Title: A novel artificial bee colony algorithm integrating K-means clustering and Q-learning
Abstract: The Artificial Bee Colony (ABC) algorithm is a stochastic, population-based optimization method. While the randomness in generating the initial population ensures solution diversity, it can also result in poor neighborhood structures due to the lack of spatial guidance. To address this challenge, this study propose a novel ABC algorithm integrating K-means clustering and Q-learning, called KQABC. First, the initial population is randomly generated, and then K-means clustering is applied to partition it into multiple clusters, where individuals in the same cluster are considered neighbors. Second, the neighborhood concept is incorporated into both the employed bee and onlooker bee phases. In the employed bee phase, each bee learns from the best individual in its neighborhood, enabling targeted local exploration and avoiding random perturbations. In the onlooker bee phase, Q-learning adaptively selects search strategies. To evaluate the performance of KQABC, we compare it with the original ABC and several state-of-the-art ABC variants on 22 benchmark functions and CEC 2014 test suit. Experimental results demonstrate that KQABC outperforms, or is at least competitive with, existing ABC variants in terms of solution quality, robustness, and convergence speed.
Review:
Basic reporting
In this article, the authors conduct a comparative analysis of the modified ABC algorithm, proposed in the optimization field, against other ABC algorithms and their variants for 22 different problems. Experiments were conducted using 22 benchmark functions. The results indicate that the new method consistently outperforms other variants and demonstrates superior solution quality across most benchmark functions.

Experiments for the new variant were repeated 30 times for the problem dimensions of 30, 50, and 100, and statistical tests were performed.

The new variant was run for KMeansUpdate values ??of 50, 100, and 150. Separate runs were also run for each of the three dimensions, with K values ??of 4, 5, 6, 7, and 10.

According to the results of the Friedman test, the KQABC algorithm achieved the lowest mean rank values across all problem dimensions (2.93 for D=30, 2.68 for D=50, and 2.61 for D=100), indicating that it consistently outperformed the other competing ABC variants.

Experimental design
The topic is truly interesting. Some of my concerns regarding this article are as follows:

1. Heatmaps of the resulting Q-tables can be examined as the iterations for each problem are completed. This allows us to assess whether using a Q-table contributes to the algorithm's operation and whether differences emerge on a problem-by-problem basis. A heatmap can be displayed.

2. How does Q-learning exploration achieve balance? Does it use the finalized Q-table as the iterations of problem-specific training progress? For the last Q table it will directly select one of the actions instead of using alternative actions. It will become fixed to only one action. If the neighborhood action is chosen randomly for the onlooker phase, it may begin to function like a scout bee phase.

3. The meanings of the FE and FEmax values ??used in epsilon calculation of the Q learning algorithm should be explained in detail.

4. The reasons for choosing the k values ??used for K-means as 4, 6, 7 and 8 depending on the problem size should be explained.

Validity of the findings
The conclusions are clearly articulated and focused solely on the supporting results. Experiments and evaluations performed satisfactorily. Conclusion also identify limitations and future works.

Article Title: Predicting student performance in Massive Open Online Courses (MOOCs) using machine learning and deep learning techniques: a comprehensive survey
Abstract: High dropout rates in online learning, particularly in Massive Open Online Courses (MOOCs), present a significant challenge to educational institutions. An accurate prediction of dropout risk is essential for designing effective intervention strategies. Unlike prior surveys, this comprehensive review synthesizes 57 studies from 2015 to 2024 to identify systematic methodological advances and critical limitations in dropout prediction. Our analysis categorizes the approaches across three dimensions: model type, learning paradigm, and performance characteristics. A key finding is the identification of inflection points: behavioral + temporal features improve accuracy by 15–25% over static features; ensemble methods achieve 85–92% accuracy with superior interpretability; and advanced Deep Learning (DL) architectures reach 95% accuracy but require substantial computational and data resources. These findings enable evidence-based model selection based on the institutional constraints. We critically evaluate the limitations of existing approaches, including inadequate class imbalance handling, lack of cross-platform generalization, and insufficient intervention validation, and propose standardized evaluation protocols. This survey serves as a practical reference for researchers and educators in designing data-driven dropout reduction strategies that align with institutional capabilities.
Review:
Basic reporting
Minor grammatical slips occur (“a significant and unresolved challenge,” “the field remains fragmented and lacks a unified synthesis that captures recent methodological advances”) — stylistically fine but overused and repetitive.
Although many models (LR, DT, RF, XGBoost, FIAR-ANN, RFXGB, etc.) are discussed, no consolidated comparison table of performance metrics is included, making the text dense and hard to cross-reference.
Although the review mentions accuracy ranges (up to 95%), there’s no statistical synthesis or cross-study comparison (e.g., average F1, ROC-AUC, or variance). Even an approximate aggregated performance metric table would add rigor.
The paper reads as an annotated bibliography rather than a critical meta-analysis. It lists many papers but offers limited cross-paper insights (e.g., model trends, common failure points, evaluation biases).

Experimental design
Despite claiming a “systematic” review, no screening flowchart or PRISMA checklist is included. This undermines reproducibility.

Validity of the findings
The reported “up to 95% accuracy” lacks context — on which dataset? under what imbalance ratio? Accuracy alone is a weak indicator; F1, ROC-AUC, and precision/recall should be emphasized.

Additional comments
Add a quantitative comparative summary — even a coarse table of mean accuracies per model type.

Article Title: Machine learning intervention on cyber-hate in code-switch texts: a systematic review with open challenges and solutions
Abstract: Perpetrators of cyber-hate are increasingly using code-switching, which is the alternation of languages within a single text, to evade automated moderation systems. While machine learning has advanced hate speech detection for monolingual content, these systems struggle to adapt to the widespread and complex reality of multilingual and low-resource communication. To address this gap, this study conducted a systematic literature review of 400 studies (2013–July 2025) to evaluate machine learning for detecting cyber-hate in code-switched texts. It identified key challenges: limited and unavailable datasets, an overreliance on bilingual data, a regional concentration of studies (notably India), underrepresentation of African and Latin American languages and narrow evaluation metrics. The analysis confirmed that while transformer-based models excel for high-resource languages like Hindi-English, their performance drops sharply for low-resource pairs like English-Swahili due to data scarcity and linguistic complexity. Synthesizing proposed solutions, the study concludes with a research roadmap prioritizing: (1) validated, open-access multilingual data; (2) models optimized for low-resource settings; (3) ethical safeguards for fairness and privacy; and (4) expanded evaluation metrics that include bias and interpretability. This study provides a diagnostic overview of the field and actionable guidance for building inclusive and context-aware hate speech detection systems. Its scope also extends to the related problems of toxicity and abusive language, which often overlap with cyber-hate and contribute to online hostility. Consequently, the review also explores how machine learning can be designed to detect and mitigate this broader spectrum of harmful content in multilingual and code-switched environments.
Review:
Basic reporting
The manuscript has poor academic writing, grammatical errors, and repetitive phrasing. Much of the content is overly descriptive with limited analytical depth. The tables listing hate phrases in various languages are redundant and superficial without analytical insight. The first table seems to be cut off for some reason, and I could not read it properly. Despite being a systematic literature review, the contribution is vague and repetitive. The claimed novelty (identifying 14 studies) is marginal and insufficient to justify publication.

Experimental design
While the authors claim to perform a Systematic Literature Review (SLR), the methodology is not rigorous. The search process is poorly documented (e.g., missing PRISMA-compliant protocol details), and no evidence of inter-rater reliability or quality scoring beyond simplistic yes/no answers is provided. The inclusion/exclusion criteria are generic and lack transparency. The final selection of only 14 papers seems insufficient for a comprehensive review. Despite claiming to synthesize ML methods, there is no deep taxonomy, no algorithmic comparison, no clear performance benchmarking, and no visualizations (e.g., heatmaps, comparative tables) of model performance or language-resource pairings.

Validity of the findings
The review reads more like an annotated bibliography than a critical synthesis. It lists papers without evaluating the strengths/weaknesses of methodologies, model architectures, or data limitations. The tables showcasing “cyber-hate speech samples” in multiple languages are speculative and ungrounded in any empirical dataset. The paper claims to cover multiple languages (e.g., Yoruba, Portuguese, Japanese) but bases its findings primarily on Indian datasets.

Additional comments
This manuscript attempts to address a relevant and timely problem, i.e., hate speech in code-switched settings. However, the execution is weak. It falls short of the standards of a systematic review mainly because of the lack of analytical insights and very poor synthesis of challenges. The paper fails to suggest good actionable recommendations, which one would usually expect from a survey paper. The paper must be entirely restructured and rewritten with a clear analytical focus.

Article Title: Multi-scale adaptive image watermarking algorithm based on cross-attention
Abstract: With the advancement of digital media in recent years, copyright protection and image information security issues have become increasingly significant. Image watermarking algorithms are crucial for protecting image copyright and information security. Traditional watermarking algorithms fail to consider the problem of imperceptibility and robustness, so this article proposes a Multi-scale Adaptive Watermarking (MSAW) algorithm based on cross-attention. By exploiting the energy concentration characteristic of Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT) coefficients to embed watermarking information in the frequency domain, MSAW effectively balances imperceptibility and robustness. Meanwhile, the proposed algorithm uses a multi-scale U-Net encoder to optimize the traditional U-Net structure, and injects multi-scale watermarking information in the up/down sampling process, which enables the algorithm to better resist geometric attacks such as isometric scaling. Additionally, MSAW introduces a cross-attention adapter to improve image quality. Based on the dynamic interaction between the cover image and the watermarking features, the intensity coefficient matrix is generated to realize pixel-by-pixel adaptive adjustment. The algorithm combines multi-scale feature injection with dynamic intensity adjustment strategies while ensuring the quality of the watermarked image. Moreover, through the adversarial training strategy of noise layer simulation, the MSAW algorithm can stably extract watermarks in complex distortion scenarios, leading to significantly higher robustness to geometric attacks. Experiments indicate that MSAW performs well on the COCO2014, Flickr, and ImageNet datasets: The decoding accuracy exceeds 90% in the face of JPEG compression, Gaussian noise, median filtering, and other attacks. Also, in scenarios such as scaling, MSAW has higher robustness than existing methods such as Universal Deep Hiding (UDH), Adaptor, and Deep Independent Template-based Watermarking (DIWT).
Review:
Basic reporting

Article Title: A novel model to predict the readability of online diabetes information based on fuzzy logic
Abstract: The recent growth of information and communication technologies, has evolved the websites into an essential and universal intermediary for disseminating diabetes-related information. Government and non-government websites are commonly visited by people to learn about diabetes symptoms, treatment, and management; therefore, a readability examination is required. So far, numerous research has been pursued the diabetes information’s quality evaluation, but there is a lack of fuzzy-based computational model for readability prediction of online diabetes information. This study aimed to develop a fuzzy-based computational model that could predict unified reading grade level by combining six readability metrics to generate audience-specific readability score specifically for online diabetes information, differing from conventional standalone readability formulas used in previous studies. The readability of diabetes information was evaluated using six metrics, which were then used as ranked input variables in fuzzy system. The proposed model used these inputs and fuzzy rules to predict the readability based on target audience. Higher grade levels signify difficult readability, in contrast to lower grade levels, which reflect easy readability. The findings revealed that the fuzzy system predicted a readability score of 6.46 ± 1.73 (mean ± SD) for government websites and 14.40 ± 2.33 (mean ± SD) for non-government websites. This suggests that the non-government’s content is challenging for average readers and appropriate for college graduates. The proposed fuzzy model’s observations yield definite and beneficial outcomes. The study suggests that the implementation of proposed fuzzy model could support quick readability prediction, enabling the healthcare providers and editors to enhance diabetes-related content’s readability before online publication.
Review:
Basic reporting
- Clear and unambiguous English

- Literature references are sufficient field

- The article structure is professional, but additional improvements are required (see detailed review).

Experimental design
- The paper presents original research: Readability of online health information is a very important and timely issue, especially in diabetes management, where patient understanding directly impacts health outcomes.

- Research question is well defined,

Validity of the findings
-

Additional comments
In short, this study develops a computational model based on fuzzy logic that could predict reading grade levels of online diabetes information.

Discussion:
The abstract does not clearly position the contribution against existing readability formulas.

Provide a clear and concise list of the main contributions at the end of the Introduction. This will help readers quickly understand the novelty and significance of the work.

Include a brief outline of the paper’s structure at the end of the Introduction to guide the reader through the flow of the manuscript.

Reorganize and structure the Related Work section more systematically (e.g., thematically, chronologically, or methodologically).
