It should be restructured to emphasize the rationale behind each step, describe the models and algorithms in detail, and provide sufficient mathematical or architectural descriptions to allow replication.

5. The RBWSSE method is introduced too briefly. The authors should explain how ReliefF feature weighting is integrated with subspace SVM ensembles.

6. It is unclear what the output of the RAL-CNN model represents. The authors state that its output is used as input to RBWSSE, suggesting that RAL-CNN acts as a feature extractor rather than a classifier. Clarification is needed: does RAL-CNN output class probabilities, feature embeddings, or attention-weighted representations? How are these features then processed by RBWSSE?

7. The RAL-CNN model alone achieves 73% accuracy. The addition of RBWSSE improves performance to 81.3%. The authors should explain why this ensemble strategy works better

8. The ablation experiments focus only on RAL-CNN, while the main result highlights RBWSSE. This is confusing.

Experimental design

Validity of the findings

Article Title: An explainable hyperparameter-optimized genetic algorithm–backpropagation (GA–BP) neural network model for medical software cost estimation
Abstract:
Background
Accurate and transparent cost estimation is pivotal for medical software, where regulatory constraints and integration complexity amplify the risk of misestimation. Traditional models rarely encode domain drivers or provide audit-ready explanations.

Methods
We propose an explainable, hyperparameter-optimized Genetic Algorithm–Backpropagation (GA–BP) neural network with SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP), denoted GA–BP+SHAP. Here, backpropagation (BP) refers to the standard feedforward neural network trained via error backpropagation, while SHAP provides interpretable feature attributions. A domain-aware feature set includes Compliance Rating (CR) and Clinical Workflow Coupling (CWC), the latter operationalized via process mining on directly-follows graphs. The dataset comprises N=1,200
 projects (670 Medical Information Mart for Intensive Care (MIMIC)-derived, 530 industry). We enforce leakage-safe preprocessing, a time-aware split (last 20% as holdout test), and group-aware nested cross-validation (company as group) for model selection. To address tail imbalance, we use within-fold Synthetic Minority Over-sampling with Gaussian Noise (SMOGN) and a quantile-weighted mean square error (MSE). A genetic algorithm tunes hidden units, activation, and learning rate under a matched evaluation budget. Interpretability uses KernelSHAP with expert review.

Results
The GA–BP model outperforms strong baselines (Elastic-Net, Support Vector Regression (SVR), Random Forest, eXtreme Gradient Boost (XGBoost)/Light Gradient-Boosting Machine (LightGBM), plain BP), achieving test mean magnitude of relative error (MMRE) ?
 21.9% and PRED(25) ?
 83.3%, with gains significant after Holm–Bonferroni correction. Calibration/residual diagnostics show reduced bias, especially in the high-cost tail. SHAP highlights CR and CWC as dominant drivers. Expert assessment reports substantial agreement with model attributions (Cohen’s ??0.73
, p<0.001
).

Conclusions
Combining domain-specific features with metaheuristic tuning and validated explainable artificial intelligence (AI) yields estimates that are both accurate and auditable. The framework supports budgeting, risk triage, and stakeholder communication in regulated settings. Limitations include concentration in three vendors and dependence on log quality for CWC; future work targets prospective, cross-organization validation and multi-objective optimization (effort, schedule, rework risk).
Review:
Basic reporting
The paper is written in clear and unambiguous English.

The literature review is sufficient, and the references are up-to-date.

The theoretical structure is well-structured.

Experimental design
The Clinical Workflow Coupling (CWC) feature is stated to be measured using process mining, but the method used is not specified.

The impact of project environment characteristics on project development processes could be discussed.

Information about whether the features used are related to each other or about the features that are related is lacking.

More information about the genetic operations should be provided, the reasons for their selection should be explained, and their impact on the model's success should be discussed.

The reason for choosing the KernelSHAP method should be stated.

The SHAP visuals should be further explained, and the significance of the feature relationships in real-world projects should be explained.

Validity of the findings
The paper could provide more information on how the developed model will work in the real world and how it will be implemented in real medical software. The potential challenges and opportunities of such model applications could be discussed.

Article Title: RNFS: a robust nature-inspired feature selection for remote sensing image classification
Abstract: Remote sensing data is characterized by its complexity and high-dimensional features. Therefore, developing effective feature selection techniques is crucial for minimizing redundancy, boosting computational performance, and achieving high accuracy. Nature-inspired optimization algorithms demonstrate a remarkable ability to efficiently select the most discriminant features. This article presents a novel nature-inspired hybrid metaheuristic algorithm (RNFS) for feature selection that effectively combines the exploitation strength of the Fox Optimization Algorithm (FOA) with the exploitation capacity of the Whale Optimization Algorithm (WOA). The proposed framework aims to improve classification precision and sensitivity while reducing overfitting. The datasets, including WHU-RS19, RSSCN7, and UCM, are used to compare the efficacy of RNFS with FOA and WOA. Experiments are conducted in a MATLAB-based environment, utilizing performance metrics such as accuracy, precision, sensitivity, specificity, and F1-score across 60 trials. The models evaluated included the wide neural network (WNN), cosine k-nearest neighbors (CKNN), and quadratic support vector machine (QSVM). Results demonstrate that the RNFS outperforms both FOA and WOA in all metrics for the WNN, CKNN, and QSVM classifiers, indicating an improvement in the true positive rate by reducing false positives. RNFS achieved the highest testing accuracy and exhibited robust classification performance, confirming its effectiveness for remote sensing applications.
Review:
Basic reporting
-

Experimental design
-

Validity of the findings
-

Additional comments
1. This study proposes a novel nature-inspired hybrid algorithm (RNFS) that combines the Fox Optimization Algorithm (FOA) and the Whale Optimization Algorithm (WOA) for effective feature selection in remote sensing data, and it is stated that RNFS outperforms both FOA and WOA in terms of classification accuracy and robustness across all classifiers.

2. In the introduction section, developments related to remote sensing, the importance of the subject, optimization techniques used, and difficulties encountered are mentioned. In addition, the main contributions of the study are clearly stated in an explanatory manner.

3. In the Related Works section, recent studies in the literature related to remote sensing are mentioned at a basic level. In this section, for this study to stand out more compared to the literature, a detailed literature table consisting of certain important columns such as "used dataset, originality, deficiencies, advantages, preprocessing" should be added, and then the difference of this study from the literature and the deficiencies in the literature it addresses should be emphasized more.

4. It is positive to choose more than one dataset within the scope of the study without sticking to a single dataset. However, since the number and type of classes in each dataset are different, it should be explained in more detail how common classes are determined and/or not determined in this study.

5. Considering the distribution of dataset amounts in the study, make a deep analysis of the data preprocessing/augmentation methods used in the studies carried out in the literature regarding this dataset for the remote sensing classification study, and compare them with your own study.

6. The Fox Optimization Algorithm and the Whale Optimization Algorithm are very positive in the study and have increased the quality of the study. However, it should be explained more clearly why these are preferred despite the fact that there are many different optimization algorithms compared to the literature.

7. When the Schematic representation of the methodology and the proposed nature-inspired hybrid algorithm are examined in detail, it is observed that they have a certain level of originality.

8. The use of cross-validation is very important for a more accurate analysis of classification problems. Since the results in classification problems are very dependent on the test dataset, how the distribution is made and its justification are very important.

9. All the details, such as the types, determination, and number of hyperparameters used in the classification study carried out in relation to the proposed model in the study, should be added and explained in a table.

10. Only basic evaluation metrics were obtained for the classification result analysis in the study, and it is very important to obtain all metrics completely for the correct analysis of the results and for the proposed model to come to the forefront. For this reason, it is very important to obtain all the metrics, "confusion matrix, receiver operating characteristic curve, area under the receiver operating characteristic curve score, Cohen’s kappa score, Matthew's correlation coefficient score" completely for the remote sensing classification results.

As a result, the study has the potential to contribute to the literature in the field of remote sensing. However, all of the above-mentioned items should be considered very carefully and each one should be considered absolutely completely, down to the smallest detail.
