Article Title: Contrast-enhanced MRI segmentation and classification with optimized vector machine classifier
Abstract: Brain tumors are a significant cause of mortality worldwide, highlighting the need for accurate and efficient diagnostic tools. Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) and Computed Tomography (CT) provide valuable imaging data; however, manual interpretation remains labor-intensive and prone to variability. This study introduces an automated framework for brain tumor detection that integrates image enhancement, segmentation, and classification. Preprocessing is performed using Contrast-Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE) and diffusion filtering to improve image clarity. Tumor regions are segmented through the Fast Marching Method (FMM), and classification is carried out using an optimized Support Vector Machine (SVM). Evaluation on a Contrast-Enhanced Magnetic Resonance Imaging (CE-MRI) dataset covering gliomas, meningiomas, and pituitary tumors demonstrates strong results, with sensitivity of 0.98, specificity of 0.99, overall accuracy of 98.6%, and a Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC) of 0.963. The proposed method achieves high performance while reducing processing time to 0.43 s per image, surpassing several existing techniques. These findings indicate that the framework offers a practical and efficient solution for clinical brain tumor diagnosis, with potential for further improvements through integration of multiple classifiers to enhance robustness.
Review:
Basic reporting
The manuscript is written in acceptable English but requires careful grammatical and structural refinement for clarity. Some sections (e.g., Section 2.2–3.4) contain long and repetitive descriptions that can be condensed. Figures and tables should be uniformly formatted and consistently referenced in the text.

The introduction provides a broad overview but lacks a strong statement of novelty. The authors should clarify what specific problem their FMM–SVM framework solves compared to prior FCM-SVM and CNN-SVM approaches.

The literature review is extensive but not critically synthesized. The authors should better connect past studies to the proposed method and highlight how this study improves over hybrid deep learning or optimization-based models published after 2022.

Some figure captions (e.g., Figs. 4–9) repeat similar phrases such as “Images are taken from a publicly available database…”; these should be summarized or moved to a single caption note.

Experimental design
The methodology is clearly described, but the scientific rigor can be improved. The proposed system heavily depends on a public CE-MRI dataset; however, no cross-dataset validation or comparison with BraTS or TCIA datasets is presented. The authors should justify why only CE-MRI was used and provide a discussion on generalizability.

The FMM segmentation algorithm is mathematically detailed, but there is no comparative computational complexity analysis to justify efficiency claims versus U-Net or CNN models.

The SVM optimization section lacks clarity on hyperparameter tuning: grid-search parameters, kernel width (?), and regularization constant (C) should be presented explicitly with corresponding validation curves.

The evaluation relies solely on accuracy, DSC, and specificity. The inclusion of confidence intervals is good, but statistical testing (e.g., Wilcoxon or t-test) against baselines should be reported to confirm significance.

Validity of the findings
Reported metrics (DSC = 0.963, accuracy = 98.6%) are impressive but seem unusually high for classical FMM–SVM frameworks. The authors should provide detailed confusion matrices and additional performance breakdowns to validate these results.

Figures 10–13 show qualitative outputs, but visual comparison with deep learning models (e.g., U-Net, ResNet) is limited. The authors should include at least one side-by-side comparison to verify their performance claim.

The Discussion section correctly identifies overfitting and dataset limitations but remains mostly qualitative. The authors should quantify training vs. validation performance to show whether early stopping or data augmentation mitigated overfitting.

The claim of “real-time feasibility at 0.43 s/image” needs hardware benchmarking details (CPU/GPU specs, memory usage, and code runtime verification).

Article Title: Augmenting large language models for financial sentiment analysis: a heuristic sparse mixture-of-experts framework
Abstract: Financial sentiment analysis (FSA) is essential for extracting actionable market insights from unstructured textual data to support investment decisions and risk management. Although large language models (LLMs) demonstrate remarkable capabilities in general natural language processing, they nevertheless face challenges in comprehensively capture the domain-specific semantics, numerical relationships, event impacts, and implicit sentiment expressions critical for robust financial sentiment interpretation within a unified framework. To address these limitations, this study proposes Heuristic Sparse Mixture-of-Experts framework for Financial Sentiment Analysis (HSMoE-FSA), a novel framework designed to augment LLMs for financial sentiment analysis. Specifically, the framework integrates four specialized LLM-based experts: Financial Semantic Comprehension (FinSem) for interpreting financial terminology, Financial Numeric Reasoning (FinNum) for modeling quantitative sensitivities, Financial Event Impact Assessment (FinEvent) for evaluating market-impact events, and Financial Implicit Sentiment Decoding (FinSent) for detecting latent sentiment cues. Leveraging an entropy-based routing mechanism, the framework dynamically activates optimal expert combinations through Top-K sparse gating and weighted aggregation, thereby enabling synergistic multi-dimensional feature fusion. Furthermore, a phased training strategy decouples expert module learning from router calibration to ensure stability. Extensive evaluations on benchmark datasets demonstrate that HSMoE-FSA achieves state-of-the-art performance, with weighted F1-scores ranging from 89.67% to 92.29%, representing absolute improvements of 0.72% to 1.74% over current leading methods. Moreover, qualitative analysis further confirms the framework’s superior capability in resolving nuanced financial semantics and implicit sentiment through expert collaboration.
Review:
Basic reporting
Strengths:
• The manuscript is clearly written in professional, academic English with excellent grammar and readability.
• The introduction provides a comprehensive background context, explaining the motivation behind Financial Sentiment Analysis (FSA) and the limitations of existing LLMs.
• Literature is extensive and up to date (e.g., references to Mixtral 2024, DeepSeek 2025, FinGPT, BloombergGPT, etc.), demonstrating awareness of current trends.
• Figures and tables (e.g., Table 1–9, Figure 1–3) are well-formatted, readable, and relevant to the discussion.
• The structure aligns with the journal norms: Abstract, Introduction, Related Work, Methodology, Experiments, Results, and Discussion.

Weaknesses & Suggested Improvements:
• While the introduction is strong, some sections (e.g., lines 43–72) could be condensed to reduce redundancy between background description and problem motivation.
• The Related Work section is comprehensive but overly detailed, reading like a literature survey rather than a critical synthesis. A concise comparison table summarizing key differences among prior FSA approaches could improve clarity.
• Some long mathematical expressions and pseudocode could be relegated to an appendix for readability.
• The manuscript occasionally overuses enumerated equations for textual explanations (e.g., Equations 11–14 could be described more intuitively).
• The term “heuristic” in “Heuristic Sparse Mixture-of-Experts” could be more explicitly justified early in the methodology—why this routing is heuristic rather than parametric.

Experimental design
Strengths:
* The study falls within the journal's domain, presenting a rigorous machine learning contribution.
* The experimental setup is detailed and transparent, listing datasets (FPB, FiQA-SA, TFNS), splits, preprocessing steps, and hyperparameters.
* Methods are well-documented: routing mechanism, entropy formulation, Top-K sparse gating, and phased optimization are all mathematically formalized.
* Reproducibility is good: dataset sources, links, and configurations (e.g., PyTorch, QLoRA settings, learning rates, epochs) are provided.
* The ablation studies (Tables 7–9) are comprehensive and demonstrate methodological rigor—particularly in validating expert contributions and optimization strategy.
* Evaluation metrics (Accuracy, Macro-F1, Weighted-F1) are appropriate for imbalanced datasets.

Weaknesses & Suggested Improvements:
* Although the hardware setup (4×A100 GPUs) is reported, the authors could enhance reproducibility by including a code repository or pseudo-config files for model training.
* Ethical considerations (e.g., data licenses, bias in financial datasets) are not explicitly discussed.
* While statistical robustness is implied through five random-seed runs, no significance testing (e.g., t-test or bootstrap confidence intervals) is provided.
* The study mentions “median results” but does not show variance or error bars—this reduces the transparency of stability claims.
* The description of data preprocessing could include tokenization details and language model input formatting for replicability.

Validity of the findings
Strengths:
* Experiments convincingly demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed model, achieving consistent improvements across three datasets (Weighted F1 gains of 0.7–1.7%).
* Results are well-documented and supported by quantitative and qualitative analyses.
* The authors provide solid reasoning for performance gains (entropy-based expert activation, phased optimization stability).
* Ablation studies reinforce internal validity by isolating component contributions (FinSem, FinNum, FinEvent, FinSent).
* Conclusions are coherent and aligned with the experimental evidence.

Weaknesses & Suggested Improvements:
* While improvements are statistically meaningful, they are relatively modest (<2%), and the authors should better contextualize the practical impact of these gains (e.g., in financial decision-making pipelines).
* The paper would benefit from a discussion of limitations—such as computational cost, model interpretability, and potential overfitting to curated financial text.
* Future directions (e.g., adaptation to multilingual financial texts or real-time inference scenarios) are only briefly hinted at; these could be elaborated to show research continuity.

Additional comments
* Figures (especially Figure 1) are visually dense; a schematic simplification could aid reader comprehension.
* The paper’s academic tone is consistent and professional.
* Citations are adequate and relevant, though the reference formatting should be double-checked for consistency with the journal style (some capitalization issues and URL formatting irregularities).
* It would be useful to include a short appendix summarizing computational complexity or inference latency.

The manuscript presents a technically sound, original, and well-written contribution to financial sentiment analysis using large language models and sparse mixture-of-experts frameworks. It is suitable for the journal after revision. However, several aspects—particularly the statistical validation, code/data reproducibility, and discussion of computational/ethical considerations—require strengthening before acceptance.

Summary of Required Revisions:
* Condense background and clarify heuristic routing motivation.
* Add significance testing or error bars to reinforce result reliability.
* Include reproducibility artifacts (GitHub repository or training configuration).
* Discuss ethical implications and dataset biases.
* Elaborate on model limitations and future work directions.

Article Title: SOSCNV: a stochastic outlier selection algorithm-based method for copy number variation detection using hybrid strategy
Abstract:
Background
Copy number variation (CNV) refers to the duplication or deletion of DNA sequences in the genome. It plays an important role in organism development and disease occurrence.

Methods
Read-depth (RD)–based strategies are among the most widely used approaches for CNV detection; however, RD signals typically allow only approximate breakpoint localization. In contrast, split-read (SR) methods can provide much more precise breakpoint resolution. As a result, hybrid RD+SR strategies have become increasingly popular in recent CNV detection frameworks. Nevertheless, these hybrid methods still exhibit limitations, particularly in detecting CNVs of diverse lengths, where performance may degrade for very short or very long variants. A new CNV detection method called SOSCNV (Detection of Copy Number Variations based on Stochastic Outlier Selection) is proposed in this article. In order to enhance the recognition and processing ability of those difficult-to-detect areas, and also to make the detection of breakpoints more accurate, SOSCNV incorporates a two-stage segmentation technique. SOS algorithm is used to model the RD signal and Guanine and Cytosine (GC) content, and calculate the correlation between datasets to identify anomalous datasets. Compared with traditional RD+SR hybrid detection strategies, SOSCNV is better suited for identifying CNVs across a wide range of variant lengths and demonstrates stronger robustness under varying sequencing depths and tumor purities.

Results
After testing on 900 simulated datasets and four real datasets, and comparing with four existing CNV detection tools, experiments show that SOSCNV can significantly improve detection precision, recall and F1-score.
Review:
Basic reporting
The authors present SOSCNV, a novel two-stage method for copy number variation (CNV) detection in the human genome, leveraging both split-read and read-depth signals to improve breakpoint precision. This hybrid approach holds promise for enhancing CNV detection efficiency. However, significant concerns remain regarding the benchmarking strategy, real-data evaluation, and computational performance assessment, necessitating substantial revisions.

Experimental design
The experimental design of this study follows standard benchmarking practices by evaluating the method on both simulated and real datasets, which is a routine approach for assessing CNV detection algorithms. While the benchmarking strategy is appropriate, there is no significant novelty in the experimental setup beyond what is typically expected. However, there are also no major flaws in the design. One important aspect missing from the evaluation is a comparison of computational efficiency. The authors should include an analysis of runtime and resource usage across different methods to demonstrate the efficiency of SOSCNV relative to existing tools. This addition would provide a more comprehensive assessment of the method’s practicality for large-scale genomic studies.

Validity of the findings
Major comments:
1. Evaluation on Simulated CNVs and Benchmarks
a. The simulation dataset is currently restricted to chromosome 21, which is acrocentric and not representative of the entire genome. Simulations should be extended across the entire genome.
b. The simulated CNVs range from 1–9 Kb, which does not fully reflect the natural spectrum of CNVs that can range from 50 bp to megabases. Larger CNVs (?10 Kb) are more likely to have functional impacts, and their absence in the benchmarking may bias the results. Additionally, the manuscript states that “the RD strategy-based methods perform poorly in identifying small CNVs in low-tumor-purity samples.” This further highlights the necessity of evaluating larger CNVs (?10 Kb), which RD-based methods typically detect more reliably. The authors should report the performance of SOSCNV and existing methods across a wider CNV size range. The authors should include simulations of CNVs ?10 Kb and up to megabase scale.
2. Evaluation on Real Data: Yoruba Trio from 1000 Genomes Project (1KGP)
a. The authors evaluated CNV calls using Yoruba trio samples from the 1KGP. For these samples, validated CNV calls have been published in numerous previous studies, including Chaisson et al., 2019, Ebert et al., 2021, and Byrska-Bishop et al., 2022. Dataset from these studies should be used as the gold standard for the benchmarking, instead of constructing a reference set by identifying overlapping CNV regions across multiple methods. The current benchmarking results does not accurately reflect the true precision and recall of the evaluated algorithms.
b. The Overlap Density Score (ODS) used in the manuscript is not a standard CNV benchmarking metric. The reciprocal overlap metric is the most widely accepted method for CNV evaluation and should be used instead.
c. Since the Yoruba trio samples are from the same family, Mendelian violation rate is a reliable and should be the most reliable approach for CNV evaluation. This should be incorporated into the benchmarking analyses.
d. The chord diagram (Figure 4) is overly complex and visually cluttered, making it difficult to interpret key findings. The authors should consider using bar plots or summary tables for clearer comparisons and ensure color schemes are consistent across all three samples.
3. Computational Performance Evaluation
a. As an algorithmic improvement, it is critical to report computational cost explicitly. The manuscript currently lacks a comparison of runtime and resource requirements across methods. The authors should provide a detailed comparison of SOSCNV and competing methods in terms of hardware specifications (e.g., server configuration, CPU/GPU cores), runtime performance (e.g., time taken per sample), and memory usage and scalability across different datasets.

Additional comments
Minor comments:
1. The manuscript requires significant language improvement, as several sections contain unclear expressions, grammatical errors, and punctuation inconsistencies. For example, in the section “2. Real Experimental Analysis of SOS Method,” the referenced figure is unclear—please specify which figure is being discussed. The manuscript should undergo thorough proofreading to ensure clarity and readability.
2. The term “significant” is used multiple times throughout the manuscript without accompanying p-values. Statistical significance should be quantified using appropriate p-values and confidence intervals to support claims, with the underlying statistical models clearly described in the method section
3. Figures 3 and 4 have low resolution, making it difficult to interpret the results. Figures should be provided in high resolution to ensure clarity.

Article Title: Interpretable prediction of gross motor coordination in children aged 9–10 using machine learning and SHAP: the influence of physical fitness, basic coordination, and executive function
Abstract:
Background
Gross motor coordination is a fundamental component of children’s physical development and motor skill acquisition, closely associated with physical fitness, cognitive function, and overall health. This study aimed to examine the influence of physical fitness, basic coordination, and executive function (EF) on gross motor coordination, and to evaluate the predictive performance of machine learning models compared with traditional multiple linear regression (MLR).

Methods
A total of 167 children (85 boys and 82 girls), aged 9–10 years, participated in the study. Gross motor coordination was assessed using the Körperkoordinationtest für Kinder (KTK). Physical fitness (e.g., 50 m sprint, standing long jump, sit-ups), basic coordination (e.g., kinesthetic differentiation, spatial orientation, balance), and EF (e.g., inhibitory control, working memory) were measured as predictors. Model performance was evaluated using R2, root mean square error (RMSE), and mean absolute error (MAE). SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) were applied to interpret the best-performing model and analyze feature importance and nonlinear effects.

Results
Among the models, Random Forest Regression (RFR) achieved the highest performance (R2 = 0.533, RMSE = 6.075, MAE = 4.850). SHAP analysis revealed that spatial orientation, body mass index (BMI), dynamic balance, standing long jump, and closed-eye balance were the most important predictors, with spatial orientation, BMI, and closed-eye balance showing notable nonlinear effects. EF contributed minimally to prediction.

Conclusion
Spatial-body integration, physical fitness, and postural control are primary determinants of gross motor coordination in children, while cognitive regulation plays a secondary role. Training programs aiming to enhance gross motor coordination should emphasize spatial orientation, body weight management, balance, and lower-limb strength.
Review:
Basic reporting
The article is written in clear and professional English. The structure is complete (abstract, introduction, methods, results, discussion, conclusion). The references are adequate and up-to-date.

Figures and tables are relevant, but the captions could be clearer to ensure readers understand them.

Suggestions:

Slight improvements in language for conciseness.

Add a flow diagram summarizing the research process (from data collection to SHAP analysis).

Experimental design
This is original research that fits within the journal’s scope. The research question is clear: predicting gross motor coordination in children using machine learning.

The methods are well-explained and reproducible. Ethical approval, participant criteria, and test procedures are well-documented.

Suggestions:

Justify the choice of the 80:20 train-test split ratio.

Report cross-validation results or consider additional resampling for model validation due to the sample size.

Validity of the findings
The results are logical and statistically sound, directly answering the research question. The Random Forest Regression model performed best (R˛ = 0.533), outperforming linear regression (R˛ = 0.465). SHAP analysis identified key predictors like spatial orientation, BMI, balance, and lower-limb strength.

Suggestions:

Discuss the limitations of the study, such as the sample size.

Clarify the connection between results and the original research question.

Additional comments
Overall, the study is interesting and scientifically valuable. The use of machine learning analysis and SHAP for interpretability is well done in the context of children’s sports science.

Article Title: Enhancing text-to-structured query language translation for seamless electronic medical record access
Abstract: Traditional models for natural language-to-SQL translation in Electronic Medical Record (EMR) systems struggle with understanding medical terminology, handling complex queries, and bridging the syntax-semantics gap, leading to scalability and accuracy issues. Advanced solutions like Large Language Model (LLM) based approaches address these challenges by leveraging deep learning and domain-specific training to enhance performance and usability. Hence, this article introduces an advanced medical Text-to-Structured Query Language (SQL) paradigm that simplifies accessing EMRs by translating natural language queries into SQL commands. This model is built on the advanced Code-T5 (Text-to-Text Transfer Transformer) architecture, further enhanced with Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) and Quantized Low-Rank Adaptation (QLoRA) techniques; it effectively addresses the challenges posed by the complexity of traditional SQL queries enabling seamless access to critical healthcare data. The innovation of the proposed model lies in its exceptional performance across multiple evaluation metrics. It achieves a Bilingual Evaluation Understudy (BLEU) score of 81.68, significantly outperforming leading models like T5, Fine-Tuned Language Net (FLAN) T5, and Bidirectional and Auto-Regressive Transformers (BART) while excelling in Recall-Oriented Understudy for Gisting Evaluation (ROUGE) metrics, underscoring its proficiency in generating semantically accurate and coherent SQL queries. Furthermore, the proposed model attains a high token-level F1-score, ensuring a balanced precision and recall and a Jaccard similarity score of 0.83, surpassing T5, Flan T5, and BART. The proposed model excels in handling complex medical queries, bridging natural language and SQL to empower data-driven decisions and advance medical informatics.
Review:
Basic reporting
This article uses clear and professional english. The language used here is easy to understand and conveys the context of the research very nicely.

The introduction clearly establishes the goal of the paper and describes the background as well.

The paper adheres to PeerJ norms. It includes a simple yet clear introduction. It explains the experiment very clearly with backing data, reproduction script. Individual references are properly noted.

The introduction and the abstract succinctly and clearly explains the background and states the goal of his paper.

The results of the paper are backed by supportive data, baking studies and the python program. This provides a solid means to recreate experiments.

Experimental design
The article presents a large language model to convert text to SQL query for patient records. All the content falls under the scope of the subject and related to the context set in the introduction.

Various methods are utilized to analyze the quality of the generated SQL query. It is tested against well established models using 6 different measuring standards.

Data set, code for reproduction are provided in the appendix. So I believe it is quite sufficient to reproduce the experiment.

Data selection and preprocessing is described in the paper. It only describes a few data models for medical patient data. It makes the dataset narrow and the resulting SQL becomes very specific to the use case.

Data and model selection methods are properly explained in the paper. Metrics and results are provided in the appendix.

References are properly quoted and data is correctly annotated with personally identifiable records removed.

Validity of the findings
The experiment is well suited for medical patent data. The data set is not very broad. The resulting SQL queries are simple and specific to the category of the data set only. But the backing data set and reproduction script is provided in the paper that is sufficient to reproduce the experiment.

Conclusion is supported by findings and data. It states the goal setup in the introduction.

The experiment provides a significant amount of data backing the result. It provides the reproduction script and data set used for the experiment.

The goal is well supported by the experiment and backed by the data attached in the appendix.

The conclusion provides limitations of the current experiment and future directions.

Additional comments
This paper is very well articulated and supported by good amount of reproduction script, code and data set. Even tough the result of the experiment is simple SQL queries generated by the LLM model only suited for specific use cases, the paper calls that out as a limitation and clarifies it as a future direction.

The efficacy of the experiment is well supported by comparing it with standard generative models using 6 different methods of measuring efficiency.

Article Title: Environmental drivers of Catostylus tagi polyp survival and reproduction: unlocking the role of temperature and salinity, supported with citizen science data
Abstract:
Background
Catostylus tagi is a scyphozoan jellyfish native to Portuguese waters. While its life cycle is known, the environmental conditions that support polyp survival, trigger strobilation, and promote asexual reproduction remain unclear. Field observations from the citizen science GelAvista project indicate that C. tagi occurs year-round in the Tagus estuary, suggesting tolerance to broad temperature and salinity ranges. However, polyps and ephyrae have not been observed in the wild, and their natural habitats and environmental preferences remain unknown. This study aims to fill these knowledge gaps by investigating how temperature and salinity affect C. tagi polyps under controlled conditions and integrating these results with field data.

Methods
Ninety-six polyps were cultured for 71 days in well plates at four temperature treatments (14, 17, 20, 23 °C) and four salinity levels (10, 17.5, 25, 35). Survival, asexual reproduction, and strobilation were monitored. Seven years of citizen science data were analysed with environmental parameters to assess estuarine distribution.

Results
Polyp survival was high, except at salinity 35, where mortality increased regardless of temperature. Podocyst production was enhanced at higher temperatures (20–23 °C) and intermediate salinities (17.5–25), although podocyst development into new polyps occurred at lower salinities (10–17.5). Strobilation occurred predominantly at intermediate temperatures (17–20 °C) and salinities (17.5–25). Medusae are most frequently found in the lower Tagus estuary and coastal adjacent areas, exhibiting a peak between July and January. Results showed that wind intensity was negatively correlated with medusa stranding abundances on the shores, possibly displacing individuals away from coastal areas during periods of strong winds. Sea surface temperature (SST), measured two to four months prior to medusae occurrence, was positively correlated with C. tagi abundance.

Discussion
Optimal polyp performance occurred at 17–20 °C and 17.5–25 salinity. When considered alongside citizen science observations, these findings suggest that polyps may occur in estuarine areas where such conditions prevail, such as upper estuary inlets and marinas. It is further hypothesized that ephyrae could disperse to downstream and adjacent coastal zones via wind and tides. The correlation between sightings and past SSTs indicates the ephyrae and young medusae dispersal rate. Furthermore, this study highlights C. tagi’s adaptability to varying environmental conditions and contributes to identifying optimal parameters for polyp well-being and reproduction, with potential applications in jellyfish farming.
Review:
Basic reporting
Good manuscript, except for a single flaw that I describe in detail in the Additional comments

Experimental design
Appropriate

Validity of the findings
well supported by data analysis

Additional comments
Review of ID-121739
This study aims to define the effect of temperature and salinity on the survival, podocyst production, and strobilation of polyps of the scyphomedusa Catostylus tagi under controlled conditions to define the best conditions for these organisms. I really enjoyed reading this manuscript as it is well organized and written, the language is clear, and all the sections are detailed. The experimental design and the statistical analyses are well-suited for the purpose. So I was very disappointed by the fact that the analysis of citizen science data tricked the authors and became a flaw for this nice paper. The authors state that the analysis of these data is carried out to corroborate the experimental results, but in reality, they give them a secondary and often unclear role. They describe the methodology used for data analysis in the Materials and Methods, make tables with results, but then they completely miss a subsection in the Results with the actual results from data analysis. Then these results reappear in the Discussion.

I suggest that the authors add a subsection with the results related to citizenj data analysis and revise the whole manuscript in light of the following comments and suggestions. In my opinion, these changes will make the manuscript suitable for publication.

Detailed comments

Abstract
In the Discussion subsection, results from field observations are mentioned, but they are not clearly described in the Results subsection, which makes it a little difficult to catch the match. Just a sentence will be enough.

L52: I guess the authors mean that the jellyfish will potentially be cultured. Because the word id mostly referred to fish, it may be better to replace it with “jellyfish farming” just to avoid potential confusion.

iNTRODUCTION
L120-129: I suggest reorganizing the aims of the study. In the present form, the analysis of data collected through the citizen science project appears as a side part, while it gains importance at the conclusion. It should be elevated to the rank of objective, with a number. It may replace objective 2, as both 1 and 2 allow for improving culturing under controlled conditions and understanding population dynamics in nature. I am not sure how we can “control” a population of jellyfish in their natural environment. Maybe the authors should explain better or find a word that matches better with both natural and controlled environments.

C. tagi has been suggested for exploitation within the context of biotechnological applications. This aspect may support the need for management of wild populations and the need for culturing in captivity (see, for example: CALEJO, M. T., MORAIS, Z. B. & FERNANDES, A. I. 2009. Isolation and biochemical characterisation of a novel collagen from Catostylus tagi. Journal of Biomaterials Science, Polymer Edition, 20, 2073-2087).

Bibliography
The biblio is a little too outdated…I suggest that the authors read relevant papers, such as:
LUCAS, C. H., GRAHAM, W. M. & WIDMER, C. 2012. Jellyfish life histories: role of polyps in forming and maintaining scyphomedusa populations. Advances in Marine Biology, 63, 133-196.
SCHIARITI, A., HOLST, S., TISEO, G. R., MIYAKE, H. & MORANDINI, A. C. 2024. Chapter Four - Life cycles and reproduction of Rhizostomeae. In: MORANDINI, A. C., HOLST, S. & REINICKE, G. B. (eds.) Advances in Marine Biology. Academic Press.

**PeerJ Staff Note:** It is PeerJ policy that additional references suggested during the peer-review process should only be included if the authors are in agreement that they are relevant and useful.

Article Title: Spatio-temporal overlap between purse seine fisheries and Humboldt penguin feeding areas in northern Chile
Abstract: Seabirds face increasing pressure from commercial fisheries through both direct mortality and indirect competition for shared prey resources. In Chile’s Humboldt Current System, artisanal purse-seine fisheries target small pelagic fish species such as anchoveta (Engraulis ringens), which also comprise key components of the Humboldt penguin’s (Spheniscus humboldti) diet. To investigate the potential for spatio-temporal overlap between purse-seine fisheries and penguin foraging areas, we tracked breeding Humboldt penguins from Isla Choros, Coquimbo Region, northern Chile, during the autumn and spring breeding seasons of 2022 using GPS dive loggers. We quantified penguin at-sea distribution and overlap with fishing effort data obtained from purse-seine vessels operating in the Coquimbo Region. Tracking data from 22 penguins revealed a bimodal foraging pattern linked to nest location. Penguins nesting on the eastern, mainland-facing side of Isla Choros foraged exclusively in coastal waters, while western-nesting individuals foraged both offshore and inshore, likely depending on environmental conditions. Kernel Density Estimation (KDE) analysis identified two core foraging zones: one along the mainland coast near Playa de los Choros and another southwest of the island. Purse-seine fishing effort in autumn was concentrated along the coast, overlapping substantially with the coastal foraging zone of the penguins. Nearly 60% of the penguins’ coastal core foraging area overlapped with the 50% KDE of autumn purse-seine activity. In contrast, spring fishing effort was more dispersed and located farther south, with no overlap observed between spring fishing and penguin foraging areas. These findings suggest a high likelihood of indirect resource competition between Humboldt penguins and fisheries during the autumn breeding season—a critical time when penguins are energetically constrained as central place foragers. We provide the first empirical evidence of substantial seasonal overlap between Humboldt penguins and inshore purse-seine fisheries at the Humboldt Archipelago and highlight the need to integrate seabird ecology into fisheries management. To protect vulnerable seabird populations such as the Humboldt penguin, marine spatial planning should consider critical foraging habitats and breeding schedules. Future work should aim to quantify dietary overlap, assess potential sub-lethal effects of prey depletion, and monitor the impact of small-scale, untracked fisheries within key penguin foraging areas.
Review:
Basic reporting
The manuscript is quite interesting for the emerging field of effective marine spatial planning by taking into consideration the ecological requirements of a vulnerable top predator, namely the Humboldt penguin (Spheniscus humboldti). As far as I know, it is the first paper that actually evaluates and presents information on the spatial overlap between the foraging distribution pattern of the target species and commercial fisheries (purse seine) in Northern Chile.

Experimental design
In general, the manuscript is properly structured, adequately refers to other relevant studies with few exceptions. The data set as well as the methodology used are adequate to sustain results (including figures and tables), discussion, and conclusions, but I have a main concern regarding the experimental design. More specifically, I strongly believe that authors have to include in their distribution models other satellite environmental parameters (e.g., sea surface temperature, bathymetry, sea surface chlorophyll, sea level anomaly, etc.) and not only fishing effort based on purse seine fisheries.

Validity of the findings
I suggest that authors have to include oceanographic parameters in their modeling process as key factors explaining the at-sea distribution of the Humboldt penguin breeders. Then, this outcome could provide robust results regarding the main aim of the current study, which is to serve as a management tool for fisheries-seabird interactions aimed at reducing potential conflicts between conservation and exploitation of marine resources. In addition, another significant limitation is that the current work didn’t take into account the possible impact of small-scale fisheries in the study area. I wonder if the authors had the chance to use relevant fishing pressure indices.

Additional comments
Materials and Methods

Lines 122-123. “The 301-hectare island lies ... during the study period.”. Provide a reference for the breeding population estimate of the study area.
Lines 137-139. “Breeding generally begins … in two main peaks: autumn and spring (Paredes et al. 2002, Simeone et al. 2002).” I feel that it is better to use months instead of austral seasons since the latter could be confusing for the international audience of the Journal.

Lines 164-165. “To identify the foraging areas … GPS loggers (TechnoSmart, Italy) on adult birds.” I suppose that the tags were GPS/GSM since you didn’t have to recover them by recapturing the tagged birds. Is that correct? Please elaborate.

Lines 199-200. “Before examining all penguins…define areas most frequently used by penguins.” Is that assumption based on other relevant studies? Please clarify and/or provide reference(s).

Lines 216-219. “The area of interest… during early chick-rearing.” Add reference (s)

Results
Line 326. Use “Two distinct 50% KDE areas were identified (Figure 3): one located west/southwest (and partly east) of …” instead of “Two distinct 50% KDE areas were identified: one located west/southwest (and partly east) of …”

Discussion
Lines 369-370. “The consistency of these patterns … as a key foraging habitat.” What about other environmental parameters, e.g., depth or SST? Please check again my general comment on the design of the spatial distribution model that you used.

Lines 421-424. “Zooplankton - key prey for pelagic fish species …successful foraging over shallower seabeds (Aarflot et al. 2020).” Again, check my general comment for the design of the spatial distribution model that you used.

Lines 473-474. “This overlap suggests …to negatively impact penguin foraging success.” I feel that you have to skip that sentence since you don’t have relevant data for that assumption.

Article Title: Plant origin and irrigation influence floral resource value and pollinator attraction to ornamental plants
Abstract: Irrigation and plant origin are key factors influencing plant growth, development, and reproductive strategies. These same factors may also affect the floral resource value of plants to pollinators, influencing the efficacy of pollinator plantings for conservation or enhancement. While these factors have been studied separately, few studies have examined how plant origin influences plant responses to irrigation for floral traits such as nectar and pollen. In this study, we evaluated 10 native and 10 non-native plants to Florida under two different irrigation treatments and measured floral density, floral display, floral resources (nectar and pollen), and pollinator visitation rates and diversity in an open field study. There was strong to very strong evidence that native plants produced higher floral densities and displays, along with greater amounts of pollen per plot, and attracted a higher number and diversity of pollinators as compared to non-native plants. Additionally, there was weak to moderate evidence that fully irrigated plants produced higher nectar volumes and pollen quantities per plot as well as pollen with higher protein content as compared to partially irrigated plants. However, there was no evidence that native and non-native plants responded differently to irrigation treatment. Additionally, individual pollinator guilds responded differently to origin and irrigation, highlighting the complexity of plant-pollinator interactions. We conclude that native plants consistently produce more flowers and attract a greater number and diversity of pollinators while irrigation affects the production and resource value of pollen and nectar. These findings emphasize the importance of considering both plant origin and irrigation on resource availability of flowers when selecting pollinator-friendly plants under varying climates and management regimes.
Review:
Basic reporting
This manuscript reports how floral display (floral density), floral resources (nectar and pollen), and pollinators (richness and visitation rate) are influenced by the origin of ornamental garden plants, as well as irrigation level (partial or full). The manuscript is well written and well-cited. The figures are clear to read, and the results are relevant to the hypotheses that are presented.

Experimental design
The research presented is original and timely. Although other studies have looked at the impact of plant origin on pollinators, this is perhaps the first to present data from an experiment that fully crosses a native plant origin treatment with an irrigation treatment, and measures floral display, floral resources, and pollinator metrics.

My major concern with this manuscript has to do with the categorization of plants as either native or non-native. I suggest that the authors add a table as an appendix, which includes more detailed information on the plant's native range. Note that the authors categorize Bidens alba (L.) as a native plant, even though some do not consider this plant to be native to Florida (the site of this study). See this link for more information: https://www.flawildflowers.org/flower-friday-bidens-alba/. Other native plants included in this study include Contrandina grandiflora, which is a threatened, local endemic. The point I am trying to make is that both of these plants are classified as 'native' in this study, even though they fall in different regions of the spectrum of what could be considered a native plant.

Similarly, the non-native plants include straight species (Bidens ferulifolia), hybrids (Salvia longispicata X S. farinacea), and hybrid cultivars (Coreopsis X 'Jethro Tull). These plants also fall along different points of the 'non native plant' spectrum, which may have implications for pollinators (see our recent study, Hayes et al. 2025, where we found that plants with wild-type phenotypes, whether natives or cultivars, attracted more pollinators). For the non-native plants, the degree of cultivation/plant breeding could influence pollinator visitation, floral resources, etc.

Validity of the findings
For the statistical analyses, I am wondering if it might be prudent to include 'plant ID' as a fixed effect factor, or if the authors considered the impact of 'plant ID'. I suggest this because the authors note that non-native salvia seemed to be driving bumble bee and carpenter bee preference for non-native plants (lines 403 -405). In our study of native plants and native cultivars, we also found that results varied by plant group/plant genus: some groups showed no difference between cultivars and native plants, and others showed big differences (Hayes et al. 2025).

Additional comments
A well-written and important contribution that will be of interest to ecologists, horticulturists, and the general public. I look forward to citing this paper in the future!

Article Title: Species-specific community structure in the microbiomes and eukaryotic communities associated with Mediterranean golf ball sponges
Abstract:
Background
Sponges harbor complex and diverse microbiomes that contribute to the host’s fitness and, ultimately, the health of the ecosystems sponges inhabit.

Methods
Using high-throughput 16S and 18S rRNA amplicon sequencing, we explore the prokaryotic and eukaryotic communities associated with three sympatric Mediterranean demosponges, namely Tethya aurantium, Tethya meloni, and Tethya citrina.

Results
We found species-specific prokaryotic and eukaryotic communities despite the close sympatry of the three Mediterranean Tethya species studied. This offers further support for the phylogenetic nature of the sponge microbiome, where microbial communities reflect the evolutionary ancestry of their host species. These patterns are both present in the eukaryotic and prokaryotic sponge-associated communities, since both display similar levels of host species specificity.
Review:
Basic reporting
The Authors aimed at characterizing the prokaryotic and eukaryotic diversity associated with three sympatric sponge species of the genus Tethya by ways of amplicon sequencing of taxonomic markers (16S rRNA and 18S rRNA genes, respectively). Eukaryotic diversity associated with sponges remains largely uncharacterized. Prokaryotic diversity in these sponges was dominated by Proteobacteria. As expected, the microbial diversity associated with these sponges showed a species-specific pattern. The Authors report more similarities in the prokaryotic diversity in T. meloni and T. aurantium, than T. citrina. The eukaryotic diversity in the sponges was also species-specific and Annelida, Nematoda and Ciliophora were among the phyla with the highest relative abundance.
The manuscript is well written and the methodology and results are presented in well detail. According to the data deposition statement, the reads are deposited in ENA and the code is provided in Github. I would like to kindly ask the Authors to make available the OTU tables with the relative abundances per sample and the full taxonomic assignment for each OTU, if not already in the repository.
• The Authors should put their findings into context by comparing them to those in Santodomingo et al. 2024, a revision on Tethya species taxonomy that includes the microbial diversity characterization (16S rRNA gene) of several Tethya species, including T. citrina and T. aurantium.
Santodomingo, N.; Díez-Vives, C.; Kenny, N. J.; Cárdenas, P.; Balcells, L.; Moles, J.; Zea, S.; Giribet, G.; Lanna, E.; Gracia-Sancha, C.; Riesgo, A. Integrative Taxonomy of Tethya: Description of Four New Species Based on Morphology, Phylogeny and Microbiome Diversity. Systematics and Biodiversity 2024, 22 (1), 2383341. https://doi.org/10.1080/14772000.2024.2383341

Experimental design
No comment

Validity of the findings
Conclusions are well supported. As mentioned above, the results should be put into context with relevant literature.

Additional comments
• I miss further discussion on whether the eukaryotic sequences retrieved here correspond to macro-symbionts of the sponges. It seems to be the case for Kinorhyncha (L402-403), but what is it possible to make an educated guess for the other groups?

• L436-439: I don’t follow the reasoning here, since LMA-HMA status is based on bacterial concentrations compared to seawater concentrations. The Authors may consider provide further arguments to this statement (e.g., compare T. citrina diversity indices to other HMA and LMAs) or remove it.

Article Title: Seasonality shapes gut microbiota composition in two sympatric sea urchins
Abstract:
Background
The gut microbiota plays critical roles in digestion, immunity, and environmental adaptation in marine invertebrates. Its composition is shaped by both host traits and abiotic factors such as temperature and seasonality. In Caribbean reefs, sea urchins like Diadema antillarum and Echinometra lucunter are important grazers that regulate algal biomass and influence benthic community dynamics. This study used 16S rRNA gene sequencing to compare, for the first time, the gut microbiota of these two sympatric species across contrasting seasons.

Methods
Adults of D. antillarum and E. lucunter were collected during summer and winter from three fringing reefs in Puerto Rico. Gut contents were extracted under sterile conditions, and bacterial DNA was extracted amplified for their 16S rRNA genes and sequenced. Microbial diversity and structure were assessed standard microbiota pipelines.

Results
Microbial communities in both echinoids were dominated by Bacillota, Bacteroidota, Desulfobacterota, and Pseudomonadota. Core microbiota analysis revealed stable taxa across hosts and seasons, most notably Propionigenium, supporting host-driven selection mechanisms that maintain functional stability despite seasonal turnover. Core microbiome analyses revealed Bacillota, Bacteroidota, Desulfobacterota_G_459543, and Pseudomonadota as persistent phyla across seasons, and genera such as Desulfotalea, Photobacterium, and Propionigenium consistently present in both species. Alpha diversity was significantly higher in winter for both D. antillarum and E. lucunter, while species-level differences were not significant. Beta diversity analyses showed significant seasonal clustering, with no consistent segregation between species within seasons. Our findings demonstrate that shared environmental conditions, particularly seasonality, are the primary modulators of gut microbiota in sympatric sea urchins, while host selection preserves a conserved core community. This dual influence of environment and host highlights the ecological plasticity and resilience of sea urchin microbiotas under fluctuating reef conditions.
Review:
Basic reporting
The background and context for the study is well stated, however more details regarding the diet of these two urchin species would be useful. Which species of algae do each feed on and has there been any microbiome work on these algae to get an idea of what may be algae/food-associated vs urchin-associated.

As well it would be better to report data along with species names for the urchins as opposed to red vs black.

The figures need some work to improve readability. For example, the coloring of the bar graphs in Figure 6 is too close, so using more distinct colors would enhance readability. As well legend labels for the other figures are needed (i.e. what do "low" and "high" refer to exactly). The supplementary figures are nice and I feel like they may actually be more useful as main text figures.

I thank the authors for making the raw data public, however with microbiome studies I feel it is also useful to provide ASV tables, taxonomy tables, and fasta files of sequences if possible.

Experimental design
My primary issue revolves around the data merging aspect. From my understanding, the authors collected 36 summer samples for the urchins, then merged this with other public data for the main comparisons made in this study. It appears from the methods that these datasets were processed independently then merged at a later step, potentially greatly influencing the results. In this case, I would hope the same processing parameters, sequencing technology, and reference database version were used for all included datasets, however this is not clear and may explain the differences between winter and summer observed, especially in the beta and alpha diversity measurements. To address this, the authors can process all the data in the same manner (merging at the very beginning of read processing) or conduct these community analysis at a higher taxonomic level, disregarding ASVs.

As well water samples were collected and sequenced, however not reported on at all.

Additionally, beta-diversity analyses should be conducted using Aitchison distances as microbiome data is compositional (See here: https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/microbiology/articles/10.3389/fmicb.2017.02224/full)

Validity of the findings
The validity of the findings greatly depends on more details in how the datasets were merged and compared, as well as a proper analysis of the water samples collected.

Additional comments
The study is biologically interesting and the work done here is important. With clarity on the methods and re-analysis if necessary I feel like this study will provide a good resource for the field.

Article Title: The distribution characteristics of medusozoans (Cnidaria) related to environmental factors in cold source water intake area of the Fangchenggang Nuclear Power Plant based on eDNA metabarcoding technology
Abstract: The free-swimming gelatinous stage of medusozoans, generally called ‘jellyfish’, frequently swarms around the intake area of the Fangchenggang Nuclear Power Plant, posing a noticeable risk to its operational safety. Traditional ecological survey methods have limitations in rapidly and effectively assessing the diversity and spatiotemporal distribution of medusozoans, particularly during their early life stages, such as planulae, polyps, and ephyrae. To compensate for these limitations, we employed environmental DNA (eDNA) metabarcoding technology—a non-invasive, efficient, and broadly applicable approach—to conduct targeted surveys during the winter and summer seasons. The findings revealed notable seasonal variations in medusozoan community composition, with 29 and 56 species identified in winter and summer, respectively (p < 0.05). A higher abundance of Hydrozoa and relatively fewer Scyphozoa were observed in both seasons. Horizontally, no significant differences were identified among the defined zones (p > 0.05); however, specific sites such as estuarine, water intake, and drainage areas exhibited unique environmental features influencing the distribution of medusozoans. Vertically, within the 3–8 m depth range, no significant differences in the distribution of them were observed, likely due to the limited depth range and enhanced vertical mixing of the water column (p > 0.05). These observations underscore the utility of eDNA metabarcoding for the rapid detection and assessment of medusozoan diversity and spatiotemporal distribution. As a valuable supplement to traditional survey techniques, this method enhances medusozoan monitoring in marine areas adjacent to Fangchenggang Nuclear Power Plant. Moreover, it enables the investigation of how temperature, salinity, dissolved oxygen, and chlorophyll ? variations—driven by both climate change and human activities—influence medusozoan distribution patterns around coastal infrastructures globally.
Review:
Basic reporting
There are big technical issue as seen in "4. Additional comments".

Experimental design
There are big technical issue as seen in "4. Additional comments".

Validity of the findings
There are big technical issue as seen in "4. Additional comments".

Additional comments
I have reviewed the manuscript by Tong Su et al. The authors conducted eDNA metabarcoding of jellyfish and compared seasonal and vertical variations in their distribution. While the study addresses an important ecological issue, there are significant methodological concerns that must be addressed before the manuscript can be considered for publication.

The most critical issue lies in the quantitative interpretation of NGS read counts. It is known that PCR amplification efficiency in eDNA samples can vary significantly between samples, making raw read counts unreliable indicators of species abundance. The authors’ use of read counts as a direct proxy for abundance is therefore inappropriate and misleading (see Ushio et al. 2018: https://doi.org/10.3897/mbmg.2.23297). All analyses based on read count comparisons should be removed, or alternatively, the authors should employ a more suitable approach such as quantitative metabarcoding.

Secondly, the manuscript lacks any mention of negative control results. Contamination is one of the most critical challenges in eDNA metabarcoding, and sequencing results from negative controls are essential for ensuring data reliability. It is important to include this information to ensure the reliability of the data.

Due to these methodological shortcomings, I cannot recommend the manuscript for publication in its current form. A substantial revision is required to address these issues and ensure the technical robustness of the study.

Article Title: A quick and reliable menthol-induced bleaching protocol for the Caribbean staghorn coral, Acropora cervicornis
Abstract: Corals and dinoflagellate algae form a unique mutualistic symbiosis that provides the energetic and structural foundation for shallow coral reef ecosystems. Despite the long success of this partnership in oligotrophic seas, coral reefs are in decline due to increasing threats from rising seawater temperatures and disease, both of which can lead to bleaching and mortality. In order to better understand the mechanisms that underpin this mutualism, it may be necessary to dismantle the coral-algal symbiosis. Previous studies have experimentally bleached corals using thermal stress, photosynthetic inhibitors (DCMU), and menthol. We compared lab-induced bleaching of staghorn coral Acropora cervicornis by menthol treatment to traditional thermal stress. The larger aim was to adapt existing bleaching protocols for this important coral species, providing a guide for future studies. Bleaching in corals treated with menthol or exposed to elevated temperature stress (31°C) was monitored by measuring photosynthetic activity determined by Fv/Fm using pulse-amplitude modulated (PAM) fluorescence and compared to untreated conspecifics. Corals were also monitored for symbiont density and overall health using the CoralWatch Coral Health Chart card throughout the experiment. We found that A. cervicornis bleached in response to both menthol treatment and thermal stress, but menthol treatment was more effective at reducing algal symbiont photosynthetic capacity (Fv/Fm) without negatively affecting the health of the coral. Our results indicate that menthol treatment at 0.38 mM rendered staghorn coral aposymbiotic within fourteen days without any visual or physiological damage to the coral. This study provides a simple and effective menthol-bleaching treatment protocol for future studies on staghorn coral.
Review:
Basic reporting
The authors optimized menthol treatment conditions to induce bleaching in the stony coral Acropora cervicornis (Line 74–88). However, the rationale for focusing specifically on this species is not sufficiently explained. It would strengthen the Introduction to briefly describe the ecological and scientific importance of A. cervicornis and why establishing a bleaching-induction protocol for this species is necessary (e.g., its conservation status, relevance as a model species, or significance in reef restoration). Additionally, clarifying how such a protocol will facilitate future research would help better justify the study’s motivation.

Experimental design
Experimental design is appropriate, using multiple menthol concentrations (0.19–0.58 mM) and thermal bleaching as control. One concern is that all experiments in this study were conducted using only a single genotype of A. cervicornis (Genotype ML-31), derived from a single colony (Line 93). Based on our experience, traits such as the density of symbiotic algae, sensitivity to menthol, and susceptibility to menthol-induced stress vary considerably among genotypes. Therefore, the authors should clarify why Genotype ML-31 was exclusively used. Is ML-31 an established experimental strain with known genomic information, similar to laboratory strains used in Aiptasia or Hydra research? If there is a specific reason for selecting this genotype, it should be explicitly stated.

Validity of the findings
It is unclear whether the structure observed by the authors truly represents mesenterial filament extension (Line 169–170). The resolution of the current image is low, making it difficult to confirm the identity of the structure. The authors should either replace the image with a higher-resolution photograph or provide a more detailed description to clearly demonstrate that the observed extension corresponds to mesenterial filaments. Besides, quantitative symbiont counts or chlorophyll data would substantiate the CoralWatch-based assessments.

Additional comments
no comment

Article Title: A different world: temporal changes in the community structure of sea slugs (Heterobranchia) in northwest Japan spanning more than a half-century
Abstract: Understanding long-term changes in marine biodiversity is essential for evaluating effects of climate change on coastal ecosystems. In this study, we compared heterobranch sea slug (hereafter referred to simply as ‘sea slugs’) assemblages in northwestern Kyushu, Japan, across three survey periods: (1) historical records from 1960–1980, for which the exact survey effort and duration were not documented; (2) surveys conducted from April 2001 to September 2003; and (3) recent surveys conducted from June 2023 to January 2024. A total of 47 sea slug species were recorded from our underwater surveys conducted in 2023–2024. Species diversity indices, including the Shannon–Wiener diversity index (H) and Simpson’s diversity index (D), showed higher values during this survey than those of 2001–2003. Comparative analysis of species composition revealed significant shifts, with 15 species exhibiting statistically significant differences in relative abundance from the past to the present. Notably, several species common in the past, such as Aplysia kurodai, were rarely observed in the recent survey, while many tropical–subtropical species appeared for the first time. The proportion of tropical–subtropical species increased markedly, whereas subarctic species were no longer detected. Jaccard’s coefficient indicated that the current community differs markedly from those in earlier periods. These findings suggest a major community reorganization, potentially driven by rising seawater temperatures and other environmental changes. This study highlights the importance of long-term monitoring using multiple indicators to detect and interpret climate-driven biodiversity shifts in coastal marine ecosystems.
Review:
Basic reporting
This manuscript compares the population structure of sea slugs in two locations on the western coast of Japan based on surveys conducted by the authors and other historical data dating back to 1960. This comparison has allowed them to observe a clear difference in the faunal composition of the species, with a decline in northern species and the arrival of tropical and subtropical species.
The authors provide environmental data that allow them to coherently link these population changes in sea slugs to global climate change.
The manuscript is well-structured in its various sections and is accompanied by relevant figures and tables, along with supplementary data from the different species observations.
The English text is clear and concise.
The references are sufficient for the development of the work, but some additional references to similar studies in other coastal areas are missing; these are indicated in the reviewed PDF.

Experimental design
The experimental design is appropiate, and the methodology employed is standard as used in other ecological studies.
Comparing the current data with historical data on sea slug populations is very interesting and novel for verifying the changes that have occurred in these populations and can serve as a basis for conducting similar studies in other marine areas in the near future.
The correlation between these population changes and the phenomenon of climate change is accurate.

Validity of the findings
The conclusions are correct and consistent with the data provided in the results section.

Additional comments
The PDF reviewed by this reviewer contains numerous comments that the authors should address to improve the manuscript's quality. In particular, they should modify the multiple references to Heterobranchia, which should always be capitalized as it is the name of an accepted taxon. The PDF also points out that not all Heterobranchia are sea slugs, as this higher taxon also includes, for example, the snails and terrestrial slugs formerly known as Pulmonata. Therefore, throughout the text, they should refer to heterobranch sea slugs.

For the taxonomy, the authors have followed the proposals of the recent work by Korshunova et al. from 2025, which considers only Cladobranchia to be true Nudibranchia, while dorid sea slugs form a separate order, Dordida. At the end of the Introduction section, the authors should indicate that they have followed the taxonomic norms proposed by Korshunova et al and cite the full reference in the reference list.

Article Title: The genetic structure of a Brachypodium hybridum population in a patchy arid landscape is independent of neighboring perennials and stable over two consecutive years
Abstract: In arid landscapes perennials can provide protection for annual plants against environmental stress such as drought. This could lead to population sub-structuring of the annuals, because of position dependent reproductive success, and/or competition for association with perennial shrubs. Although such interactions are well known drivers of plant community dynamics, little is known about the local and temporal effects of perennial presence on the genetic structure of annuals. As a first step to address this, we used a set of microsatellite markers to assess the genetic structure of the annual grass Brachypodium hybridum (L.) P. Beauv. growing underneath and outside the canopy cover of perennials at an arid site in southern Spain over two consecutive years (2018 and 2019). Upon sampling, we observed clear but inconsistent seasonal differences in phenology between individuals underneath or outside canopies. However, we did not find genetic differentiation between sampling location or sampling year (four populations: overall FST = 0.024). An analysis of the overall genetic structure inferred three putative clusters, but these clusters were neither associated with location nor with year of sampling. We conclude that the genetic structure of this B. hybridum population is independent of neighboring perennials and stable over two consecutive years and is not associated with phenological differences. The data further show a high level of homozygosity in the population, and the recurrent presence of identical genotypes, both with respect to location and year, indicating a major role of selfing in B. hybridum reproduction. However, the samples collected outside the canopies in 2019 show a slightly higher value of estimated heterozygosity. This may indicate the effect of immigration from other B. hybridum populations. These results show that neighboring perennial plants do not affect the stable genetic structure of the B. hybridum population within this arid site, but that other factors such as immigration, could eventually result in local and temporal differences. These results also indicate a possible patchy distribution of identical genotypes of the annuals, which should be considered when sampling such populations.
Review:
Basic reporting
In this manuscript, the authors studied genetic variation in annual grass Brachypodium hybridum concerning their position in neighboring perennials. In my opinion, the study suffers from conceptual as well as methodological flaws, namely (1) insufficient justification of the study, (2) unclear identification of the studied species, (3) inappropriate methodology (choice of microsatellite markers covering only one third of chromosomes). Specific comments on the manuscript are below.

Experimental design
l. 54-55. ‘The canopy cover of perennial plants may provide protection for annual plants against prevailing stressful environments. This positive effect...’ Although the authors talk about the positive effect of such a relationship, I imagine that there may be a negative effect as well (e.g. competition for water, reduced solar radiation, etc.). There are probably many factors (biotic as well as abiotic) that can shape such coexistence, so I wonder which particular factor the authors have in mind. In this case, it is not clear what the study sites look like and whether the microhabitats studied have the same conditions. What is the basis for the assumption that plants under perennial vegetation will be genetically different from those growing outside the vegetation? In this regard, there is no meaningful hypothesis tested in the study.
l. 94-99. It would be advisable to mention which plant communities in terms of phytocoenology occur at the study sites.
l. 101-102. I assume in/out means collecting samples inside and outside the stand. Why wasn't a full design applied that included in and out sampling in every period?
l. 102-103. I am missing information on the number of samples taken, and the number of study sites (patches).
l. 123. ‘The amplification reaction to identify the species was performed in Taq PCR’...what locus was used for this purpose?
l. 130-139. Do I understand correctly that the plants were identified only by PCR? This should be clarified. I also wonder if in Brachypodium there is for example backcrossing with parents, or if all plants are sterile F1 generations. Any hybridization could complicate the interpretation of genetic variation. I would also be interested to know if other Brachypodium species were present at the study sites and, if so, how each species was identified to avoid confusion. Last but not least, I would question the selection of microsatellite markers that were derived from B. distachyon species. This would mean that they represented only part of the genome (10 chromosomes out of 30) of the species studied, which is quite problematic.
l. 139. ‘Specific forward primer sequences all started with the universal M13 sequence ...’ I don’t understand this idea. The markers didn't have their own forward primers?
l. 157. 305 + 385 = 690, not 685
l. 157-158. If only 173 samples out of 690 were B. hybridum, what was the rest? Have you tried different methods of DNA isolation?
l. 166-167. ...‘we amplified loci from both the B. stacei and B. distachyon subgenomes.’ Do you have any evidence for this statement?
l. 179-180. Are the plants of B. hybridum fertile? What is the species’ reproduction mode? A short overview of this aspect, given in the introduction, would be welcome.

Validity of the findings
Since I see major flaws in the methodology of the work, I do not evaluate the data obtained. Nevertheless, the data obtained, especially the absolute predominance of homozygous genotypes, is not surprising to me. The question is how meiosis and the associated sexual reproduction work in a species with an unequal number of chromosomes within each subgenome. I would expect complications in meiosis, so I think clonal reproduction is more likely.

Additional comments
Manuscript title: to be correct, the title should be corrected saying ...and stable over TWO consecutive years.

Check the style of references (e.g., Nei 1973); Marques 2017 is missing from the refs list.

Article Title: Ontogenetic shifts in sound production and shared sonic mechanisms in two priacanthid fishes
Abstract: Sound production in teleost fishes relies on diverse anatomical adaptations, yet convergent mechanisms involving extrinsic sonic muscles acting on the swim bladder are widespread. This study investigates the acoustic and morphological features of two priacanthid species, Indo-Pacific glasseye Heteropriacanthus carolinus and moontail bullseye Priacanthus hamrur to explore interspecific similarities in sound production. Using recordings and anatomical analyses, we show that both species rely on a forced-response mechanism, where the contraction rate of fast sonic muscles determines the fundamental frequency. This is corroborated by the smaller diameter of sonic fibres compared to epaxial fibres in both species. Despite belonging to different genera, both species exhibit extrinsic sonic muscles originating from the first pleural rib and inserting on the anterior swim bladder. However, P. hamrur displays anterior bladder projections potentially involved in enhanced hearing, absent in H. carolinus. Acoustic signals were broadly similar between species, suggesting that the morphological shift in muscle insertion does not affect sound structure. Comparative analysis across geographically distant populations of H. carolinus (Indian and Pacific Oceans) revealed variation in acoustic features that was size-dependent rather than region-specific. Juveniles emitted continuous pulse trains with high fundamental frequencies, whereas adults produced more segmented calls with lower frequencies. These ontogenetic differences reflect developmental modulation of vocal output, not anatomical changes. Overall, our findings highlight the conserved nature of sonic mechanisms in Priacanthidae, the influence of body size on acoustic variation, and the potential role of swim bladder morphology in auditory enhancement rather than sound generation.
Review:
Basic reporting
The article is extremely well written. I have little to comment except for those below.

1. The introduction is well written but the overview of extrinsic swimbladder muscles could be expanded to include recent work that has identified sonic muscles in cypriniform fishes (e.g., Danionella). The sonic muscles of Danionella appear to be some of the most powerful identified to date. The same could be said for the absence of Ophidiidae from the introduction. My recommendation here is to add some more examples of extrinsic sonic muscles and mechanisms to the introduction and by adding these the authors would further strengthen the argument that these muscles are widespread across teleosts.

2. I recommend to manipulate the histology figure (figure 6) so that the background appears white. The colour of the muscle is not important here. Only the size. This should not impact the quality or meaning of the figure.

3. If possible it should be indicated where the section shown in Figure 6A is derived (perhaps this could be shown as a dotted line in figure 4B or C).

Otherwise, the study is excellent and the paper extremely well written. The figures are excellent

Experimental design
no comment

Validity of the findings
no comment

Additional comments

Article Title: Impact of integrating zeolite and PGPR on restoring soil health and enhancing crop yields following the leaching process
Abstract: Soil leaching is a traditional approach for reclaiming saline soil; however, it often leads to nutrient loss that further weakens soil health. Therefore, this study examines the use of zeolite in combination with plant growth-promoting rhizobacteria (PGPR) as a sustainable approach to restore soil health and enhance rice and wheat yields following soil leaching. The study farm affected by salinity and sodicity is located near Kafr Basat, Dakahlia Governorate, Egypt, during the period from 2023 to 2024. A 10-week sequential leaching process (L1–L5) reduced electrical conductivity (EC) by approximately 45% and exchangeable sodium percentage (ESP) by 42% across the soil profile. However, leaching resulted in declines in soil nutrient contents and microbial activity. Four treatments: control, zeolite (Z), PGPR, and combined zeolite + PGPR (Z+PGPR), were applied using a randomized block design with three replicates. The Z+PGPR treatment significantly improved soil chemical and biological properties, including increases in cation exchange capacity (CEC) (up to 37.21 cmol kg?1), nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium availability, as well as microbial biomass carbon (MBC), which increased from 4.04 to 4.37 mg/g soil. CO2 respiration increased by 62.3%, indicating enhanced microbial activity. Additionally, EC and ESP were further reduced, and infiltration rate improved to 1.09 cm h?1 under the Z+PGPR treatment. Correspondingly, rice and wheat yields increased to 11.78 t/ha and 7.44 t/ha, respectively. These results suggest that the combined application of zeolite with PGPR can enhance soil health and crop productivity following soil leaching. Nevertheless, long-term studies, multiple sites and economic assessments are needed to confirm the broader applicability and sustainability of this approach.
Review:

Some references regarding the zeolites characteristics are necessary.
Some additional figures would enhance the manuscript quality.

Experimental design
The experimental design is well described.
Analytical methods used to analyse the physico-chemical characteristics should be described.

Validity of the findings
The results are well described and discussed.

Additional comments
The manuscript Impact of integrating zeolite and PGPR on restoring soil health and enhancing crop yields following the leaching process presents the results obtained by testing the zeolite in conjunction with plant growth-promoting rhizobacteria to restoring soil health and enhancing rice and wheat yields following leaching. The results are significant since soil salinity is a significant environmental constraint on global crop production. The manuscript is in general well written, but some improvements are still necessary:

2. Lines 79-80. Typically, zeolites have an overall negative charge that attract mainly cations. For anions like phosphate ion, a chemical modification can be necessary. Please add more characteristics of natural zeolites. You can see for references: https://doi.org/10.3390/ma16082965, https://doi.org/10.3390/agriculture12030321

**PeerJ Staff Note:** It is PeerJ policy that additional references suggested during the peer-review process should only be included if the authors are in agreement that they are relevant and useful.
