This is the first study examining the potential long-term effects of elevated maternal thyroid hormones within the natural range. Although we found no evidence of long-term effects, other traits may still be affected and remain to be studied.
Review:
Basic reporting
I enjoyed reading this manuscript. The writing was clear and easy to follow the rationale for the study. I found it interesting that the components regulating TH action are expressed early in development (day 1 in passerines and day 4 in chickens) well before the development of the thyroid gland.

The authors included the relevant literature to support the basis for their study. I perused the literature cited and used Google Scholar to find relevant papers and the author cited the key references.

I was able to access the raw data and scan the results.

The structure of the manuscript follows the standard format. The manuscript as a whole is a publishable unit.

Experimental design
1. In my opinion the manuscript falls within the Aims and Scope of PeerJ.

2. The core research question addresses whether maternal thyroid hormones (TH), which are regulators of embryo development and gene expression, have an effect on hatchling behavior and physiology. Moreover, the study investigates long-term effects of maternal TH on behavior, plasma thyroid hormone levels, and brain gene expression. The authors use the Japanese Quail (Coturnix japonica) as model system. The study fills a knowledge gap in two ways. First, previous studies have yielded conflicting results on the effects of TH on phenotypic traits. Second, there is scant data available on the long-term effects of elevated TH levels.

3. The authors describe a comprehensive set of experiments to evaluate the role of TH levels (T3 and T4) on behavioral traits revealed by past studies to be influenced by TH. This study is noteworthy, because plasma TH levels were raised within a normal range. Because TH were introduced by injection into the egg yolk, the authors also assessed whether TH levels remain elevated to the adult stage. The experimental design was appropriate for addressing the questions posed in the introduction. In addition, the authors noted that the methods used in the study had been approved by the University IACUC.

4. The methods section has sufficient detail to replicate the study. However, I would encourage the authors to provide the names of the functions used to analyze the data and not just the package name.

Validity of the findings
The authors provided the data as a link to a figshare page. The excel spreadsheet can be opened and consists of multiple worksheets with the data available for each aspect of the study: behavior tests (Immobility, Emergence and Open Arena), the plasma thyroid hormone assays, and qPCR results. There is also a worksheet that provides a description of the variables used in the analysis.

The rationale and motivation for the study was clearly articulated in the Introduction. The relevant literature was also cited in the introduction. The discussion and conclusion included a summary of the major findings and linked the result to the research questions posed in the introduction.

Additional comments
I liked the goals of the study. The manuscript was well written and the experimental design easy to follow. The results were somewhat surprising given that other hormones (e.g., Corticosterone) can have a major effect on the phenotype of the offspring. However, in terms of whether TH levels would remain elevated once the chicks reach adult size, I should think that there could be downregulation of T3 and T4 once the thyroid gland was functional in the chicks. Thus one prediction would be that TH levels would return to baseline levels. I think it would have been interesting to measure the baseline TH levels for the hens and include as a covariate in the analyses.

Line 60. Delete “the maternal hormones”

Line 67. Ruuskanen et al. 2010 should be Ruuskanen & Laaksonen 2010.

Lines 190 – 191. 30 chicks in 1m2? That seems to be too crowded.

Lines 322 – 325. I would suggest putting the diagnostic plots in a supplementary materials.

Line 334. I recommend adding “(ddf)” after “denominator degrees of freedom.”

Lines 341 – 356. I don’t think you mentioned whether there was a significant “dam”/”maternal” effect in the analyses. Also, presumably you have repeated measures on the same individuals. Why not present a graph the shows the shift in behaviors of chicks when they reach sexual maturity?

Many of the figures show points that appear to be outliers, e.g., Figure 1. I would present means and standard errors. Plus, I would run a test to determine if some of the points were outliers or extreme values.

Article Title: Pathogens on fire: a scoping review of smoke-borne pathogen ecology in the One Health framework
Abstract:
Background
Wildland fires are increasing in both frequency and severity in many areas globally. Smoke from wildland fires (wildfires and prescribed burns), as well as agricultural burning, releases not only pollutants but also viable microorganisms, including pathogens capable of long-distance dispersal, potentially posing unrecognized risks to human, animal, and plant health.

Objectives
This scoping review synthesizes knowledge about pathogenic microbial dispersal in smoke from wildland fires, identifies gaps in pathogen ecology and epidemiology, and outlines research priorities in a One Health framework.

Methods
This review followed the Arksey & O’Malley framework with PRISMA-ScR guidance, using systematic searches in PubMed, Google Scholar, and grey literature sources (USDA Forest Service, World Health Organization, U.S. Environmental Protection Agency). After screening and applying inclusion criteria, 36 studies were retained that addressed microbial transport, viability, and disease associated with wildland fire smoke.

Results
There is evidence that wildland fire smoke can aerosolize diverse microbial assemblages, including pathogenic fungi such as Coccidioides and Puccinia, and bacteria capable of forming heat-resistant spores. If microbes can remain viable in smoke across greater distances, there would be the possibility of long-distance dispersal while suspended in smoke plumes. However, data about infection outcomes, dose–response relationships, and host susceptibility are lacking. Current wildland fire smoke surveillance focuses almost exclusively on abiotic pollutants, leaving microbial risks largely ignored.

Conclusions
A One Health approach integrates fire ecology, aerobiology, microbiology, and epidemiology across host species. After determining how important the role of dispersal in smoke is for human, animal, and plant health, priority actions may include improving pathogen viability sampling, incorporating microbial monitoring into smoke surveillance networks, and developing predictive models to assess health and ecological risks.
Review:
Basic reporting
Title & Abstract
Title: Although the Title is appropriate, it can be edited to enhance the significance of the study as follows: “A scoping review on a health perspective to understand the role of wildfire in the dispersal of smoke-borne pathogens.”
Abstract: In the current format, the non-structured Abstract is inadequate. Please consider re-writing it according to the following suggestions to enhance the relevance and the prime findings of the study:
• Justify the need for the study. Why are smoke-borne pathogens of prime importance to public health?
• Clarify the objectives of this scoping review.
• Indicate the database used for the literature search.
• Mention the time frame of the study and the usage of PRISMA.
• Specify the prime findings of the study. Also, the need for further research is stated without describing the current status of the field.
• Explain the role of one health integration.

Introduction
The Introduction section does not elaborate on the subject, i.e., the need for one health approach in wildfire microbial ecology is not described clearly. Consider incorporating the missing information based on the following suggestions:
• The flow of literature can be reorganized in the following order: Citations for an increase in wildfire, types of pathogens that are dispersed to a wider area due to smoke, microbial ecology of the smoke, and health impact.
• Cite and discuss a few case studies.
• In the current format, the potential risk of wildfire smoke-transmitted pathogens is not perceived. It is recommended that the authors include information from the literature to describe the role of wildfire ecology in public health. For example, Mulliken et al. provide evidence that wildfire smoke is responsible for increased hospitalization due to fungal infections.
Mulliken JS, Hampshire KN, Rappold AG, Fung M, Babik JM, Doernberg SB. Risk of systemic fungal infections after exposure to wildfires: a population-based, retrospective study in California. Lancet Planet Health. 2023;7(5):e381-e386. doi:10.1016/S2542-5196(23)00046-3
• Expand the description on how the integration of one health helps in understanding wildfire microbial ecology and plays a decisive role in framing public health policies.
• Add a paragraph on the need for a review of wildfire microbial ecology.
• Define the objectives of the review.
• Include a paragraph on how the methodology adopted by the authors improves the understanding of wildfire microbial ecology and its implications for public health.
Include the following suggested articles to support the description in the Introduction:
1. Kobziar LN, Thompson GR 3rd. Wildfire smoke, is a potential infectious agent. Science. 2020;370(6523):1408-1410. doi:10.1126/science.abe8116
2. Mulliken JS, Hampshire KN, Rappold AG, Fung M, Babik JM, Doernberg SB. Risk of systemic fungal infections after exposure to wildfires: a population-based, retrospective study in California. Lancet Planet Health. 2023;7(5):e381-e386. doi:10.1016/S2542-5196(23)00046-3

Figures & Tables
Although clear and adequate, the Figures are not self-explanatory. The legend can be expanded to provide a detailed explanation. In addition, include a table on the type of fire, year, pathogens associated, viability, or infectivity of the pathogens.

Experimental design
Material and Methods
The Materials and Methods section is adequate. However, some clarification is required based on the following suggestions to enhance the reproducibility and reliability of the data presented in the manuscript.
• Line 119: “We did not restrict the time interval for papers included in the study; most studies were within the last two decades.” – The word “most” is relative and can be interpreted differently by readers. Therefore, specify the duration of the study.
• The authors should describe how the review process was carried out for the inclusion and exclusion of the articles. Did they have independent reviewers? Was there any disagreement on the inclusion or exclusion of an article? If yes, how was it resolved? Describe the consensus reached regarding the discrepancy in the inclusion of the articles.
• Line 133: “We summarize the research based on pathogen type..” Please provide information on how the data were organized. Describe and present specific tables, if created, for data analysis.
• Line 135: “We used a narrative synthesis approach to categorize..” The authors need to provide information on how the narrative was derived. Did any statistical analysis support the narrative or were the conclusions endorsed by independent experts in the field?
• Line 139: “We did not track the funding sources from the papers included in this study because for this topic there is not a risk of conflict of interest.” – Studies on environmental pollution and its impact on human health can be biased significantly due to conflict of interest. Therefore, consider excluding the articles that specifically cite conflict of interest.

Validity of the findings
Results
The Results are organized appropriately; however, the section lacks quantitative data. Consider incorporating the following suggestions to fill in the missing information gaps:
• Line 133: The authors have mentioned, “We summarize the research based on pathogen type (fungi, bacteria, viruses)..” The current results are biased toward bacterial and fungal pathogens; what was the effect with respect to viral load?
• The data provided in this section are mostly qualitative in nature; therefore, it is difficult for the reader to understand the associated risk factors. Therefore, the authors could provide a table indicating the type of fire, year, pathogens associated, viability, or infectivity of the pathogens.
• Lien 142: “this review expands on the pyroaerobiology framework….” Please specify what was the expansion. The authors need to describe the limitations of those studies and describe how those limitations can be addressed in future work.
• Line 175: “It is possible that smoke-borne pathogens can be transported long distances with smoke to impact host health.” – The Conclusion is speculative in nature. Please discuss the findings based on wind speed and terrain. Also, cite appropriate literature.
• Provide a table or quantitative information that indicates an increase in pathogen spread due to wildfire and that is causative to public health concerns.

Discussion
The section is well-written and organized appropriately. However, it can be revised substantially to enhance the impact of the study.
Suggestions:
• The implications of one health only appear in the Title and Conclusion, but the need for one health integration does not appear anywhere in the Discussion. Please discuss the need for critical analysis and evidence integration across disciplines to support the role of one health approach.
• Discuss the challenges associated with air sampling and assessing the viability of the pathogens.
• Discuss the gaps in the current methods of wildfire smoke surveillance where epidemiological screening of pathogens is ignored.
• The authors have raised several questions, but the related suggestions are only speculative. Hence, the authors need to support these speculations with adequate citations from the literature.
• Add a paragraph on the limitations of the study.

Conclusion
The Conclusion is descriptive and does not provide information on the practical implications of the study.
In the current format, the Conclusion lists a series of questions repetitive from the Results and Discussion sections. The authors need to specify whether they have identified any novel questions and also specify the most actionable questions that can be prioritized.

Additional comments
The manuscript requires substantive language editing.

Article Title: HFSA: hybrid feature selection approach to improve medical diagnostic system
Abstract: Thanks to the presence of artificial intelligence methods, the diagnosis of patients can be done quickly and accurately. This article introduces a new diagnostic system (DS) that includes three main layers called the rejection layer (RL), selection layer (SL), and diagnostic layer (DL) to accurately diagnose cases suffering from various diseases. In RL, outliers can be removed using the genetic algorithm (GA). At the same time, the best features can be selected by using a new feature selection method called the hybrid feature selection approach (HFSA) in SL. In the next step, the filtered data is passed to the naive Bayes (NB) classifier in DL to give accurate diagnoses. In this work, the main contribution is represented in introducing HFSA as a new selection approach that is composed of two main stages; fast stage (FS) and accurate stage (AS). In FS, chi-square, as a filtering methodology, is applied to quickly select the best features while Hybrid Optimization Algorithm (HOA), as a wrapper methodology, is applied in AS to accurately select features. It is concluded that HFSA is better than other selection methods based on experimental results because HFSA can enable three different classifiers called NB, K-nearest neighbors (KNN), and artificial neural network (ANN) to provide the maximum accuracy, precision, and recall values and the minimum error value. Additionally, experimental results proved that DS, including GA as an outlier rejection method, HFSA as feature selection, and NB as diagnostic mode, outperformed other diagnosis models.
Review:
Basic Reporting
Strengths:
The paper follows a standard IMRaD (Introduction, Methods, Results, and Discussion) structure, making it easy to follow.
The abstract and introduction clearly state the problem, the proposed solution, and the key contributions.
The "Related Work" section (Section 2) is thorough, providing a well-summarized table (Table 1) comparing recent feature selection techniques, which is very useful for readers.
The use of figures (e.g., Fig. 2 for the DS architecture, Fig. 3 for HFSA steps) aids in understanding the proposed methodology.
The experimental results are presented with multiple graphs and metrics, providing a multi-faceted view of performance.

Weaknesses:
Clarity and Language: The manuscript would benefit from professional proofreading to correct grammatical errors and improve sentence fluency (e.g., "Diagnosis several diseases take more attention", "It is concluded that HFSA is better...").
Algorithm 1 Description: The pseudocode for the core HFSA algorithm is confusing and contains errors. For instance, Step 21 references an undefined variable h_l(t), and the loop structures (e.g., Steps 6-14) are unclear. The relationship between the GA and T^2A populations is not intuitively described in the algorithm steps.
Repetition: There is some redundancy, particularly in the introduction and the description of the three layers, where the same points are repeated multiple times.
Experimental Design
Strengths:
Comprehensive Comparison: The authors compare their HFSA against a wide range of recent and relevant feature selection methods (FSGA, IBGWO, EST, HSM, etc.) using three different classifiers (NB, KNN, ANN). This strengthens the claim of HFSA's robustness.Holistic System Evaluation: The proposed full Diagnostic System (DS) is compared against other diagnostic models (SNB, HDM, MLM, EDM), which is crucial for demonstrating practical utility.
Use of Standard Metrics: The use of accuracy, error, precision, recall, ROC-AUC, and implementation time provides a well-rounded evaluation.Cross-Validation: The use of 10-fold cross-validation is a good practice to ensure results are not due to a fortunate data split.

Weaknesses:Dataset Description: The description of the dataset is a major weakness. The paper states it contains "4,920 cases" and "132 symptoms (features)" for "41 distinct illness categories." However, no source, name, or public repository is provided. The claim that it is "collected from patients with various illnesses" is vague. Without knowing the source, balance of classes, or specific diseases, it is impossible to assess the dataset's quality, representativeness, or potential biases. This severely impacts the reproducibility and generalizability of the findings.
Parameter Justification: While Table 2 lists the parameter values for GA, T^2A, KNN, and ANN, the paper lacks a clear justification for how these specific values were chosen. For example, why is P_cross = 0.9 and P_mut = 0.01 optimal? Was a parameter sensitivity analysis conducted? This is a critical omission for a paper heavily reliant on optimization algorithms.
Baseline Clarification: The description of the baseline models (e.g., SNB, HDM, MLM, EDM) is brief. A more detailed explanation of what these models entail would help the reader understand what the DS is being compared against.
Statistical Significance: The results show performance differences, but no statistical significance tests (e.g., t-tests, ANOVA) are reported to confirm that the improvements of HFSA and DS are statistically significant and not due to random chance.

Article Title: Numerical dispersed flow simulation of fire-flake particle dynamics and its learning representation
Abstract: In this article, we propose methods for simulating the detailed flow of dispersed fire-flake particles in response to the movement of a flame, using chaotic advection and various buoyant flow techniques. Furthermore, we utilize these techniques to gather a synthetic dataset of detailed fire-flake particles and extend the solver to represent the movement of fire-flake particles based on learning-based approaches. Fire-flake particles not only exhibit unique and complex movements on their own, but they are also significantly influenced by the movement of the flame and the surrounding airflow. Modeling the flow of fire-flake particles realistically is challenging due to their chaotic and constantly changing nature. Instead of explicitly modeling the complex fire-flake particles in the flame based on fluid mechanics, this article efficiently approximates the chaotic motion of fire-flake particles using two approaches: 1) chaotic advection to simulate the flow and 2) controlled buoyant flow, which varies based on the temperature and lifespan of the fire-flake particles. Additionally, we collect a fire-flake dataset through this simulation and extends the solver to learn the representation of fire-flake motion using neural networks. During the advection process of fire-flake particles, a new stochastic solver is used to calculate the subgrid interactions between them. In this article, not only we propose algorithms that can express these techniques through numerical simulation, but we also extend this solver using artificial intelligence techniques to enable learning representation. By using the proposed technique, it is possible to efficiently simulate fire-flake particles with various movements in chaotic regions, and it allows for more detailed representation of fire-flake particles compared to existing methods. Unlike the typical random walk approach that adds noise randomly to the movement, our method considers the size and direction of the flame. This allows us to express fire-flake particles stably in most scenes without the need for parameter adjustments.
Review:
Basic Reporting
The paper is well-structured and comprehensively written. The background is thorough, the methodology is described in detail with supporting equations and figures, and the results are presented with extensive visual comparisons. The language is generally clear, though some technical sections (e.g., the probabilistic solver description) could be slightly more streamlined for readability.
Experimental Design
The experimental design is strong for a computer graphics paper. The authors test their method across multiple challenging scenarios (rising flames, moving flames, obstacle interaction, thin flames) to demonstrate robustness. The side-by-side comparisons with a key previous method (Kim et al., 2017) are effective and essential. However, the evaluation is predominantly qualitative (visual). A more quantitative analysis, such as a user study to statistically validate the perceived realism, would significantly strengthen the claims of superior visual quality.
Validity of the Findings
The findings are valid within the paper's scope and claims. The visual results consistently show that the proposed method produces more chaotic, dispersed, and detailed fire-flake motion compared to the previous approach, which appears more coupled to the underlying flame. The learning-based results successfully replicate the behavior of the simulation, proving the concept's viability. The authors are also commendably transparent about the method's limitations, notably its lack of strict physical accuracy and its 2D nature.

Additional Comments
Strengths: The dual simulation-and-learning approach is a notable contribution. The method is practical and has been successfully integrated with an image-based framework, increasing its applicability.

Weaknesses: The primary weakness is the heavy reliance on visual, qualitative assessment. The choice of some parameters and network architectures appears heuristic, lacking a rigorous ablation study to justify them.

Impact: The work provides a valuable tool for the computer graphics and visual effects industry, offering a new way to generate complex secondary effects more efficiently and with greater artistic control

Article Title: FLASC: a flare-sensitive clustering algorithm
Abstract: Exploratory data analysis workflows often use clustering algorithms to find groups of similar data points. The shape of these clusters can provide meaningful information about the data. For example, a Y-shaped cluster might represent an evolving process with two distinct outcomes. This article presents flare-sensitive clustering (FLASC), an algorithm that detects branches within clusters to identify such shape-based subgroups. FLASC builds upon HDBSCAN*—a state-of-the-art density-based clustering algorithm—and detects branches in a post-processing step using within-cluster connectivity. Two algorithm variants are presented, which trade computational cost for noise robustness. We show that both variants scale similarly to HDBSCAN* regarding computational cost and provide similar outputs across repeated runs. In addition, we demonstrate the benefit of branch detection on two real-world data sets. Our implementation is included in the hdbscan Python package and available as a standalone package at https://github.com/vda-lab/pyflasc.
Review:
Basic Reporting
The paper is well-structured and clearly written. The abstract and introduction effectively establish the motivation for detecting branching structures (flares) within clusters. The methodology is described in sufficient detail, with clear links to the foundational HDBSCAN* algorithm. The figures are relevant and support the textual explanations. The paper successfully communicates its main contribution: a computationally efficient post-processing step for HDBSCAN* that identifies flare-based subgroups.
Experimental Design
The experimental design is appropriate and comprehensive. The authors include two synthetic benchmarks to rigorously test the algorithm's core capabilities (branch detection and computational performance) and two real-world use cases to demonstrate practical utility. The comparison against a well-chosen set of algorithms from different categories (density-based, hierarchical, partitional, etc.) strengthens the validity of the claims. The use of the Adjusted Rand Index (ARI) for quantitative evaluation is standard and fitting. A minor weakness is the use of a single, star-shaped synthetic data pattern for the primary branch-detection benchmark, which could be expanded to include other topologies (e.g., trees with unequal branches).
Validity of the Findings
The findings are valid and support the authors' claims. The first benchmark convincingly shows that FLASC outperforms other clustering algorithms in detecting branches that lack local density maxima. The computational benchmark demonstrates that the added cost of flare detection is relatively low compared to the base HDBSCAN* clustering. The real-world use cases provide compelling evidence of FLASC's practical value in identifying meaningful, non-convex subgroups that standard clustering misses (diabetes data) and in extracting a more informative hierarchy of the data's shape (cell development data). The discussion thoughtfully addresses limitations, such as the sensitivity of the centroid-based eccentricity measure.
Additional Comments
This is a solid and valuable contribution to the clustering and exploratory data analysis toolbox. The integration with the popular hdbscan Python package will facilitate adoption. The discussion on alternative eccentricity metrics and potential visualizations provides excellent directions for future work. The paper would be strengthened by a brief mention of the algorithm's primary limitations in the conclusion, such as its performance on clusters with highly irregular, non-star-shaped flares. Overall, the algorithm delivers on its promise of being a flare-sensitive, practical, and efficient tool for data exploration.

Article Title: Dynamic prediction of carbon prices based on the multi-frequency combined model
Abstract: As a central participant and important leader in the global climate governance system, China is facing the urgent need to predict and regulate the price of carbon emissions to promote the sound development of its carbon market. In this article, a rolling prediction model based on Least Absolute Shrinkage and Selection Operator-cheetah optimization algorithm-extreme gradient boosting (Lasso-COA-XGBoost) carbon price decomposition integration is proposed to address the defects of low prediction accuracy and insufficient model stability of a single machine learning model in the carbon price prediction problem. During the modeling process, the adaptive Lasso method is first employed to select factors from 15 primary indicators of carbon prices, identifying the most important influencing factors. Next, the COA-XGBoost model is built and the parameters of the XGBoost model are optimized using the COA algorithm. Finally, the complete ensemble empirical Mode Decomposition with adaptive noise (CEEMDAM) method is utilized to decompose the residual sequence of the COA-XGBoost model and reconstruct it into high-frequency and low-frequency components. Appropriate frequency models are applied to achieve error correction, thereby constructing the combined Lasso-COA-XGBoost-CEEMDAN model. To further enhance the predictive accuracy and practicality of the model, a rolling time window is introduced for forecasting in the Hubei and Guangzhou carbon emission trading markets, ensuring that the forecasting model can adapt to market changes in real-time. The experimental results show that, taking the carbon price prediction in Hubei as an example, the proposed hybrid model has a significant improvement in prediction accuracy compared with the comparison model (XGBoost model): the RMSE is improved by 99.9987%, the MAE is improved by 99.9039%, the MAPE is improved by 99.9960%, and the R2 is improved by 0.2004%, and the advantages of this hybrid model are also verified in other experiments. The results provide an effective experimental method for future carbon price prediction.
Review:
Basic Reporting
The paper is clearly structured and well-written. The introduction effectively establishes the importance of carbon price forecasting. The methodology for the proposed Lasso-COA-XGBoost-CEEMDAN hybrid model is described in detail, with a helpful flowchart (Fig. 1). The presentation of results is comprehensive, using multiple tables and figures. However, the abstract's claim of a 99.9% improvement in RMSE is exceptionally high and requires careful scrutiny.

Experimental Design
The experimental design is robust. The authors compare their model against a strong set of benchmarks (GBDT, SVR, XGBoost, WOA-XGBoost) and ablated versions of their own model (with VMD). Testing on three distinct markets (Hubei, Guangzhou, EU) and a different commodity (oil) convincingly demonstrates generalizability. The use of a rolling window approach is a major strength, effectively preventing look-ahead bias. The inclusion of the Baidu index is a novel and valuable addition to the feature set.

Validity of the Findings
The reported performance metrics (MSE, MAE, MAPE, R˛) are extraordinarily low, suggesting near-perfect predictions. While the progressive improvement from benchmark to optimized (COA-XGBoost) to final hybrid model is logical, the magnitude of the gains, especially from the error correction step, is unprecedented and should be interpreted with caution. It raises questions about potential data leakage, overfitting on the test set, or the specific nature of the test period. The comparison with other recent hybrid models (Table 9) is favorable but requires ensuring the exact same train-test splits were used for a fair comparison.

Additional Comments
This is a technically sound and comprehensive study with a well-executed hybrid methodology. The primary concern is the almost too-good-to-be-true results, which should be tempered. The discussion of limitations is honest, particularly regarding model complexity and single-step forecasting. The provided policy recommendations are practical and directly derived from the feature importance analysis. Future work should focus on multi-step forecasting and model simplification for practical deployment. Overall, the proposed framework is a significant, if complex, contribution to the field.

Article Title: A genetic programming-based ensemble method for long-term electricity demand forecasting
Abstract: This study introduces a novel genetic programming-based ensemble method for forecasting long-term electricity consumption in Ethiopia. The technique utilizes a two-stage ensemble approach to project Ethiopia’s electricity consumption through 2031. In the initial stage, genetic algorithms, particle swarm optimization, and simulated annealing methods are applied to various regression models (linear, quadratic, and exponential). The preliminary forecast values generated in this stage were further refined in the second stage. Here, the genetic programming method was utilized to develop a formula based on the initial forecast values, which then provided the final forecast results. The most accurate predictions in the first stage were obtained using the GA_Quadratic, PSO_Quadratic, and SA_Quadratic methods, resulting in mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) values of 3.61, 3.63, and 4.68, respectively. In the second stage, the GP-based prediction achieved an even lower MAPE value of 2.83. Other error metrics, including MSE, root mean square error (RMSE), and R2, were also evaluated, with the proposed model outperforming all methods from the first stage on these metrics. The study projected Ethiopia’s total annual electricity consumption through 2031 under two different scenarios. Both scenarios indicate that by 2031, electricity consumption will have tripled compared to 2021 levels.
Review:
Basic Reporting
The paper is well-structured and clearly written. The abstract effectively summarizes the work, and the introduction provides a strong rationale for the study, contextualizing it within Ethiopia's specific energy challenges. The methodology is described in sufficient detail, and the results are presented clearly with supporting tables and figures. The use of AI for language polishing is appropriately acknowledged.

Experimental Design
The two-stage ensemble design is novel and well-conceived. Using multiple optimization algorithms (GA, PSO, SA) to fit different regression models in Stage 1 and then feeding the best performers into a GP for final integration in Stage 2 is a robust approach. The use of two distinct future scenarios for forecasting adds practical value. However, the extremely small dataset (n=16) is a significant limitation, which the authors acknowledge but cannot fully mitigate, potentially affecting model generalizability.

Validity of the Findings
The findings are valid and supported by the data and analysis presented. The proposed GP ensemble method demonstrably outperforms all individual Stage 1 models and conventional curve-fitting methods across all error metrics (MAPE reduced to 2.83). The use of statistical tests (Wilcoxon) and sensitivity analysis (Monte Carlo) strengthens the validity of the comparisons and model insights. The projection of a tripling in electricity demand by 2031 is a critical and well-supported finding for energy planners.

Additional Comments
The study makes a valuable contribution by introducing a novel ensemble method and providing crucial long-term forecasts for Ethiopia. The main weakness is the limited dataset, which is a common challenge in such national-scale, long-term studies. Future work should prioritize expanding the dataset, either with more historical data or through sophisticated synthetic data generation. It would also be beneficial to see a brief discussion on the interpretability of the final GP-derived formula, as this is often a trade-off with complex ensemble models. Overall, this is a solid and impactful piece of research.

Article Title: Feature selection using a multi-strategy improved parrot optimization algorithm in software defect prediction
Abstract: Software defect detection is a critical research topic in the field of software engineering, aiming to identify potential defects during the development process to improve software quality and reduce maintenance costs. This study proposes a novel feature selection and defect prediction classification algorithm based on a multi-strategy enhanced Parrot Optimization (PO) algorithm. Firstly, to address the limitations of the original Parrot Optimization algorithm, such as strong dependence on the initial population, premature convergence, and insufficient global search capability, this article develops a multi-strategy enhanced Parrot Optimization algorithm (MEPO). Experiments conducted on eight benchmark test functions validate the superior performance of MEPO in terms of convergence speed and solution accuracy. Secondly, to mitigate the adverse impact of irrelevant features on model performance in traditional software defect prediction methods, this study introduces a binary multi-strategy enhanced Parrot Optimization algorithm (BMEPO) for optimizing feature selection. Comparative experiments demonstrate that BMEPO exhibits stronger competitiveness in feature selection quality and classification performance compared to advanced feature selection algorithms. Finally, to further enhance the classification performance of defect prediction, a heterogeneous data stacking ensemble learning algorithm (HEDSE) based on feature selection is proposed. Experimental evaluations on 16 open-source software defect datasets indicate that the proposed HEDSE outperforms existing methods, providing a novel and effective solution for software defect prediction. The proposed approaches in this study hold significant practical value, particularly in improving software quality, optimizing testing resource allocation, and reducing maintenance costs, offering broad potential for application in real-world software engineering scenarios.
Review:
Basic Reporting
The paper is exceptionally well-structured and clearly written. The abstract effectively summarizes the contributions, and the introduction provides a strong motivation. The methodology is described in meticulous detail, with comprehensive pseudocode, flowcharts, and parameter settings. The results are presented thoroughly with extensive tables and figures, making the experimental process easy to follow and replicate.

Experimental Design
The experimental design is rigorous and comprehensive. The study is logically structured in three main parts: 1) validating the improved MEPO optimizer on benchmark functions, 2) applying its binary version (BMEPO) for feature selection against other metaheuristics, and 3) proposing and evaluating a novel heterogeneous ensemble (HEDSE). The use of 16 real-world defect datasets, multiple classifiers, and state-of-the-art comparators provides a robust evaluation framework. The inclusion of statistical tests (Win/Draw/Loss, Cliff’s Delta) strengthens the claims.

Validity of the Findings
The findings are highly valid and strongly supported by the data. The proposed MEPO demonstrably outperforms the original PO on benchmark functions. The BMEPO feature selector consistently achieves higher AUC values than other algorithms across all three classifiers (DT, SVM, KNN). Most significantly, the final HEDSE model shows a clear and statistically substantial improvement over both homogeneous ensemble variants and contemporary state-of-the-art methods, validating the core innovation of the paper.

Additional Comments
This is a high-quality contribution with a novel and effective heterogeneous ensemble approach. The primary limitation, correctly acknowledged by the authors, is the computational cost of BMEPO, though this is a reasonable trade-off for its performance gains. The use of the Promise repository, while standard, slightly limits the demonstration of generalizability to other domains. Future work could explore the framework's application to different types of data, as suggested. Overall, this is a solid and impactful piece of research.

Article Title: A rapid method for methanol quantification in spirits using UV-visible spectroscopy and FTIR
Abstract: Although standards methods of food safety assessment are important, these methods are relatively expensive and require intensive work and time. In alcohol beverage industries, ultraviolet visible (UV-Vis) spectroscopy and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy are reliable techniques for quality assessment of alcohol, however, testing methods are often varying with calibration techniques and instrument specification. In this work, methanol content in ethanol was assessed in two approaches using UV-Vis with a developed calibration technique and FTIR spectroscopy with a factory default scan function at every 2 nanometer (nm). For UV-Vis method, potassium dichromate was used as the chromogenic reagent, tested with methanol concentration ranging from 0.12% to 1% (mV?1). For FTIR method, spectra data was collected every 2 nm interval and calibration curve was built by increasing methanol ratio from 0% to 40% (mV?1) at the expense of ethanol while keeping deionised (DO) water constant at 5% (mV?1) concentration. This helps gauge the change in methanol concentration relative to ethanol. Results of analysis using UV-Vis showed a strong negative correlation for methanol concentration and absorbance value at UV region from 900 to 1,100 cm?1 (r = 98.00, RMSE = 0.023) relative to increasing ethanol concentration. A strong peak was observed for methanol concentration at spectral region of 975 cm?1 which is related to the methanoic acid C-O bond. The FTIR spectra region at 900 to 1,050 cm?1 was used for observing methanol concentration with absorbance. A strong correlation was established from spectral region of 1,010 to 1,026 cm?1, enabling quantification of methanol (r = 0.99, RMSEC = 0.55). Methanol peak was observed at 1,020 cm?1 region of the spectrum. A set of experimental repetition was made with methanol concentration of 0.02% to 0.5% and 0.1% to 5% for UV-Vis and FTIR, respectively, to determine the limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ). The observation showed a 0.04% and 0.29% (mV?1) LOD for UV-Vis and FTIR method, respectively. The LOQ was 0.12% and 0.89% (mV?1) for UV-Vis and FTIR respectively. The integration of UV-Vis with potassium dichromate as chromogenic reagent and FTIR spectroscopy with comparatively 50% less data point still present a significant advancement in the test method for safety and quality control of alcohol beverage products. These techniques not only enhance the ability to detect harmful substances but also provide a cost-effective and rapid alternative to traditional methods, making them invaluable tools for distilleries aiming to uphold high standards of quality.
Review:
Basic Reporting
The paper is generally well-structured, but contains significant errors in spectroscopic terminology that undermine its clarity. Most notably, UV-Vis spectral ranges are incorrectly reported in wavenumbers (cm?ą) instead of nanometers (nm), and there is confusion between wavelength and wavenumber units throughout. The abstract and results sections are particularly affected, making the methodology difficult to interpret accurately.

Experimental Design
The experimental design is appropriate for developing rapid screening methods. The study effectively compares two accessible techniques (UV-Vis with potassium dichromate derivatization and FTIR-ATR) for methanol quantification. The use of PLS regression for calibration and a 70/30 validation split is sound. However, the FTIR method's high LOD (0.29%) relative to regulatory limits (?0.4% mass/volume for a 40% ABV spirit) is a critical limitation for direct compliance testing.

Validity of the Findings
The findings are valid within the context of the developed methods. Both techniques show strong calibration correlations (r > 0.99). The UV-Vis method demonstrates superior sensitivity (LOD 0.04%). However, the practical utility of the FTIR method is questionable for detecting methanol near the legal threshold, as its LOD is very close to the maximum permitted level in many jurisdictions, limiting its use for definitive safety compliance.

Additional Comments
This work provides a valuable proof-of-concept for low-cost screening in resource-limited settings. The core scientific oversight is the consistent misuse of spectroscopic units, which requires major correction. The FTIR's performance with 2 nm data spacing is a relevant finding. For future work, focusing on the more sensitive UV-Vis method and exploring its application to real-world spirit matrices beyond model solutions would strengthen the practical impact. The study meets its goal of presenting accessible alternatives to GC-MS.

Article Title: Authentication of Jarrah (Eucalyptus marginata) honey through its nectar signature and assessment of its typical physicochemical characteristics
Abstract: Jarrah (Eucalyptus marginata) is a dominant forest tree endemic to the southwest of Western Australia. Its honey is appreciated for its highly desirable taste, golden colour, slow crystallisation, and high levels of bioactivity, which have placed Jarrah in the premium product range. However, whilst customers are willing to pay a high price for this natural product, there is currently no standard method for its authentication. As honey is naturally sourced from flower nectar, a novel route of authentication is to identify the nectar signature within the honey. This study reports on a high-performance thin layer chromatography (HPTLC)-based authentication system which allows the tracing of six key marker compounds present in Jarrah flower nectar and Jarrah honey. Four of these markers have been confirmed to be epigallocatechin, lumichrome, taxifolin and o-anisic acid with two (Rf 0.22 and 0.41) still chemically unidentified. To assist with the characterisation of Jarrah honey, a range of physicochemical tests following Codex Alimentarius guidelines were carried out. A blend of authenticated Jarrah honey samples was used to define the properties of this honey type. The blend was found to have a pH of 4.95, an electric conductivity of 1.31 mS/cm and a moisture content of 16.8%. Its water-insoluble content was 0.04%, its free acidity 19 milli-equivalents acid/kg and its diastase content 13.2 (DN). It also contains fructose (42.5%), glucose (20.8%), maltose (1.9%) and sucrose (<0.5%). The HPTLC-based authentication system proposed in this study has been demonstrated to be a useful tool for identifying Jarrah honey and might also act as a template for the authentication of other honey types.
Review:
Basic reporting:
The paper is well-structured, clearly written, and follows a logical flow from introduction to conclusion. The abstract succinctly summarizes the objectives, methods, key findings, and implications. Methods are described in sufficient detail to allow reproducibility, and results are supported by appropriate tables and figures. The study effectively addresses the need for authenticating Jarrah honey using a novel HPTLC-based approach.

Experimental design:
The experimental design is robust, incorporating both nectar and honey samples to establish a reliable nectar-derived chemical signature. The use of a blended honey sample to define typical physicochemical parameters is appropriate. HPTLC methods are well-optimized, and validation through comparison with standards strengthens the findings. However, the study could benefit from inclusion of negative controls or non-Jarrah honey samples to further validate specificity.

Validity of the findings:
The findings are valid and well-supported by the data presented. The identification of four marker compounds and the consistent HPTLC profiles across authentic samples provide strong evidence for the proposed authentication method. Physicochemical data align with Codex standards and previous studies, reinforcing their reliability. The unidentified markers (Rf 0.22 and 0.41) are acknowledged, maintaining transparency.

Additional comments:
The study presents a practical and innovative method for honey authentication, with potential applicability to other monofloral honeys. The link between nectar and honey chemistry is convincingly demonstrated. Future work should focus on identifying the unknown markers and validating the method with a larger, more diverse sample set, including adulterated or mislabeled commercial samples.

Article Title: Baltic Sea shipwrecks as a source of hazardous pollution
Abstract: Shipwrecks on the Baltic Sea seabed pose a serious threat to the marine environment. Fuel, ammunition and chemicals in their holds can enter the ecosystem at any time, causing an ecological disaster. It is known that oil spills from ship accidents can affect life and health of different species of animals, both immediately after catastrophe and for many years thereafter. This article discusses the negative impact of shipwrecks on the ecological status of the Baltic Sea and presents the contamination status of bottom sediment core samples taken in the vicinity of shipwrecks located in the South Baltic, i.e., S/s Stuttgart, t/s Franken, S/T Burgmeister Petersen and m/s Sleipner. It is based on the results of research carried out by the Maritime Institute between 2011 and 2016.
Review:
Basic reporting:
The paper is clearly structured and well-written, with a logical flow from introduction to conclusions. The abstract accurately summarizes the study's focus, methods, and key findings. Data are effectively presented in tables and figures, and the methods section provides sufficient detail for reproducibility.

Experimental design:
The study design is appropriate for its objective, analyzing sediment cores from around four shipwrecks using standardized analytical methods (GC-MS, GC-FID). Quality control measures are adequately described. However, the lack of consistent sampling strategies across wrecks (e.g., varying numbers of sampling points, different years) and the absence of proper non-wreck reference sites slightly weaken the comparative analysis.

Validity of the findings:
The findings are valid and well-supported by the data. The significantly elevated contaminant levels around the S/s Stuttgart are convincingly demonstrated and contextualized with regional sediment quality guidelines and literature values. The conclusions are cautious and acknowledge limitations, such as the need for further sampling to confirm potential leaks from other wrecks.

Additional comments:
The paper successfully highlights the S/s Stuttgart as a major pollution point source. Its strength lies in its practical, monitoring-based approach. Future work should include systematic sampling across all wrecks, incorporate reference sites, and consider temporal monitoring to assess contaminant progression. The study provides valuable data for regional environmental management.

Article Title: Effect of acidity/alkalinity of deep eutectic solvents on the extraction profiles of phenolics and biomolecules in defatted rice bran extract
Abstract: This study investigated the influence of deep eutectic solvent (DES) acidity/alkalinity on the extraction profiles of phenolics and other biomolecules (phytic acid, reducing sugar, and protein) in defatted rice bran (DFRB). The DES with varying pH levels were prepared using different hydrogen bond acceptors (choline chloride (ChCl) and potassium carbonate (K2CO3)) and hydrogen bond donors (lactic acid, urea, and glycerol). The results reveal that the acidic DES (ChCl-lactic acid; pH 0.42) demonstrated superior extraction efficiency for total phenolic acids (4.33 mg/g), phytic acid (50.30 mg/g), and reducing sugar (57.05 mg/g) while having the lowest protein content (5.96 mg/g). The alkaline DES (K2CO3-glycerol; pH 11.21) showed the highest levels of total phenolic acid (5.49 mg/g) and protein content (12.81 mg/g), with lower quantities of phytic acid (1.04 mg/g) and reducing sugar (2.28 mg/g). The weakly acidic DES (ChCl-glycerol; pH 4.72) exhibited predominantly total phenolics (3.46 mg/g) with lower content of protein (6.22 mg/g), reducing sugar (1.68 mg/g) and phytic acid (0.20 mg/g). The weakly alkaline DES (ChCl-urea; pH 8.41) resulted in lower extraction yields for total phenolics (2.81 mg/g), protein (7.45 mg/g), phytic acid (0.10 mg/g), and reducing sugar (7.36 mg/g). The study also explored the distribution of phenolics among various DESs, with the alkaline DES (K2CO3-glycerol) containing the highest concentration of free phenolics. Notably, ChCl-based DESs predominantly contained soluble esterified bound phenolics and soluble glycosylated bound phenolics. Furthermore, a significant correlation between antioxidant activities and phenolic contents was observed. In conclusion, this study has revealed that the acidity and alkalinity of a DES significantly impact the extraction of phenolics and other value-added biomolecules in DFRB. These findings highlight the potential for manipulating the properties of DESs through pH variation, making them versatile solvents for extracting and isolating valuable compounds from agricultural by-products like DFRB and offering opportunities for sustainable utilization and value addition in various industries.
Review:
Basic reporting:
The paper is well-structured and clearly written, with a logical flow from introduction to conclusions. The abstract accurately summarizes the study's objectives, methods, and key findings. Data are effectively presented in tables and figures, and the methods section provides sufficient detail for reproducibility.

Experimental design:
The experimental design is appropriate and systematic, comparing four DES formulations with varying pH levels. The inclusion of different phenolic forms (free, esterified-bound, glycosylated-bound) and multiple biomolecules provides comprehensive profiling. Standardized analytical methods (HPLC, spectrophotometry) were properly employed. However, the study could be strengthened by including a conventional solvent as a control for comparison.

Validity of the findings:
The findings are valid and well-supported by the data. Clear trends between DES pH and extraction efficiency for different compound classes are demonstrated. Statistical analysis confirms significant differences between treatments. The correlation between phenolic content and antioxidant activity adds credibility to the results.

Additional comments:
The study successfully demonstrates pH as a key parameter for tuning DES selectivity. The practical implications for sustainable extraction are valuable. Future work should explore optimization of other extraction parameters and scale-up potential. The research contributes meaningfully to green extraction technology development.

Article Title: Comprehensive analysis of groundwater hydrochemistry and nitrate health risks in the Baiquan basin, Northern China
Abstract: Groundwater is a crucial water source and strategic resource, essential for sustaining both urban and rural livelihoods, supporting economic and social development, and maintaining ecological balance. This study investigates the hydrochemical properties and controlling factors of groundwater in the Baiquan basin (BQB) by analyzing water quality data collected during both dry and wet periods. Additionally, the suitability of groundwater for drinking and agricultural irrigation was evaluated. The findings reveal that groundwater in BQB is generally weakly alkaline and primarily consists of hard-fresh water. Although there are seasonal variations in the main ion concentrations, HCO3? and Ca2+ are the predominant anions and cations, respectively. Consequently, the hydrochemical type is mainly HCO3-Ca?Mg type, with a secondary classification of SO4?Cl-Ca ? Mg. The hydrochemical composition is primarily influenced by the dissolution of carbonate and silicate minerals, as well as cation exchange processes. Additionally, it is affected by anthropogenic inputs, particularly from the use of agricultural fertilizers. The water quality assessment results indicated that all water samples are classified as either good or moderate, with a significant majority falling into the good category. Additionally, the northern section of the BQB exhibited lower entropy weight water quality index (EWQI) values during the dry season in comparison to the wet season. For irrigated agriculture, groundwater in the BQB serves as a high-quality water source for irrigation throughout both the dry and rainy seasons. Furthermore, non-carcinogenic risks are notably concentrated in the north-western and south-eastern regions of the study area. Health risks associated with nitrates in groundwater are elevated during the rainy season. Notably, non-carcinogenic risks for infants were significantly high across both seasons and substantially exceeded those for children and adults. These results provide valuable scientific insights for the management and development of groundwater resources in the BQB.
Review:
Basic Reporting
The manuscript is well-structured, with clear sections including abstract, introduction, methods, results, discussion, and conclusions. The language is generally clear, though some figures and captions (e.g., Fig. 3, 4) could be better integrated with the text. The study objectives are clearly stated, and the conclusions align with the findings.

Experimental Design
The study design is robust, incorporating 52 sampling points across dry and wet seasons. Standard methods for sampling, analysis, and quality control (e.g., CBE < 5%) are appropriately applied. The use of EWQI, Piper diagrams, Gibbs plots, ion ratios, and health risk models is methodologically sound and well-documented.

Validity of the Findings
The findings are supported by comprehensive data and multiple analytical approaches. Results on hydrochemical types, rock weathering dominance, and anthropogenic nitrate inputs are consistent with regional geology and land use. Seasonal variations in EWQI and health risks are logically explained. However, the reliance on a single year’s data limits the assessment of long-term trends.

Additional Comments
The health risk assessment effectively highlights higher vulnerability of infants to nitrate exposure.

Spatial analysis using GIS adds value, but some maps (e.g., Fig. 12) are cluttered and could be simplified.

Recommendations for groundwater management are practical and policy-relevant.

Future work should include multi-year monitoring to capture temporal dynamics.

Article Title: Nutrient cycling characteristics along a chronosequence of forest primary succession in the Hailuogou Glacier retreat area, eastern Tibetan Plateau
Abstract: The Hailuogou Glacier has been continuously retreating since the end of the Little Ice Age, resulting in a 125-year soil chronosequence and a complete primary forest succession sequence. Nutrient cycling and utilization are the foundation to forest succession processes and dynamic changes, directly influencing the structure and stability of ecosystems. However, our understandings on the characteristics of ecosystem nutrient accumulation and recycling during succession, especially in the context of primary succession within glacier retreat areas, remain limited. To address this, we investigated nutrient characteristics across six forest primary succession sites in the Hailuogou Glacier retreat area.
Review:
Basic reporting:
The manuscript is clearly structured and well-written, with a logical flow from introduction to conclusions. The abstract effectively summarizes the study’s aims, methods, and key findings. Figures and tables are relevant and support the results, though some figure labels (e.g., Figs. 5–9) could be more descriptive. The language is generally clear, though minor grammatical inconsistencies are present.
Experimental design:
The study employs a well-established chronosequence approach across six sites representing 15–125 years of glacial retreat. Sampling of vegetation, litter, and soil across three quadrats per site is methodologically sound. The use of allometric equations and standardized nutrient cycling formulas (e.g., utilization coefficient, turnaround time) is appropriate and aligns with similar ecological studies.

Validity of the findings:
The findings are robust and supported by comprehensive data. The reported increases in nutrient pools (N, P, K, Ca, Mg) and shifts in distribution between soil and vegetation are consistent with successional theory. The distinction between broad-leaved and coniferous forest nutrient strategies is well-substantiated. Comparisons with vertical zonation and other coniferous forests add contextual validity.

Additional comments:
The study provides valuable insights into nutrient dynamics during primary succession in a glacier retreat area. However, the use of a space-for-time substitution (chronosequence) assumes environmental constancy, which may not fully hold. Inclusion of microbial or enzymatic data could strengthen the nutrient cycling mechanisms discussed. The conclusions are well-supported and relevant to predicting ecosystem responses under climate change.

Article Title: Optimization and action mechanism of pollutant removal performance of unsaturated vertical flow constructed wetland (UVFCW) driven by substained-release carbon source
Abstract: Constructed wetland (CW) technology has attracted much attention due to its economical and environmentally friendly features. The low dissolved oxygen (DO) and low carbon/nitrogen (C/N) ratio in the wetland influent water affect the treatment performance of CW, resulting in a decrease in the removal efficiency of ammonia nitrogen (NH4+-N) and nitrate nitrogen (NO3?-N). In order to address this problem, this study optimized the pollutants removal performance of unsaturated vertical flow constructed wetland (UVFCW) by adding sustained-release carbon sources (corn cobs + polybutylene adipate terephthalate (PBAT)). The results showed that the sustained-release of carbon source increased the carbon source in UVFCW, thus increasing the abundance and activity of denitrifying microorganisms and enhancing the denitrification reaction, ultimately improving the removal of NO3?-N, with its removal efficiency reaching up to 95.50%. The placement method of sustained-release carbon source mainly affected the distribution of carbon source and DO in water body, thus influencing the relative abundance of microorganisms, finally affecting the removal of pollutants. Among them, the removal efficiency of total nitrogen (TN), NO3?-N, and total phosphorus (TP), and the relative abundance of denitrifying microorganisms in the CWR-Cu (uniform placement of sustained-release carbon source) were significantly higher than those in the CWR-Ca (centralized placement above) and CWR-Cb (centralized placement below) (p < 0.05). The surface C:O (carbon:oxygen) ratio of sustained-release carbon source after water treatment showed a decreasing trend, and CWR-Cu exhibited the greatest decrease in C:O ratio. In summary, CWR-Cu achieved the highest utilization of the carbon source and produced the largest number of heterotrophic microorganisms. This study reveals that CWR-Cu is a structural process for the efficient removal of nitrogen and phosphorus pollutants, and our findings provide theoretical basis and technical support for actual projects.
Review:
Basic reporting:
The manuscript is clearly written and well-structured, with a logical flow from introduction to conclusions. The abstract effectively summarizes the study's aims and key findings. Figures and tables are relevant and support the results, though some acronyms (e.g., CWR-Cu) are overused and can make the text slightly cumbersome to read.

Experimental design:
The experimental design is robust, systematically comparing a control (CW_R) against three carbon source placement strategies (uniform, top-concentrated, bottom-concentrated). The use of a composite carbon source (corn cob + PBAT) is innovative. The integration of water quality monitoring, material characterization (SEM/EDS), and microbial community analysis (16S rRNA sequencing) provides a comprehensive, multi-faceted approach to investigate the treatment mechanisms.

Validity of the findings:
The findings are valid and well-supported by the data. The superior performance of the uniformly placed carbon source (CWR-Cu) is convincingly demonstrated through higher denitrification rates, greater microbial abundance of key denitrifiers, and material characterization showing the highest utilization of the carbon source. The conclusions logically connect the placement method to the distribution of carbon and DO, and subsequently to microbial activity and treatment efficacy.

Additional comments:
This is a strong applied study with clear engineering implications. A minor limitation is the single, synthetic wastewater recipe used, which may not fully represent the variability of real wastewaters. The conflict of interest, with several authors employed by the funding company, is transparently declared. The research successfully identifies an optimal configuration (uniform placement) and provides a mechanistic explanation for its success, offering valuable practical guidance.

Article Title: Time series (ARIMA) as a tool to predict the temperature-humidity index in the dairy region of the northern desert of Mexico
Abstract: The environment in which an animal is situated can have a profound impact on its health, welfare, and productivity. This phenomenon is particularly evident in the case of dairy cattle, then, in order to quantify the impact of ambient temperature (°C) and the relative humidity (%) on dairy cattle, the temperature-humidity index (THI) is employed as a metric. This indicator enables the practical estimation of the stress imposed on cattle by ambient temperature and humidity. A seasonal autoregressive integrated moving average (SARIMA) (4,1,0)(0,1,0)365 model was estimated using daily data from the maximum daily THI of 4 years (2016–2019) of the Comarca Lagunera, an arid region of central-northern Mexico. The resulting model indicated that the THI of any given day in the area can be estimated based on the THI values of the previous four days. Furthermore, the data demonstrate an annual increase in the number of days the THI indicates a risk of heat stress. It is essential to continue building predictive models to develop effective strategies to mitigate the adverse effects of heat stress in dairy cattle (and other species) in the region.
Review:
Basic reporting:
The paper is clearly structured and well-written, with a logical flow from introduction to conclusions. The methods are described in sufficient detail, and the results are supported by appropriate figures and statistical outputs. However, the manuscript contains minor formatting inconsistencies, such as incomplete equation numbering and occasional repetition in the methodology section.

Experimental design:
The study uses a robust time-series approach, applying SARIMA modeling to daily THI data from 2016–2019. The train-test split and use of established R packages (e.g., fable, forecast) are appropriate. However, the justification for selecting a 12-month test set is not fully explained, and the geographic distribution of data points, while representative, could benefit from more detailed spatial analysis.

Validity of the findings:
The SARIMA(4,1,0)(0,1,0)??? model demonstrates reasonable predictive performance (R˛ = 0.63), and residual diagnostics support model adequacy. The reported upward trend in THI aligns with existing literature on heat stress. However, the model’s explanatory power is moderate, and the absence of external validation using independent data limits the generalizability of the findings.

Additional comments:
The study provides a valuable application of SARIMA modeling for THI prediction in an understudied arid dairy region. Future work could integrate additional climatic variables or machine learning methods to improve accuracy. The practical implications for farm management are clearly stated, enhancing the paper’s relevance to both researchers and producers.

Article Title: Turbidivision: a machine vision application for estimating turbidity from underwater images
Abstract: The measurement of turbidity serves as a key indicator of water quality and purity, crucial for informing decisions related to industrial, ecological, and public health applications. As existing processes require both additional expenses and steps to be taken during data collection relative to photography, we seek to generate accurate estimations of turbidity from underwater images. Such a process could give new insight to historical image datasets and provide an alternative to measuring turbidity when lower accuracy is acceptable, such as in citizen science and education applications. We used a two-step approach to a machine vision model, creating an image classification model trained on image data and their corresponding turbidity values recorded from a turbidimeter that is then used to generate continuous values through multiple linear regression. To create a robust model, we collected data for model training from a combination of in situ field sites and lab mesocosms across suspended sediment and colorimetric profiles, with and without a Secchi disk for visual standard, and binned images into 11 classes 0–55 Formazin Nephelometric Units (FNU). Our resulting classification model is highly accurate with 100% of predictions within one class of the expected class, and 84% of predictions matching the expected class. Regression results provide a continuous value that is accurate to ±0.7 FNU of true values below 2.5 FNU and ±33% between 2.5 and 55 FNU; values that are less accurate than conventional turbidimeters but comparable to field-based test kits frequently used in classroom and citizen science applications. To make the model widely accessible, we have implemented it as a free and open-source user-friendly web, computer, and Google Play application that enables anyone with a modern device to make use of the tool, the model, or our repository of training images for data collection or future model development.
Review:
Basic reporting:
The paper is clearly written and well-structured, with a logical flow from introduction to conclusions. Methods are described in sufficient detail for reproducibility, and results are supported by appropriate figures and statistical outputs. All data and code are openly available, enhancing transparency.

Experimental design:
The study employs a robust two-step machine learning approach using YOLOv8 for classification followed by regression. Data collection from both field and lab environments across diverse conditions (sediment, color, flow) strengthens model generalizability. The 75/15/10 train/test/validation split is appropriate.

Validity of the findings:
Results demonstrate strong performance with 84% exact classification accuracy and 100% within one class. The regression model achieves high explanatory power (R˛=0.975). The stated accuracy (±0.7 FNU below 2.5 FNU, ±33% above) is appropriately compared to commercial test kits rather than research-grade instruments.

Additional comments:
The development of a freely available web/mobile app significantly enhances the work's impact for citizen science and education. The study appropriately acknowledges limitations and suggests valuable future directions, including direct regression approaches and above-water image applications. The dataset, while comprehensive, remains relatively small for deep learning standards.

Article Title: Towards sustainable coastal management: aerial imagery and deep learning for high-resolution Sargassum mapping
Abstract: The massive arrival of pelagic Sargassum on the coasts of several countries of the Atlantic Ocean began in 2011 and to date continues to generate social and environmental challenges for the region. Therefore, knowing the distribution and quantity of Sargassum in the ocean, coasts, and beaches is necessary to understand the phenomenon and develop protocols for its management, use, and final disposal. In this context, the present study proposes a methodology to calculate the area Sargassum occupies on beaches in square meters, based on the semantic segmentation of aerial images using the pix2pix architecture. For training and testing the algorithm, a unique dataset was built from scratch, consisting of 15,268 aerial images segmented into three classes. The images correspond to beaches in the cities of Mahahual and Puerto Morelos, located in Quintana Roo, Mexico. To analyze the results the f?-score metric was used. The results for the Sargassum class indicate that there is a balance between false positives and false negatives, with a slight bias towards false negatives, which means that the algorithm tends to underestimate the Sargassum pixels in the images. To know the confidence intervals within which the algorithm performs better, the results of the f0.5-score metric were resampled by bootstrapping considering all classes and considering only the Sargassum class. From the above, we found that the algorithm offers better performance when segmenting Sargassum images on the sand. From the results, maps showing the Sargassum coverage area along the beach were designed to complement the previous ones and provide insight into the field of study.
Review:
Basic reporting:
The paper is well-structured and clearly written, with a logical flow from introduction to conclusions. Methods are described in sufficient technical detail, and results are supported by comprehensive statistical analysis and visualizations. The dataset and code availability enhance reproducibility.

Experimental design:
The study employs a robust methodology using pix2pix (cGAN) for semantic segmentation of drone imagery. The creation of a novel 15,268-image dataset with three classes (Sargassum, sand, others) is a significant contribution. The 80/20 train/test split and use of appropriate evaluation metrics (F?-score) are methodologically sound.

Validity of the findings:
Results demonstrate the model's effectiveness with F0.5 scores of 0.8637 (all classes) and 0.6369 (Sargassum only). The bootstrap resampling provides reliable confidence intervals. The identified tendency toward underestimation rather than overestimation is appropriately discussed as acceptable for management applications. The produced high-resolution area maps represent a tangible output.

Additional comments:
The work makes valuable contributions through both the novel dataset and practical mapping methodology. The discussion honestly addresses limitations, including challenges with floating Sargassum and class imbalance. The application for beach management logistics is well-justified. Future work could benefit from addressing dataset imbalance and expanding to more diverse coastal environments.

Article Title: Comprehensive analysis of bioplastics: life cycle assessment, waste management, biodiversity impact, and sustainable mitigation strategies
Abstract: Coral resilience varies across species, with some exhibiting remarkable stability and adaptability, often mediated by their associated microbiomes. Given the species-specific nature of coral-microbiome interactions, investigating the microbiomes of urban-adapted corals provides critical insights into the health, dynamics, and functioning of coral holobionts. In this study, we examined the microbiome of Madracis auretenra, a Caribbean coral from Santa Marta, Colombia, across contrasting environmental conditions. Over two years, we compared the microbiomes of healthy and stressed coral colonies from two distinct reef habitats—urban and protected—using 16S rRNA gene sequencing (V4 region) to assess microbial diversity. Our findings revealed microbial richness and diversity were primarily influenced by seasonal and local factors rather than host-specific traits such as interaction with algae, health status, or microhabitat. These variations were not substantial enough to disrupt the overall microbial community structure, which remained stable across temporal and spatial scales. Dominant taxa included Endozoicomonas, along with Vibrionaceae and Rhodobacteraceae, which form dense ecological interaction networks. Notably, nutrient and oxygen levels emerged as key drivers of microbiome fluctuations, yet Vibrionaceae populations exhibited exceptional temporal stability. These findings highlight the presence of a well-structured and resilient coral microbiome with minimal seasonal variability, even in urban-influenced environments. We propose that the dominance of Endozoicomonas and the stability of Vibrionaceae populations play a pivotal role in maintaining microbiome balance, ultimately contributing to the ecological resilience of M. auretenra in dynamic reef habitats.
Review:
Basic Reporting
The paper is well-structured, with clear sections covering introduction, methodology, results, discussion, and conclusions. The language is generally clear, though some sections are overly descriptive and could benefit from more concise writing. Tables and figures are appropriately used to summarize comparative data. The abstract effectively outlines the scope and significance of the review.
Experimental Design
As a review article, the study does not involve original experiments. The literature search methodology is adequately described, including databases used and search terms. The inclusion of a consumer survey adds empirical insight, though its limited sample size (384 respondents) and geographic bias (Europe and Asia) may affect generalizability. The use of statistical analysis (Chi-square tests) is appropriate for the survey data.
Validity of the Findings
The findings are consistent with current literature on bioplastics. The life cycle assessment (LCA) comparisons are well-supported by cited studies, and trade-offs (e.g., land use vs. carbon footprint) are clearly acknowledged. The discussion on biodiversity impacts and waste management challenges is balanced and evidence-based. However, the review could better address contradictory findings or limitations in existing LCA studies.
Additional Comments
Strengths: Comprehensive coverage of bioplastics' life cycle; useful comparative tables; emphasis on sustainability and circular economy.

Weaknesses: Limited critical analysis of conflicting evidence; over-reliance on certain regions in survey data; minimal discussion of economic scalability.

Suggestions: Include more diverse geographic studies; address policy-implementation gaps; explore social and economic dimensions of bioplastic adoption more deeply.

Article Title: Endozoicomonas dominance and Vibrionaceae stability underpin resilience in urban coral Madracis auretenra
Abstract: Coral resilience varies across species, with some exhibiting remarkable stability and adaptability, often mediated by their associated microbiomes. Given the species-specific nature of coral-microbiome interactions, investigating the microbiomes of urban-adapted corals provides critical insights into the health, dynamics, and functioning of coral holobionts.
