Article Title: Enhancing text-to-structured query language translation for seamless electronic medical record access
Abstract: Traditional models for natural language-to-SQL translation in Electronic Medical Record (EMR) systems struggle with understanding medical terminology, handling complex queries, and bridging the syntax-semantics gap, leading to scalability and accuracy issues. Advanced solutions like Large Language Model (LLM) based approaches address these challenges by leveraging deep learning and domain-specific training to enhance performance and usability. Hence, this article introduces an advanced medical Text-to-Structured Query Language (SQL) paradigm that simplifies accessing EMRs by translating natural language queries into SQL commands. This model is built on the advanced Code-T5 (Text-to-Text Transfer Transformer) architecture, further enhanced with Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) and Quantized Low-Rank Adaptation (QLoRA) techniques; it effectively addresses the challenges posed by the complexity of traditional SQL queries enabling seamless access to critical healthcare data. The innovation of the proposed model lies in its exceptional performance across multiple evaluation metrics. It achieves a Bilingual Evaluation Understudy (BLEU) score of 81.68, significantly outperforming leading models like T5, Fine-Tuned Language Net (FLAN) T5, and Bidirectional and Auto-Regressive Transformers (BART) while excelling in Recall-Oriented Understudy for Gisting Evaluation (ROUGE) metrics, underscoring its proficiency in generating semantically accurate and coherent SQL queries. Furthermore, the proposed model attains a high token-level F1-score, ensuring a balanced precision and recall and a Jaccard similarity score of 0.83, surpassing T5, Flan T5, and BART. The proposed model excels in handling complex medical queries, bridging natural language and SQL to empower data-driven decisions and advance medical informatics.
Review:
Basic reporting:

This manuscript presents a well-motivated and technically sound study on developing a natural language interface (MedQuery) for querying electronic medical record (EMR) databases. The paper is clearly written and well-structured, with a logical flow from the introduction, through a review of related work and baseline models, to a detailed explanation of the proposed methodology and a comprehensive experimental evaluation. The introduction effectively establishes the practical problem: the need for non-technical healthcare professionals to access complex EMR data without SQL expertise.

The literature review is thorough and covers the key developments in NL2SQL research, including recent frameworks like ZNL2SQL, PURPLE, and domain-specific models like Clinical-T5 and BioGPT. The authors correctly identify the limitations of existing approaches and position their work as a solution that combines domain-specific optimization with parameter-efficient fine-tuning. The description of the proposed MedQuery system, including its pipeline (Figure 1), architecture (Figure 2), and the integration of LoRA and QLoRA (Figure 3), is detailed and clear. The mathematical formulations for the attention mechanism and probability calculations are correctly presented and support the technical description.

Experimental design:

The experimental design is robust and well-executed, demonstrating a systematic approach to validating the proposed system.

Dataset: The use of the MIMICSQL dataset, derived from the well-known MIMIC-III database, is highly appropriate. It is a domain-specific, challenging benchmark for text-to-SQL generation in healthcare. The description of the dataset's composition, including the distribution of query types (simple SELECTs, aggregations, joins, nested queries), provides valuable context for interpreting the results.

Baselines: The selection of three strong, general-purpose sequence-to-sequence models (BART, T5, FLAN-T5) as baselines is appropriate. These are widely used and represent the state-of-the-art for many NLP tasks, making for a fair and challenging comparison.

Evaluation Metrics: The choice of evaluation metrics is comprehensive and well-justified. The authors use a mix of:

Syntactic/surface-level metrics: BLEU, ROUGE, Levenshtein Distance.

Semantic/content-overlap metrics: Jaccard Similarity, Token-level F1-score.

Strict correctness metric: Exact Match Accuracy.
This multi-faceted evaluation provides a complete picture of model performance, from n-gram overlap to exact structural correctness.

Ablation Study: The ablation study (Table 2) is a critical strength. It systematically compares full fine-tuning, LoRA-only, QLoRA-only, and the combined LoRA+QLoRA approach on the CodeT5 backbone. This cleanly isolates the contribution of each technique and provides valuable insights into the trade-offs between memory usage, training time, and accuracy.

Hardware and Training Details: The authors provide clear details on the computational environment (Tesla T4 GPU), optimizer (AdamW), learning rate, batch size, and training epochs, ensuring reproducibility.

Error Analysis and Use Cases: The inclusion of a brief error analysis (identifying common failure patterns) and two representative use cases (with figures showing tabular and visual outputs) adds practical value and demonstrates the system's real-world applicability.

Validity of the findings:

The findings are valid and strongly supported by the experimental data.

The primary claim that MedQuery (CodeT5 + LoRA + QLoRA) outperforms the baselines is convincingly demonstrated in Table 1 and Figures 4-9. The improvements in BLEU (81.68 vs. 62.31 for T5), Exact Match (35.2% vs. 25.6%), and ROUGE scores are substantial and meaningful.

The ablation study (Table 2) provides clear evidence for the effectiveness of the proposed approach. It shows that LoRA+QLoRA not only drastically reduces the memory footprint (from 10.8 GB to 3.8 GB) and training time but also achieves the highest accuracy across most metrics. This directly answers Research Question 1 and 2.

The convergence analysis (Figure 10) visually supports the claim of faster and more stable training for the proposed model compared to the baselines.

The error analysis is honest and adds credibility. Acknowledging that the model sometimes struggles with complex logical operators or case sensitivity issues is a sign of rigorous evaluation.

The use cases (Figures 11 & 12) effectively demonstrate the practical utility of the system, bridging the gap between technical performance metrics and real-world clinical/administrative tasks, thus addressing Research Question 3.

Additional comments:

This is a high-quality piece of research that makes a significant contribution to the field of applied NLP in healthcare. The combination of a domain-specific dataset, a strong backbone model (CodeT5), and a rigorous evaluation of parameter-efficient fine-tuning techniques (LoRA, QLoRA) is a model for how to develop practical, deployable AI systems for resource-constrained environments. The paper is well-written, methodologically sound, and the conclusions are fully supported by the evidence.

Major Comments:

None. The paper is technically sound and the conclusions are well-supported.

Minor Comments:

These are very minor suggestions for further polish.

Clarity on Baseline Model Sizes: In Table 1, the performance of BART is significantly lower than the other models. It would be helpful to briefly note in the text that BART, while a strong model, may be less suited for the highly structured task of SQL generation compared to T5 and FLAN-T5, which are built on the text-to-text framework.

Token-Level F1-score Comparison: In Table 1, the proposed model has a Token-level F1-score of 0.96, while T5 achieves 0.98. The text mentions this slight difference (Page 20, lines 15-16). It would be good to add a very brief explanation for this, e.g., "This minor trade-off is likely due to the model's focus on structural and semantic accuracy, which boosts BLEU and ROUGE scores, sometimes at the cost of marginally lower token-level precision."

Table 2 Formatting: In Table 2, the column header "Levenshtein ?" is clear, but for consistency with the other columns that include the units (e.g., "BLEU (%)"), it could be written as "Levenshtein ? (Edits)" or simply ensure the downward arrow is clearly understood as "lower is better." The same applies to "Jaccard ?" and "Token-F1".

Typo/Grammar:

Page 11, line 22: "an example" should be "example".

Page 22, line 22: "optimised" (British spelling) is used, while the rest of the paper uses American spelling (e.g., "optimized" on page 21). Choose one style for consistency.

Article Title: Environmental drivers of Catostylus tagi polyp survival and reproduction: unlocking the role of temperature and salinity, supported with citizen science data
Abstract:
Background
Catostylus tagi is a scyphozoan jellyfish native to Portuguese waters. While its life cycle is known, the environmental conditions that support polyp survival, trigger strobilation, and promote asexual reproduction remain unclear. Field observations from the citizen science GelAvista project indicate that C. tagi occurs year-round in the Tagus estuary, suggesting tolerance to broad temperature and salinity ranges. However, polyps and ephyrae have not been observed in the wild, and their natural habitats and environmental preferences remain unknown. This study aims to fill these knowledge gaps by investigating how temperature and salinity affect C. tagi polyps under controlled conditions and integrating these results with field data.

Methods
Ninety-six polyps were cultured for 71 days in well plates at four temperature treatments (14, 17, 20, 23 °C) and four salinity levels (10, 17.5, 25, 35). Survival, asexual reproduction, and strobilation were monitored. Seven years of citizen science data were analysed with environmental parameters to assess estuarine distribution.

Results
Polyp survival was high, except at salinity 35, where mortality increased regardless of temperature. Podocyst production was enhanced at higher temperatures (20–23 °C) and intermediate salinities (17.5–25), although podocyst development into new polyps occurred at lower salinities (10–17.5). Strobilation occurred predominantly at intermediate temperatures (17–20 °C) and salinities (17.5–25). Medusae are most frequently found in the lower Tagus estuary and coastal adjacent areas, exhibiting a peak between July and January. Results showed that wind intensity was negatively correlated with medusa stranding abundances on the shores, possibly displacing individuals away from coastal areas during periods of strong winds. Sea surface temperature (SST), measured two to four months prior to medusae occurrence, was positively correlated with C. tagi abundance.

Discussion
Optimal polyp performance occurred at 17–20 °C and 17.5–25 salinity. When considered alongside citizen science observations, these findings suggest that polyps may occur in estuarine areas where such conditions prevail, such as upper estuary inlets and marinas. It is further hypothesized that ephyrae could disperse to downstream and adjacent coastal zones via wind and tides. The correlation between sightings and past SSTs indicates the ephyrae and young medusae dispersal rate. Furthermore, this study highlights C. tagi’s adaptability to varying environmental conditions and contributes to identifying optimal parameters for polyp well-being and reproduction, with potential applications in jellyfish farming.
Review:
Basic reporting:

This manuscript presents a comprehensive and well-structured study on the environmental drivers (temperature and salinity) affecting the survival, asexual reproduction (podocyst production and development), and strobilation of Catostylus tagi polyps. The paper is exceptionally well-written, with a clear and logical flow from the introduction, through the detailed methodology, to the results and a thoughtful discussion. The introduction effectively establishes the importance of understanding the polyp stage of scyphozoans and the specific knowledge gaps for C. tagi, a locally significant species in Portugal. The literature review is thorough and appropriately cited, covering the complex life cycle of jellyfish and the known effects of environmental variables on different life stages.

The methods are described in exhaustive detail, leaving no ambiguity about the experimental setup, the acclimatization process, the monitoring protocol, and the statistical analyses. This level of detail is a major strength and ensures the study is highly reproducible. The figures are of excellent quality and are highly informative. For example, Figure 1 clearly shows the different life stages, Figure 2 illustrates the complex experimental design, Figure 3 clarifies the strobilation phases, and Figures 5 and 7 provide excellent photographic documentation of podocyst formation and the strobilation cycle. The integration of laboratory data with seven years of citizen science data from the GelAvista project is a significant and valuable aspect of the study.

Experimental design:

The experimental design is a major strength of this work and is executed with a high degree of rigor.

Laboratory Experiment: The factorial design with four temperatures (14, 17, 20, 23°C) and four salinities (10, 17.5, 25, 35) is excellent. These levels are well-chosen to reflect the natural range of conditions in the Tagus estuary. The use of 6 replicates per treatment (96 polyps total) provides a solid statistical foundation. The careful acclimatization protocol and the 71-day experimental period are appropriate. The authors' attention to detail, such as recording pre-existing podocysts to distinguish them from newly produced ones, is commendable.

Data Collection: The daily observations and the clear definition of the three strobilation phases (pre-strobilation, bet-strobilation, strobilation) are methodologically sound and allow for a nuanced analysis of the process.

Citizen Science Data: The use of the GelAvista dataset is appropriate and adds significant ecological context. The authors' approach to data handling is rigorous: they only used records with the highest confidence level, conservatively converted abundance ranks to point estimates, and log-transformed the data to mitigate variability. The consideration of multiple environmental variables (wind, precipitation, air/sea temperature, chlorophyll) and the use of lagged SST in the analysis are sophisticated and appropriate for understanding the timing of the life cycle. The application of Generalized Estimating Equations (GEE) to account for spatial and temporal autocorrelation is a strong statistical choice.

Validity of the findings:

The findings are robust, valid, and strongly supported by the comprehensive dataset and the rigorous experimental and analytical approach.

The primary finding that polyps are eurythermal and euryhaline, but with high mortality at salinity 35, is clearly demonstrated in Table S1 and Figure 4. The 100% survival at lower salinities across all temperatures is a definitive result.

The identification of podocyst production as the sole asexual reproduction strategy is consistent with prior work and is further validated. The significant effects of temperature (higher production at 20-23°C) and salinity (higher production at intermediate salinities) are well-supported by the data in Figure 6 and the statistical analyses (Tables 1 & 2).

The key finding that podocyst development into new polyps occurs predominantly at lower salinities (10-17.5) is a novel and important contribution, clearly shown in Table S1 and supported by the statistical results in Table 1.

The effects of temperature and salinity on strobilation are convincingly demonstrated. The finding that intermediate salinities (17.5-25) and temperatures (17-20°C) promote more strobilation cycles, while higher salinities suppress it, is a clear outcome (Figure 8, Table 1). The detailed analysis of the durations of the strobilation phases (Figure 9, Table 1) provides valuable insights into the underlying physiological processes.

The integration with citizen science data is highly effective. The correlation between SST 2-4 months prior and medusae abundance (Table 6) elegantly links the environmental conditions during the early life stages (polyp strobilation, ephyra development) to the adult population observed later. The negative correlation with wind speed (Tables 5 & S3) is a logical and well-supported finding regarding the dispersal and stranding of medusae.

The authors' discussion of the results in the context of Dynamic Energy Budget theory and their hypothesis that polyps are located in the upper estuary is thoughtful, well-reasoned, and directly supported by their combined laboratory and field data.

Additional comments:

This is an exemplary piece of research. It is a model of how to combine a well-controlled laboratory experiment with long-term field observations to gain a holistic understanding of an organism's life cycle and ecology. The work is thorough, the analysis is rigorous, and the conclusions are fully supported by the evidence. The paper makes a significant contribution to the biology and ecology of C. tagi and has broader implications for understanding scyphozoan population dynamics and for aquaculture applications.

Major Comments:

None. The paper is of exceptional quality.

Minor Comments:

These are very minor suggestions for further polish.

Figure 1 Caption: The caption for Figure 1 could be slightly expanded to note that the polyp (B) is in pre-strobilation stage with podocysts visible, and the ephyra (C) is a recently released juvenile. This would make the figure even more self-explanatory.

Figure 3 Reference: In the caption for Figure 3, the text says "Figure adapted from Gueroun et al. (2021), presented at the 6th GelAvista Meeting." For clarity, it might be better to state that the conceptualization of the phases is adapted, as the images themselves appear original to this study (credit to Pedro F. Silva). Perhaps: "Schematic representation of the strobilation phases (adapted from Gueroun et al., 2021) with images from this study (credits: Pedro F. Silva)."

Typos/Grammar:

Page 4, line 7: "Scyphistoma" is singular; the plural "scyphistomae" or "polyps" would be more appropriate in the context.

Page 18, line 27: "N-W", "S-W", etc. in Table 5 are clear, but expanding them in the caption (e.g., "N-W: north-westerly winds") would be helpful for readers not immediately familiar with the abbreviations.

Page 19, line 3: "lagged two or three months earlier" could be simplified to "lagged by two or three months".

Article Title: Spatio-temporal overlap between purse seine fisheries and Humboldt penguin feeding areas in northern Chile
Abstract: Seabirds face increasing pressure from commercial fisheries through both direct mortality and indirect competition for shared prey resources. In Chile’s Humboldt Current System, artisanal purse-seine fisheries target small pelagic fish species such as anchoveta (Engraulis ringens), which also comprise key components of the Humboldt penguin’s (Spheniscus humboldti) diet. To investigate the potential for spatio-temporal overlap between purse-seine fisheries and penguin foraging areas, we tracked breeding Humboldt penguins from Isla Choros, Coquimbo Region, northern Chile, during the autumn and spring breeding seasons of 2022 using GPS dive loggers. We quantified penguin at-sea distribution and overlap with fishing effort data obtained from purse-seine vessels operating in the Coquimbo Region. Tracking data from 22 penguins revealed a bimodal foraging pattern linked to nest location. Penguins nesting on the eastern, mainland-facing side of Isla Choros foraged exclusively in coastal waters, while western-nesting individuals foraged both offshore and inshore, likely depending on environmental conditions. Kernel Density Estimation (KDE) analysis identified two core foraging zones: one along the mainland coast near Playa de los Choros and another southwest of the island. Purse-seine fishing effort in autumn was concentrated along the coast, overlapping substantially with the coastal foraging zone of the penguins. Nearly 60% of the penguins’ coastal core foraging area overlapped with the 50% KDE of autumn purse-seine activity. In contrast, spring fishing effort was more dispersed and located farther south, with no overlap observed between spring fishing and penguin foraging areas. These findings suggest a high likelihood of indirect resource competition between Humboldt penguins and fisheries during the autumn breeding season—a critical time when penguins are energetically constrained as central place foragers. We provide the first empirical evidence of substantial seasonal overlap between Humboldt penguins and inshore purse-seine fisheries at the Humboldt Archipelago and highlight the need to integrate seabird ecology into fisheries management. To protect vulnerable seabird populations such as the Humboldt penguin, marine spatial planning should consider critical foraging habitats and breeding schedules. Future work should aim to quantify dietary overlap, assess potential sub-lethal effects of prey depletion, and monitor the impact of small-scale, untracked fisheries within key penguin foraging areas.
Review:
Basic reporting:

The manuscript is exceptionally well-written and clearly structured. The English is fluent and precise, making the complex methodology and results easy to follow. The introduction provides a comprehensive and relevant review of the global issue of fisheries impacts on seabirds, effectively narrowing the focus to the knowledge gap concerning Humboldt penguins in Chile. The methods are described in thorough detail, allowing for reproducibility, and the results are presented logically with high-quality figures and tables that directly support the narrative. The authors do an excellent job of explaining the nuances of their data, such as the pooling of penguin data across seasons to correct for a sampling bias.

Experimental design:

The experimental design is appropriate and robust for the research questions posed.

The use of GPS dive loggers on chick-rearing penguins is a standard and effective method for determining at-sea foraging distribution. The authors correctly acknowledge the limitations of their sampling (e.g., the underrepresentation of eastern nests in autumn) and implement a conservative, justifiable solution by pooling seasonal data for the core spatial analysis.

The use of Kernel Density Estimation (KDE) to define utilisation distributions for both penguins and the fishery is an excellent choice for quantifying spatial overlap. The application of a cross-type pair correlation function (cross-PCF) provides a sophisticated, statistical validation of the spatial clustering observed in autumn, moving beyond simple area-overlap calculations.

The study design cleverly leverages the natural "experiment" of the island's topography and the different breeding seasons to reveal nuanced foraging patterns and their interaction with a seasonally variable fishery. The comparison between the spatially concentrated autumn anchoveta fishery and the more dispersed spring fishery targeting other species is a key strength of the design, allowing the authors to highlight the specific conflict during autumn.

Validity of the findings:

The findings are valid and robust.

The core finding—a substantial spatial overlap between penguin foraging areas and the autumn purse-seine fishery—is strongly supported by both the KDE overlap analysis (56.7% overlap of core coastal foraging areas) and the cross-PCF analysis (g(r) = 7 at 700m). The conclusion that this overlap is minimal in spring is also well-supported.

The inference that this spatial overlap implies potential resource competition is logical and appropriately cautious. The authors clearly state the limitations of their study, noting the absence of quantitative diet and prey-availability data. They correctly frame their findings as "suggesting a high likelihood" of competition, which is a fair and scientifically sound interpretation.

The discussion thoughtfully explores alternative explanations for the observed foraging patterns, such as nest-site proximity and the search for predictable prey, which strengthens the overall argument by considering complexity. The acknowledgment of the unquantified but potentially significant impact of small-scale (<12m) vessels is another example of the authors' balanced and critical perspective on their findings.

Additional comments:

This is a high-quality piece of applied conservation science. The manuscript could be further strengthened by addressing the following points:

Depth of Discussion on Competition: While the discussion on resource competition is good, it could be deepened by more explicitly linking the findings to the concept of "competition for space" vs. "competition for prey." The study brilliantly quantifies spatial overlap, but a more detailed exploration of why this is problematic (beyond the spatial co-use) would be beneficial. For example, consider discussing that even if the fish stock is healthy overall, the localized depletion of schools directly adjacent to the colony during the energetically demanding guard stage could force penguins to travel further or work harder, with direct fitness consequences. This ties in well with the "Ashmole's halo" concept and central-place foraging theory.

Clarity on "Autumn" and "Spring" Definitions: The manuscript defines the study periods clearly in the text (e.g., lines 220-223, 310-311). However, for a quick-reference, it would be helpful to add a brief note to the caption of Figure 4 or Table 2, explicitly stating the months included for each penguin season (e.g., "Penguin data are pooled from June 2022 and Nov-Dec 2022") and each fishing season. This would prevent any potential confusion for readers scanning the figures.

Minor Suggestion on Terminology: The term "bimodal foraging distribution" is used to describe the two core areas (coastal and offshore). This is accurate. However, it might be slightly clarified that this is a "colony-level" bimodal distribution, driven by nest-site location, rather than individual birds exhibiting two distinct foraging modes (though some western birds do).

Article Title: Plant origin and irrigation influence floral resource value and pollinator attraction to ornamental plants
Abstract: Irrigation and plant origin are key factors influencing plant growth, development, and reproductive strategies. These same factors may also affect the floral resource value of plants to pollinators, influencing the efficacy of pollinator plantings for conservation or enhancement. While these factors have been studied separately, few studies have examined how plant origin influences plant responses to irrigation for floral traits such as nectar and pollen. In this study, we evaluated 10 native and 10 non-native plants to Florida under two different irrigation treatments and measured floral density, floral display, floral resources (nectar and pollen), and pollinator visitation rates and diversity in an open field study. There was strong to very strong evidence that native plants produced higher floral densities and displays, along with greater amounts of pollen per plot, and attracted a higher number and diversity of pollinators as compared to non-native plants. Additionally, there was weak to moderate evidence that fully irrigated plants produced higher nectar volumes and pollen quantities per plot as well as pollen with higher protein content as compared to partially irrigated plants. However, there was no evidence that native and non-native plants responded differently to irrigation treatment. Additionally, individual pollinator guilds responded differently to origin and irrigation, highlighting the complexity of plant-pollinator interactions. We conclude that native plants consistently produce more flowers and attract a greater number and diversity of pollinators while irrigation affects the production and resource value of pollen and nectar. These findings emphasize the importance of considering both plant origin and irrigation on resource availability of flowers when selecting pollinator-friendly plants under varying climates and management regimes.
Review:
Basic reporting:

The manuscript is exceptionally well-written, clear, and logically structured. The English is fluent and precise throughout. The introduction provides a thorough and well-cited background, effectively establishing the importance of floral resources for pollinators, the threats they face from habitat loss and climate change, and the specific knowledge gap regarding the interaction between plant origin and irrigation. The methods are described in exhaustive and reproducible detail, from the selection of congeneric plant pairs to the nuanced statistical analyses. The results are presented clearly, with effective use of figures and tables that directly support the narrative. The discussion thoughtfully interprets the findings in the context of the existing literature, acknowledges the study's limitations (e.g., single-site nectar/pollen sampling, mild drought treatment, degree of plant cultivation), and provides clear, practical conclusions for gardeners and landscape managers.

Experimental design:

The experimental design is a significant strength of this study and is highly appropriate for the research questions.

The use of congeneric native and non-native pairs is an excellent and rigorous approach that controls for phylogenetic differences, allowing the authors to confidently attribute observed effects to plant origin rather than major differences in floral morphology or basic biology.

The study is well-replicated across two distinct sites in Florida and over two full years, which greatly increases the robustness and generalizability of the findings. The randomized block design with split-plot irrigation treatments is standard and correctly implemented.

The response variables measured are comprehensive, moving beyond simple pollinator counts to quantify the mechanisms driving visitation—namely, the quantity and quality of floral resources (nectar volume, pollen quantity, pollen protein content). This multi-faceted approach allows for a much deeper understanding of the system.

The statistical approach is sophisticated and appropriate, using generalized linear mixed models with the correct distributions for different data types (e.g., negative binomial for counts, Tweedie for skewed continuous data). The inclusion of "person" as a random effect in the nectar analysis demonstrates a keen attention to potential sources of experimental error.

Validity of the findings:

The findings are robust, well-supported by the data, and interpreted with appropriate caution.

The core finding—that native plants generally outperform non-natives in floral display and in attracting a higher abundance and diversity of most pollinator groups—is strongly supported (very strong statistical evidence) and aligns well with ecological theory about co-evolution.

The finding that irrigation, while not affecting flower-level resources, had subtle but significant cumulative effects on plot-level nectar and pollen quantity and pollen protein quality is nuanced and valid. The authors' explanation that minor per-flower reductions, when summed across a plot, can become biologically significant is logical.

The differential responses of pollinator guilds (e.g., honey bees showing no preference, bumble bees preferring non-natives, solitary bees strongly preferring natives) are a fascinating and valid result that highlights the complexity of plant-pollinator networks and the danger of over-generalization. These patterns are well-documented in the supplementary materials.

The authors are appropriately cautious in their interpretation of the non-significant interaction between origin and irrigation. They correctly note that the mild drought treatment and the high background rainfall during the study period may have limited their ability to detect differential responses, a key point that prevents over-interpretation of the null result.

Additional comments:

This is a model study for applied ecology. I have only minor suggestions to further strengthen an already excellent manuscript.

Clarification on "Partial Irrigation" Treatment: While the Methods mention the target of 10% volumetric water content (lines 235-238), it would be helpful to briefly explain in the text what this practically meant for the plants. Was this a mild stress, or did it cause visible wilting? Adding a qualitative observation of plant stress levels (e.g., "Plants in the partial irrigation treatment showed occasional midday wilting but no long-term decline") would help readers interpret the ecological relevance of the irrigation treatment.

Depth of Discussion on Pollinator Guilds: The finding that bumble bees and carpenter bees preferred non-native plants is intriguing. The discussion touches on this (lines 448-455), but it could be slightly expanded. For instance, was this preference driven by a single highly attractive non-native species (like Salvia longispicata), or was it a consistent pattern across multiple non-natives? Briefly addressing whether this preference was species-specific or a general trend would add depth.

Consistency in Figure Captions: Ensure all figure captions are fully self-explanatory. For example, in Figure 2, the panels are labeled A-F, but the caption lists variables (e.g., "pollen quantity per plot") without clearly linking them to the letters in a way that is immediately scannable. A simple addition like "(A) Floral density, (B) Floral display, (C) Pollen quantity per plot..." would improve readability.

Article Title: Species-specific community structure in the microbiomes and eukaryotic communities associated with Mediterranean golf ball sponges
Abstract:
Background
Sponges harbor complex and diverse microbiomes that contribute to the host’s fitness and, ultimately, the health of the ecosystems sponges inhabit.

Methods
Using high-throughput 16S and 18S rRNA amplicon sequencing, we explore the prokaryotic and eukaryotic communities associated with three sympatric Mediterranean demosponges, namely Tethya aurantium, Tethya meloni, and Tethya citrina.

Results
We found species-specific prokaryotic and eukaryotic communities despite the close sympatry of the three Mediterranean Tethya species studied. This offers further support for the phylogenetic nature of the sponge microbiome, where microbial communities reflect the evolutionary ancestry of their host species. These patterns are both present in the eukaryotic and prokaryotic sponge-associated communities, since both display similar levels of host species specificity.
Review:
Basic reporting:

The manuscript is generally well-written and clear. The introduction provides a good overview of the holobiont concept and sponge microbiology, effectively setting the stage for the study's rationale. The methods are described in sufficient detail to be reproducible, and the results are presented systematically, with a logical flow from prokaryotic to eukaryotic communities. The figures and tables are of high quality and effectively illustrate the key findings, such as the species-specific clustering in NMDS plots and the differences in core communities. The discussion thoughtfully interprets the results in the context of existing sponge microbiome literature and acknowledges the study's limitations.

Experimental design:

The experimental design is robust and well-suited to the research questions.

The choice of three sympatric, congeneric Tethya species is a major strength. This natural experimental setup controls for environmental variation, allowing the authors to confidently attribute differences in microbiome composition to host identity rather than external factors.

The sample sizes (12-19 individuals per species) are adequate for the community-level analyses performed.

The use of high-throughput sequencing targeting both the V4 region of 16S rRNA (for prokaryotes) and the V9 region of 18S rRNA (for eukaryotes) provides a comprehensive picture of the holobiont. This dual approach is commendable and addresses a common gap in the literature, which often focuses solely on prokaryotes.

The bioinformatic and statistical analyses are appropriate and thorough. The use of multiple complementary methods—such as OTU overlap analyses, core community definitions, NMDS ordination with PERMANOVA, and rank-abundance dominance (RAD) modeling—provides a robust and multi-faceted view of the data. The authors also correctly test for and discuss the issue of heterogeneous group dispersions, which adds rigor to their interpretation of the PERMANOVA results.

Validity of the findings:

The findings are valid, well-supported by the data, and make a significant contribution to the field.

The conclusion that prokaryotic communities are species-specific is strongly supported by the data (PERMANOVA, CCA) and aligns with the broader sponge microbiome literature. The finding that T. citrina has a distinct, more even, and OTU-rich prokaryotic community compared to its congeners is robust and intriguing.

The finding that eukaryotic communities are also species-specific is particularly novel and valuable, as this component of the sponge holobiont is often understudied. The data clearly show species-level clustering (Fig. 11), supporting this conclusion.

The observation that the prokaryotic communities share a higher proportion of OTUs across species (58%) compared to the eukaryotic communities (18%) is a fascinating and valid result. This suggests different assembly rules or transmission mechanisms for these two microbial components and opens up interesting avenues for future research.

The authors are appropriately cautious in their interpretations. They acknowledge the potential for primer bias (e.g., co-amplification of sponge DNA) and the high number of unclassified eukaryotic OTUs, which limits detailed taxonomic interpretation but does not undermine the core finding of species-specificity.

Additional comments:

This is a strong manuscript that provides a comprehensive dataset on the microbiomes of sympatric sponges. I have only minor suggestions that could further improve the clarity and impact of the work.

Clarification on "Core Community" Definition: The authors define the core community as OTUs present in at least 90% of samples for a given species (line 193). This is a reasonable threshold, but it is worth briefly noting in the text that this is a relatively stringent definition. A quick comment on how results might change with a different threshold (e.g., 80% or 100%) would add nuance, though it is not necessary for the current analysis.

Discussion of Eukaryotic "Unclassified" OTUs: The high proportion of unclassified eukaryotic OTUs (73%) is a key finding, highlighting a vast unknown biodiversity. While the authors mention this (lines 508-511), the discussion could be slightly strengthened by speculating on the potential identity or ecological role of these organisms. For example, could they be parasitic, commensal, or serve as a food source? This would help readers appreciate the significance of this "dark diversity."

Integration of Prokaryotic and Eukaryotic Findings: The discussion treats the prokaryotic and eukaryotic communities largely in separate sections. A brief, dedicated paragraph explicitly comparing and contrasting the patterns observed in the two datasets (e.g., difference in shared OTU proportions, differences in core community structure) would be a valuable addition. This would synthesize the two halves of the study and more powerfully deliver the main message about the distinct yet host-specific nature of the entire holobiont.

Article Title: Seasonality shapes gut microbiota composition in two sympatric sea urchins
Abstract:
Background
The gut microbiota plays critical roles in digestion, immunity, and environmental adaptation in marine invertebrates. Its composition is shaped by both host traits and abiotic factors such as temperature and seasonality. In Caribbean reefs, sea urchins like Diadema antillarum and Echinometra lucunter are important grazers that regulate algal biomass and influence benthic community dynamics. This study used 16S rRNA gene sequencing to compare, for the first time, the gut microbiota of these two sympatric species across contrasting seasons.

Methods
Adults of D. antillarum and E. lucunter were collected during summer and winter from three fringing reefs in Puerto Rico. Gut contents were extracted under sterile conditions, and bacterial DNA was extracted amplified for their 16S rRNA genes and sequenced. Microbial diversity and structure were assessed standard microbiota pipelines.

Results
Microbial communities in both echinoids were dominated by Bacillota, Bacteroidota, Desulfobacterota, and Pseudomonadota. Core microbiota analysis revealed stable taxa across hosts and seasons, most notably Propionigenium, supporting host-driven selection mechanisms that maintain functional stability despite seasonal turnover. Core microbiome analyses revealed Bacillota, Bacteroidota, Desulfobacterota_G_459543, and Pseudomonadota as persistent phyla across seasons, and genera such as Desulfotalea, Photobacterium, and Propionigenium consistently present in both species. Alpha diversity was significantly higher in winter for both D. antillarum and E. lucunter, while species-level differences were not significant. Beta diversity analyses showed significant seasonal clustering, with no consistent segregation between species within seasons. Our findings demonstrate that shared environmental conditions, particularly seasonality, are the primary modulators of gut microbiota in sympatric sea urchins, while host selection preserves a conserved core community. This dual influence of environment and host highlights the ecological plasticity and resilience of sea urchin microbiotas under fluctuating reef conditions.
Review:
Basic reporting:

The manuscript is clearly written and well-structured. The abstract provides an accurate and concise summary of the study's background, methods, results, and conclusions. The introduction effectively establishes the ecological importance of the two sea urchin species, the functional roles of gut microbiota, and the known impacts of environmental factors like seasonality, leading logically to a clear hypothesis and well-defined objectives. The methods are described in extensive detail, covering sample collection, DNA extraction, sequencing, and a comprehensive suite of bioinformatic and statistical analyses, ensuring reproducibility. The results are presented systematically, with high-quality figures and tables that clearly illustrate the key findings, such as the seasonal clustering in PCoA plots and the seasonal shifts in alpha diversity and taxonomy. The discussion thoughtfully interprets the findings in the context of existing literature, addresses potential mechanisms (e.g., diet, temperature), and acknowledges the study's limitations.

Experimental design:

The experimental design is robust and well-suited to the research questions.

The choice of two sympatric species is a major strength, as it naturally controls for broad-scale environmental variation and allows for a direct test of whether host identity or shared environment is the primary driver of microbiota composition.

The seasonal sampling (summer and winter) is appropriate for testing the hypothesis that temperature and associated environmental changes shape microbial communities.

The sample size (a total of 50 individuals across two species and two seasons) is adequate for the community-level analyses performed.

The use of 16S rRNA gene amplicon sequencing is the standard method for prokaryotic community characterization. The authors employed a consistent and well-documented bioinformatics pipeline (Qiita, Deblur, Greengenes2), which is critical for data comparability.

The statistical approach is thorough and appropriate, employing a wide range of complementary methods: alpha diversity metrics, beta diversity (Bray-Curtis, UniFrac) with PERMANOVA and ANOSOM, Random Forest for biomarker discovery, MaAsLin2 for multivariable association, and core microbiome analysis. The inclusion of 'qita_study_id' as a covariate in MaAsLin2 to control for potential batch effects between sequencing runs is a particularly strong and commendable aspect of the experimental design, demonstrating rigorous attention to a common confounding factor.

Validity of the findings:

The findings are valid, well-supported by the data, and represent a significant contribution to the field.

The central finding—that seasonality is the primary driver of gut microbiota structure, with both species showing parallel and significant shifts between summer and winter—is robustly supported by the beta diversity analyses (PERMANOVA, ANOSOM, PCoA plots) and the consistent patterns in alpha diversity (higher in winter for both species).

The conclusion that the two species do not harbor significantly different gut communities within a given season (especially in summer) is also well-supported by the statistical comparisons and the overlapping clustering in ordination plots. This strongly reinforces the message that shared environmental conditions are paramount.

The identification of a stable core microbiota, including genera like Propionigenium, is a valuable finding. The authors' interpretation that this represents host-driven selection for functionally important symbionts (e.g., propionate production) is plausible and well-supported by previous literature.

The use of Random Forest and MaAsLin2 to identify seasonally-enriched taxa (e.g., Desulfobacterota, Spirochaetota in winter; Bacillota in summer) adds depth and specificity to the findings. The authors are appropriately cautious in their interpretation of these patterns, linking them to potential changes in diet and temperature.

The acknowledgment of study limitations, particularly the potential for batch effects between sequencing runs and the relatively low rarefaction depth, is transparent and demonstrates scientific rigor. The steps taken to mitigate these issues (e.g., using 'qita_study_id' as a covariate) further validate the robustness of the core conclusions.

Additional comments:

This is a strong and important manuscript. The integration of new summer data with previously published winter data provides a powerful longitudinal perspective. I have only minor suggestions that could further enhance the clarity and impact of the work.

Clarity on the 2022 Mortality Event: The discussion briefly mentions the 2022 D. antillarum mass mortality event and associated microbial shifts (lines 506-513). This is a highly relevant and compelling point. To strengthen this section, the authors could add a brief sentence speculating on how their findings on seasonal stability and core microbiota might inform our understanding of such disease events. For example, does the seasonal shift in diversity (lower in summer) potentially correlate with increased disease susceptibility? This would provide a powerful "so what?" moment for conservation-focused readers.

Integration of Diet Discussion: The section on diet (lines 440-470) is informative, but it is somewhat disconnected from the main results. The authors suggest that dietary shifts may drive the seasonal changes in microbiota. To strengthen this argument, it would be helpful to briefly revisit the main findings (e.g., increase in fermentative phyla in winter) in this section and explicitly link them to the hypothesized dietary changes (e.g., "The increase in fiber-fermenting taxa like *x* in winter is consistent with a diet shift towards more complex macroalgae..."). This would create a more cohesive narrative.

Figure Legends: The figure legends are generally good but could be made slightly more self-explanatory. For example, in Figure 1, it would be helpful to state in the legend that "Black" and "Red" refer to D. antillarum and E. lucunter, respectively, as these colors are used throughout the figures. While this is clear from the methods and some figure labels, adding it to the main figure caption would improve readability.

Article Title: The distribution characteristics of medusozoans (Cnidaria) related to environmental factors in cold source water intake area of the Fangchenggang Nuclear Power Plant based on eDNA metabarcoding technology
Abstract: The free-swimming gelatinous stage of medusozoans, generally called ‘jellyfish’, frequently swarms around the intake area of the Fangchenggang Nuclear Power Plant, posing a noticeable risk to its operational safety. Traditional ecological survey methods have limitations in rapidly and effectively assessing the diversity and spatiotemporal distribution of medusozoans, particularly during their early life stages, such as planulae, polyps, and ephyrae. To compensate for these limitations, we employed environmental DNA (eDNA) metabarcoding technology—a non-invasive, efficient, and broadly applicable approach—to conduct targeted surveys during the winter and summer seasons. The findings revealed notable seasonal variations in medusozoan community composition, with 29 and 56 species identified in winter and summer, respectively (p < 0.05). A higher abundance of Hydrozoa and relatively fewer Scyphozoa were observed in both seasons. Horizontally, no significant differences were identified among the defined zones (p > 0.05); however, specific sites such as estuarine, water intake, and drainage areas exhibited unique environmental features influencing the distribution of medusozoans. Vertically, within the 3–8 m depth range, no significant differences in the distribution of them were observed, likely due to the limited depth range and enhanced vertical mixing of the water column (p > 0.05). These observations underscore the utility of eDNA metabarcoding for the rapid detection and assessment of medusozoan diversity and spatiotemporal distribution. As a valuable supplement to traditional survey techniques, this method enhances medusozoan monitoring in marine areas adjacent to Fangchenggang Nuclear Power Plant. Moreover, it enables the investigation of how temperature, salinity, dissolved oxygen, and chlorophyll ? variations—driven by both climate change and human activities—influence medusozoan distribution patterns around coastal infrastructures globally.
Review:
Basic reporting:

The manuscript is generally well-written and the narrative flows logically from the introduction to the conclusions. The English is clear and understandable. The introduction provides an excellent and comprehensive overview of the challenges posed by jellyfish to nuclear power plants, the limitations of traditional survey methods, and the potential of eDNA metabarcoding, effectively setting the stage for the study's rationale and objectives. The methods are described in substantial detail, allowing for reproducibility, particularly regarding the rigorous contamination controls and bioinformatics pipeline. The results are presented systematically, with a logical progression from seasonal to horizontal to vertical distribution patterns. The figures and tables are of high quality and effectively illustrate the key findings, such as the seasonal shifts in dominant species (Fig. 3) and the comparative advantage of eDNA over net sampling (Fig. 8). The discussion thoughtfully interprets the findings, linking observed patterns to environmental factors (temperature, salinity, Chl \(\alpha\)) and anthropogenic influences (thermal discharge, estuarine inflow), and acknowledges the study's limitations.

Experimental design:

The experimental design is robust and well-suited to the research questions.

The strategic selection of eight sampling sites, categorized into four zones representing different environmental conditions (estuarine, intake, outfall, and distal areas), is a major strength. This spatial design allows for a nuanced investigation of how specific features of the power plant's influence (e.g., thermal discharge, entrainment) and natural gradients (e.g., estuarine salinity) affect medusozoan distribution.

The seasonal sampling (winter and summer) is appropriate for testing the hypothesis of significant temporal variation, as jellyfish populations are known to be highly seasonal. The collection of both surface and bottom samples also allows for a preliminary assessment of vertical distribution, although the shallow depth limits this analysis.

The use of a multi-faceted methodological approach, comparing eDNA metabarcoding results with traditional net sampling, is commendable. This validation step strengthens the conclusions about the relative strengths and weaknesses of each method.

The choice of the COI marker and the mlCOIintF-jgHCO2198 primer pair is justified and appropriate for targeting a wide range of metazoans, including medusozoans, with a short amplicon length suitable for Illumina sequencing. The rigorous contamination controls (negative extraction/PCR controls) and bioinformatic filtering (removing low-abundance OTUs, cross-referencing negative controls) are essential and well-documented.

The statistical analyses are appropriate, using non-parametric tests for alpha diversity comparisons and RDA for linking community composition to environmental variables. The use of Spearman correlation at the species level provides a complementary, more detailed view of species-environment relationships.

Validity of the findings:

The findings are valid, well-supported by the data, and represent a significant contribution to the field of marine monitoring and power plant safety.

The core finding that eDNA metabarcoding detects a much higher diversity of medusozoans (especially Hydrozoa) than traditional net sampling is robust and convincingly demonstrated (Fig. 8, Table S2). This highlights the technology's value for comprehensive biodiversity assessments and for detecting small, fragile, or early life stages that are otherwise missed.

The significant seasonal difference in community composition, with higher diversity in summer, is strongly supported by the alpha diversity indices and the clear shift in dominant taxa (Figs. 2 & 3). This aligns with the expectation that warmer temperatures and increased productivity support greater biodiversity.

The identification of environmental drivers (temperature, salinity, Chl \(\alpha\)) correlating with medusozoan distribution patterns is plausible and well-supported by the RDA and Spearman correlation analyses. The specific observations, such as the shift in species dominance along the thermal gradient from the outfall in winter (Fig. 3A) and the detection of freshwater Hydra near the estuary, provide compelling, site-specific examples of these environmental influences.

The authors are appropriately cautious in their interpretations. They thoroughly discuss the limitations of eDNA, including database incompleteness, primer bias, the potential for false positives/negatives, and the inability to distinguish between eDNA from living organisms versus degraded or transported material. The acknowledgment of "species inquirendae" and the suggestion to focus on higher taxonomic levels for broader patterns are honest and scientifically sound.

Additional comments:

This is a high-quality, applied study with clear practical implications. I have only minor suggestions that could further enhance the manuscript.

Clarity on "Species inquirendae": The term "species inquirendae" is used but could be more clearly defined for a broader audience. In lines 226-227 and again in the discussion (lines 759-771), it is described as representing potentially under-recognized or undescribed species. It would be helpful to explicitly state that these are OTUs that match to sequences in the database with a high confidence level, but the corresponding taxonomic entry is flagged as "inquirenda" (meaning "to be investigated") due to uncertain taxonomic status. This clarifies that it is a database issue, not just a low-similarity match.

Integration of Net Sampling Data: The comparison with net sampling in Figure 8 and Table S2 is a key strength. To make this even more impactful, consider adding a brief qualitative statement in the main results or discussion about the size or life stage of the species uniquely detected by each method. For example, "eDNA uniquely detected small hydrozoans and likely early life stages, while net sampling exclusively captured large, adult scyphozoans like Rhopilema hispidum." This would reinforce the message that the methods are complementary.

Minor Suggestion on Figure 1: The map in Figure 1 is clear. Adding a very brief note in the caption about the water depth range (3-8 m) would be helpful for readers as they interpret the vertical distribution results later in the manuscript.

Article Title: A quick and reliable menthol-induced bleaching protocol for the Caribbean staghorn coral, Acropora cervicornis
Abstract: Corals and dinoflagellate algae form a unique mutualistic symbiosis that provides the energetic and structural foundation for shallow coral reef ecosystems. Despite the long success of this partnership in oligotrophic seas, coral reefs are in decline due to increasing threats from rising seawater temperatures and disease, both of which can lead to bleaching and mortality. In order to better understand the mechanisms that underpin this mutualism, it may be necessary to dismantle the coral-algal symbiosis. Previous studies have experimentally bleached corals using thermal stress, photosynthetic inhibitors (DCMU), and menthol. We compared lab-induced bleaching of staghorn coral Acropora cervicornis by menthol treatment to traditional thermal stress. The larger aim was to adapt existing bleaching protocols for this important coral species, providing a guide for future studies. Bleaching in corals treated with menthol or exposed to elevated temperature stress (31°C) was monitored by measuring photosynthetic activity determined by Fv/Fm using pulse-amplitude modulated (PAM) fluorescence and compared to untreated conspecifics. Corals were also monitored for symbiont density and overall health using the CoralWatch Coral Health Chart card throughout the experiment. We found that A. cervicornis bleached in response to both menthol treatment and thermal stress, but menthol treatment was more effective at reducing algal symbiont photosynthetic capacity (Fv/Fm) without negatively affecting the health of the coral. Our results indicate that menthol treatment at 0.38 mM rendered staghorn coral aposymbiotic within fourteen days without any visual or physiological damage to the coral. This study provides a simple and effective menthol-bleaching treatment protocol for future studies on staghorn coral.
Review:
Basic reporting:

The manuscript is exceptionally well-written, clear, and focused. The English is fluent and precise. The introduction succinctly establishes the ecological importance of A. cervicornis, the need for experimental bleaching tools in symbiosis research and cryopreservation, and the rationale for testing menthol as an alternative to thermal stress. The methods are described in thorough, step-by-step detail, ensuring the protocol is easily replicable. The results are presented logically, with high-quality figures that clearly illustrate the key findings, such as the dose-dependent response of Fv/Fm (Fig. 3) and the comparative advantage of menthol over thermal bleaching (Fig. 4). The discussion effectively interprets the results, compares them to existing literature, thoughtfully addresses the distinct mechanisms of menthol vs. thermal bleaching, and outlines the practical applications and future directions for the protocol.

Experimental design:

The experimental design is robust and perfectly suited to the study's goal of protocol development.

The use of a single, well-characterized genotype (ML-31) is a key strength. This controls for genetic variation in bleaching response, allowing the observed effects to be attributed definitively to the treatments.

The experimental setup with controlled aquaria, filtered seawater, and monitored environmental parameters (temperature, light) is appropriate and ensures replicable conditions.

The experimental design includes three key treatment groups: a negative control (ambient temperature, no menthol), a positive control (elevated temperature stress), and the experimental menthol treatments across three doses (0.19, 0.38, and 0.58 mM). This allows for a clear and direct comparison of the efficacy and side effects of the new method against the existing standard.

The response variables are well-chosen. The use of PAM fluorometry (Fv/Fm) provides a quantitative, non-invasive measure of symbiont photosynthetic performance. The CoralWatch Coral Health Chart offers a simple, rapid, and standardized visual assessment of symbiont density and overall coral health, which is practical for a protocol-focused study.

The menthol treatment regimen (daily 6-hour exposures, followed by overnight recovery) is well-considered, mimicking a pulse-stress event and allowing for monitoring of the bleaching progression.

Validity of the findings:

The findings are robust, clearly supported by the data, and directly address the study's objectives.

The conclusion that 0.38 mM menthol is the optimal dose is well-supported. The data show that this dose achieves a significant and rapid reduction in Fv/Fm (comparable to the higher 0.58 mM dose) without the acute toxicity (mesenterial filament extension, partial mortality) observed at the highest dose (0.58 mM). The 0.19 mM dose was demonstrably less effective.

The comparison showing that one week of menthol treatment reduces Fv/Fm more than four weeks of thermal stress (Fig. 4) is a striking and valid result that powerfully demonstrates the efficiency of the new protocol.

The observation that menthol-treated corals maintained good health (polyp extension, feeding, no necrosis) during treatment, while thermally-stressed corals showed signs of stress and mortality, is a critical finding that validates the "gentler" nature of the chemical bleaching method for this species.

The authors are appropriately cautious in their interpretation. They acknowledge the delayed partial mortality in aposymbiotic corals post-treatment, correctly attributing it to starvation rather than direct toxicity, and suggest solutions like nutritional support or re-inoculation. They also importantly note that the cellular mechanisms of menthol and thermal bleaching are distinct, meaning the protocol may not perfectly replicate natural bleaching physiology, which is a crucial caveat for researchers planning to use it.

Additional comments:

This is an excellent example of a methods-focused study that will be of high value to the coral research and restoration community. I have only minor suggestions for improvement.

Clarify Post-Treatment Mortality Statement: In the discussion (lines 309-312), the authors state that "partial mortality occurred in subsequent weeks after the experiment, impacting 56.3% of frags and which was seemingly independent of treatment." It would be helpful to clarify whether this mortality was evenly distributed across all treatment groups (including controls) or if it was specific to the bleached corals. If it occurred only in bleached corals (menthol and temperature), this would strengthen the argument that it was due to aposymbiosis and starvation. If it also occurred in controls, it would suggest a different cause (e.g., handling stress).

Menthol Mechanism in Discussion: The discussion of the potential mechanisms of menthol bleaching (lines 319-332) is excellent. To further enhance this section, the authors could briefly mention that, regardless of the exact mechanism, the practical outcome for users of this protocol is a reliable and rapid method to generate aposymbiotic corals, which is the primary goal of the study. This would tie the mechanistic discussion back to the paper's applied focus.

Minor Suggestion for Figure 4: Figure 4 is very effective. To make it even more informative, consider adding the treatment duration on the x-axis labels (e.g., "Menthol (7 days)" and "Thermal (28 days)") as is already done in the figure legend. This is already clear in the legend, but adding it to the axis labels would make the figure slightly more self-explanatory at a quick glance.

Article Title: A different world: temporal changes in the community structure of sea slugs (Heterobranchia) in northwest Japan spanning more than a half-century
Abstract: Understanding long-term changes in marine biodiversity is essential for evaluating effects of climate change on coastal ecosystems. In this study, we compared heterobranch sea slug (hereafter referred to simply as ‘sea slugs’) assemblages in northwestern Kyushu, Japan, across three survey periods: (1) historical records from 1960–1980, for which the exact survey effort and duration were not documented; (2) surveys conducted from April 2001 to September 2003; and (3) recent surveys conducted from June 2023 to January 2024. A total of 47 sea slug species were recorded from our underwater surveys conducted in 2023–2024. Species diversity indices, including the Shannon–Wiener diversity index (H) and Simpson’s diversity index (D), showed higher values during this survey than those of 2001–2003. Comparative analysis of species composition revealed significant shifts, with 15 species exhibiting statistically significant differences in relative abundance from the past to the present. Notably, several species common in the past, such as Aplysia kurodai, were rarely observed in the recent survey, while many tropical–subtropical species appeared for the first time. The proportion of tropical–subtropical species increased markedly, whereas subarctic species were no longer detected. Jaccard’s coefficient indicated that the current community differs markedly from those in earlier periods. These findings suggest a major community reorganization, potentially driven by rising seawater temperatures and other environmental changes. This study highlights the importance of long-term monitoring using multiple indicators to detect and interpret climate-driven biodiversity shifts in coastal marine ecosystems.
Review:
Basic reporting:

The manuscript is exceptionally well-written, clear, and logically structured. The English is fluent and precise. The abstract provides an excellent summary of the study's aims, methods, key results, and implications. The introduction effectively establishes the context, highlighting the sensitivity of sea slugs to environmental change and the scarcity of long-term studies, particularly in Japan. The methods are described in sufficient detail to be reproducible, with careful attention paid to standardizing species names across different eras and transparently acknowledging the limitations of the historical datasets (e.g., lack of standardized effort in the 1960s-80s data). The results are presented clearly, with high-quality figures (e.g., Fig. 3 heatmap, Fig. 5 climatic preferences, Fig. 6 SST anomalies) that powerfully illustrate the temporal shifts. The discussion thoughtfully interprets the findings, linking them to the well-documented warming trend and broader biogeographic patterns, while also considering other contributing factors like changes in food availability.

Experimental design:

The experimental design is robust and appropriate for a study of this nature, which inherently relies on comparing modern data with historical records.

The use of multiple time points (three periods spanning ~50 years) is a major strength, allowing the authors to document a trajectory of change rather than just a snapshot comparison.

The careful effort to standardize the data is commendable. This includes updating all species names to current WoRMS/MolluscaBase taxonomy, geographically constraining the historical datasets to sites comparable to the modern surveys, and applying a consistent climatic preference classification to all species.

The modern survey (2023-2024) was conducted with a clear methodology (SCUBA, free-search, 27 dives, 25.45 hours), providing a robust contemporary baseline.

The choice of analytical methods is appropriate. Using both Shannon-Wiener and Simpson diversity indices captures complementary aspects of community structure. Fisher's exact tests with FDR correction are suitable for identifying species with significant changes in relative abundance. The Jaccard coefficient provides a simple, clear measure of overall community similarity. The inclusion of sea surface temperature anomaly data (Fig. 6) grounds the biological observations in a well-documented physical driver.

Validity of the findings:

The findings are robust, well-supported by the data, and their interpretation is appropriately nuanced given the limitations of the historical data.

The core conclusion—that the sea slug community has undergone a major reorganization towards higher diversity and a greater proportion of warm-water species—is strongly supported by the data. The increase in diversity indices (Fig. 2), the clear shift in relative abundances (Fig. 3, Fig. 4), and the striking change in the proportion of climatic preference groups (Fig. 5) are all consistent and compelling.

The link to rising sea surface temperatures (Fig. 6) is highly plausible and forms the central narrative of the discussion. The authors correctly identify this as the most likely primary driver, aligning with a large body of literature on climate-driven range shifts.

The authors are commendably transparent about the limitations of their study. They explicitly acknowledge the lack of standardized effort in the oldest dataset, the potential for observer bias in SCUBA surveys, and the relatively limited number of sampling sites. This honesty strengthens the reader's confidence in the conclusions, as the authors demonstrate a clear understanding of their data's constraints.

The finding that 24 species were newly recorded, with 71% being tropical-subtropical, is a powerful and valid result that directly supports the warming hypothesis.

The discussion of alternative and interacting factors (e.g., changes in algal communities, food availability) shows a sophisticated understanding of ecosystem dynamics and prevents the argument from being overly simplistic.

Additional comments:

This is an excellent and important study. I have only minor suggestions that could further improve the manuscript.

Clarify the Use of the 1960s-80s Dataset: The authors do a great job of acknowledging the limitations of the Matsubayashi (1989) data. However, it is used for the climatic preference comparison (Fig. 5) and the Jaccard analysis (Table 2), but not for the diversity indices. It would be helpful to add a very brief, explicit statement in the Results or Discussion reiterating why it was excluded from the diversity index calculations (i.e., lack of individual counts) but included in the other analyses (presence/absence data). This reinforces the methodological rigor.

Figure 3 (Heatmap) Interpretation: The heatmap in Figure 3 is very effective. To make it even more informative for the reader, consider adding a very brief note in the caption about how to interpret the gradient (e.g., "Darker shades of blue represent higher relative abundance"). While this is intuitive, a quick clarification is always helpful.

Potential for Range Shifts vs. Temporary Vagrancy: In the discussion of newly recorded tropical species (lines 340-350), the authors suggest these are "populations transported from offshore areas during summer." This is an important point. A brief mention of the distinction between temporary vagrancy (individuals transported by currents that may not survive the winter) and true range expansion (establishment of self-sustaining populations) would add a layer of ecological nuance. This could be linked to a suggestion for future research, such as year-round monitoring to determine if these species are overwintering and breeding.

Article Title: The genetic structure of a Brachypodium hybridum population in a patchy arid landscape is independent of neighboring perennials and stable over two consecutive years
Abstract: In arid landscapes perennials can provide protection for annual plants against environmental stress such as drought. This could lead to population sub-structuring of the annuals, because of position dependent reproductive success, and/or competition for association with perennial shrubs. Although such interactions are well known drivers of plant community dynamics, little is known about the local and temporal effects of perennial presence on the genetic structure of annuals. As a first step to address this, we used a set of microsatellite markers to assess the genetic structure of the annual grass Brachypodium hybridum (L.) P. Beauv. growing underneath and outside the canopy cover of perennials at an arid site in southern Spain over two consecutive years (2018 and 2019). Upon sampling, we observed clear but inconsistent seasonal differences in phenology between individuals underneath or outside canopies. However, we did not find genetic differentiation between sampling location or sampling year (four populations: overall FST = 0.024). An analysis of the overall genetic structure inferred three putative clusters, but these clusters were neither associated with location nor with year of sampling. We conclude that the genetic structure of this B. hybridum population is independent of neighboring perennials and stable over two consecutive years and is not associated with phenological differences. The data further show a high level of homozygosity in the population, and the recurrent presence of identical genotypes, both with respect to location and year, indicating a major role of selfing in B. hybridum reproduction. However, the samples collected outside the canopies in 2019 show a slightly higher value of estimated heterozygosity. This may indicate the effect of immigration from other B. hybridum populations. These results show that neighboring perennial plants do not affect the stable genetic structure of the B. hybridum population within this arid site, but that other factors such as immigration, could eventually result in local and temporal differences. These results also indicate a possible patchy distribution of identical genotypes of the annuals, which should be considered when sampling such populations.
Review:
Basic reporting:

The manuscript is clearly written and logically structured. The abstract provides an accurate and concise summary of the study. The introduction effectively sets the stage, reviewing the concepts of facilitation, its potential to create divergent selective pressures, and the lack of studies linking it to population genetic structure. This leads logically to the well-defined objective. The methods are described in sufficient detail to ensure reproducibility, covering the study site, sampling strategy, DNA extraction, species discrimination, and the extensive microsatellite genotyping and analysis. The results are presented clearly, with informative figures (e.g., Fig. 3 on allele frequencies, Fig. 4 on Structure analysis) and tables that support the narrative. The discussion thoughtfully interprets the null results, linking the high homozygosity and repeated genotypes to the selfing reproductive strategy of B. hybridum, and offering plausible explanations for the subtle temporal signal (potential immigration) and the lack of genetic structure (phenotypic plasticity, selection on unlinked loci).

Experimental design:

The experimental design is robust and well-suited to the research question.

The study system is appropriate. The arid site with a patchy distribution of perennial nurse plants creates a natural experiment to test for microenvironmentally-driven genetic differentiation. The use of a single, well-defined site controls for broad-scale environmental variation.

The stratified random sampling design across two years is a strength, allowing for an assessment of both spatial (under vs. outside canopy) and temporal stability.

The authors transparently address the challenge of sampling a species with variable phenology, clearly explaining why sample sizes for "in" and "out" categories differ between years (Table S1). Importantly, they statistically test for and rule out a bias in DNA extraction success (Fisher's exact test), confirming that the asymmetrical sample sizes reflect real biological patterns and not technical artifacts.

The use of microsatellite markers is appropriate for assessing fine-scale population genetic structure. The careful validation of 17 primer sets, the identification of 23 amplified loci across both subgenomes, and the transparent reporting of why five loci were excluded (monomorphic, unreliable amplification) demonstrate rigorous marker development.

The statistical analyses are comprehensive and appropriate. The use of F-statistics, AMOVA, and STRUCTURE provides a multi-faceted view of the data, from summary statistics to clustering algorithms.

Validity of the findings:

The findings are robust, well-supported by the data, and interpreted with appropriate caution.

The core finding—no genetic differentiation between "in" and "out" subpopulations—is strongly supported by multiple lines of evidence: a very low overall

Article Title: Ontogenetic shifts in sound production and shared sonic mechanisms in two priacanthid fishes
Abstract: Sound production in teleost fishes relies on diverse anatomical adaptations, yet convergent mechanisms involving extrinsic sonic muscles acting on the swim bladder are widespread. This study investigates the acoustic and morphological features of two priacanthid species, Indo-Pacific glasseye Heteropriacanthus carolinus and moontail bullseye Priacanthus hamrur to explore interspecific similarities in sound production. Using recordings and anatomical analyses, we show that both species rely on a forced-response mechanism, where the contraction rate of fast sonic muscles determines the fundamental frequency. This is corroborated by the smaller diameter of sonic fibres compared to epaxial fibres in both species. Despite belonging to different genera, both species exhibit extrinsic sonic muscles originating from the first pleural rib and inserting on the anterior swim bladder. However, P. hamrur displays anterior bladder projections potentially involved in enhanced hearing, absent in H. carolinus. Acoustic signals were broadly similar between species, suggesting that the morphological shift in muscle insertion does not affect sound structure. Comparative analysis across geographically distant populations of H. carolinus (Indian and Pacific Oceans) revealed variation in acoustic features that was size-dependent rather than region-specific. Juveniles emitted continuous pulse trains with high fundamental frequencies, whereas adults produced more segmented calls with lower frequencies. These ontogenetic differences reflect developmental modulation of vocal output, not anatomical changes.
